## vvx
Job Jockey Kit - MCP tools for project initiative management

attachment.content:
- cma-prep-pr: Prepare branch for upstream PR contribution
- rbk-prep-release: Prepare release candidate for upstream delivery
- rbk-wrangle: Muster, then roster RBK and JJK heats as two space-padded plain-text blocks
- vvc-BREAK-LOCK: Force-break a stuck VVC lock after a crash.
- vvc-commit: Guarded git commit
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.content:
1	# jjezs_paddock ₣BZ
2	
3	## Boundary — shape, not mechanism (cinched)
4	
5	This heat adopts the federation *shape* — the identity model and the converged
6	cult verbs — without building the federation *mechanism*. The backing stays
7	keyfile (service-account keys); a no-org operator runs it unchanged. The payoff:
8	a later switch to federation changes only what is behind the token — every
9	command and concept the operator has learned stays identical, so the switch is
10	non-disruptive. Explicitly OUT of scope: Workforce pool/provider setup, STS
11	token exchange, IdP device flow, and the RBRD mode-enum activation. Those belong
12	to the federation tier's own future work (RBSHR "Operator federation";
13	memo-20260527).
14	
15	## The target model
16	
17	- **Identity** = one principal per person, named for the person, not the role.
18	  In keyfile backing a principal is a service account holding one RBRA key; the
19	  role prefix (`director-…`) leaves the SA name.
20	- **Capability** = the IAM grants on that identity. A role (director, retriever,
21	  governor) becomes a named capability-set — the IAM-role list currently
22	  hardcoded inside the per-role invest verbs — that a grant verb applies. The
23	  same set applies identically to an SA member (keyfile) or a `principal://`
24	  member (federation); that sameness is the convergence.
25	- **Verbs converge** to two axes: add/remove a person (identity lifecycle) and
26	  grant/revoke a capability for a person (membership) — plus rekey (keyfile-only
27	  key rotation) and roster (read). These map onto the credential-lifecycle split
28	  already underway: add = establish, grant/revoke = the binding lifecycle (revoke
29	  closing the never-wired teardown half, now generic across roles), rekey =
30	  key-only rotation, remove = teardown.
31	- **One mode-seam**: only "add person" (mint SA+key vs register a federated
32	  principal) and the token accessor (signed-JWT vs STS) ever branch on backing.
33	  Everything above is backing-blind.
34	
35	## Why this is tractable (the load-bearing finding)
36	
37	The token layer is already identity-based: a minted token asserts only "I am this
38	SA," and Google grants whatever that SA's IAM bindings allow — enforcement is
39	server-side and un-hackable. Role-typing is cosmetic, living only in the SA name
40	prefix and the per-role selection constants. So this is mostly removing a naming
41	convention atop an already-identity-based mechanism, not rebuilding enforcement.
42	
43	## Cinched decisions
44	
45	- Enforcement is server-side IAM. No file or client-side check is ever the
46	  boundary; any honor-system gate a malicious operator bypasses by not running
47	  our client.
48	- Roster derives from IAM (an `rbk-identity` label marks RBK-managed SAs once the
49	  role leaves the name), not a stored list — it cannot drift.
50	- Capability-sets become named code (lift the inline IAM-role lists out of the
51	  invest verbs); they are not a config file.
52	- The mode enum (keyfile vs federation) belongs in RBRD (depot-uniform,
53	  tripwire-protected) and is added only when federation lands — not in this heat.
54	- Payor is a capability on the grant/revoke axis like the others, but its
55	  identity lifecycle (install/refresh) branches because its backing is a human
56	  OAuth refresh token, not an SA key. RBRP already encodes this ("not every
57	  operator has a Payor identity").
58	- Migration grandfathers existing role-named SAs: backfill the label, read grants
59	  from IAM, let legacy `director-…`/`retriever-…` names age out. No flag day.
60	
61	## Open forks (resolve within the heat)
62	
63	- **Governor capability breadth.** Governor holds `roles/owner` today, so it can
64	  already mint peer admins. Keep it broad and catch dangerous unions by roster
65	  audit, or scope it so the rung is a hard IAM wall — the latter needs IAM
66	  Deny/Conditions because project `setIamPolicy` is not role-scoped. Same question
67	  federation will later ask of the governor principal.
68	- **Intended-roster.** Whether to store a declared roster (names → intended
69	  capabilities) alongside the IAM-derived actual, for legibility and an
70	  intended-vs-actual audit.
71	
72	## Inherited concern — governor teardown leak
73	
74	Governor teardown is part of this heat's generic-teardown convergence (mantle →
75	remove+add). It leaks today: `rbgp_governor_mantle` deletes each outgoing
76	`governor-*` SA without first revoking its `roles/owner` (+ establish grants), so
77	every re-mantle accrues a `deleted:…?uid=` project tombstone — H1's mechanism,
78	governor tier, the cousin rbk-08 deliberately scoped out (8 ghosts and counting on
79	the canonical depot). The convergence closes it for free — the generic
80	revoke-before-delete covers the governor like any role, and an idempotent governor
81	(rekey, not delete+recreate) drops the delete entirely — and must reuse the rbk-08
82	revoke layer + Class-C propagation tolerance, not reinvent them. If this heat is
83	deferred or descopes the governor, the standalone fix (lean project-scope
84	revoke-before-delete in the mantle delete loop — the Payor already owns the
85	project, so no Class-C) is captured in
86	`Memos/memo-20260605-governor-mantle-tombstone-leak.md`.
87	
88	## What done looks like (the tectonic surface)
89	
90	Every cult-verb surface migrated to the converged identity/verb vocabulary, not
91	just the bash that mints tokens:
92	
93	- workstation bash (the ~30 role-keyed selection sites collapse to one identity);
94	- the governor/payor admin verbs (invest/divest/roster/mantle become
95	  add/grant/revoke/remove parameterized by capability);
96	- cloud-side bash wherever it names roles;
97	- the .adoc specs that voice the cult verbs;
98	- the Rust test cases (theurge) that drive them;
99	- the onboarding handbook tracks that teach them.
100	
101	## Sources
102	
103	The per-person design exploration in rbk-08-credential-repairs that birthed this
104	heat; memo-20260604-credential-churn-leak-and-propagation-races (H1/H2 lifecycle
105	split — the enabler); memo-20260527-operator-credential-models (the two-tier plan
106	and cult-verb shape); RBSHR "Operator federation".
107

file.content:
# jjezs_paddock ₣BZ

## Boundary — shape, not mechanism (cinched)

This heat adopts the federation *shape* — the identity model and the converged
cult verbs — without building the federation *mechanism*. The backing stays
keyfile (service-account keys); a no-org operator runs it unchanged. The payoff:
a later switch to federation changes only what is behind the token — every
command and concept the operator has learned stays identical, so the switch is
non-disruptive. Explicitly OUT of scope: Workforce pool/provider setup, STS
token exchange, IdP device flow, and the RBRD mode-enum activation. Those belong
to the federation tier's own future work (RBSHR "Operator federation";
memo-20260527).

## The target model

- **Identity** = one principal per person, named for the person, not the role.
  In keyfile backing a principal is a service account holding one RBRA key; the
  role prefix (`director-…`) leaves the SA name.
- **Capability** = the IAM grants on that identity. A role (director, retriever,
  governor) becomes a named capability-set — the IAM-role list currently
  hardcoded inside the per-role invest verbs — that a grant verb applies. The
  same set applies identically to an SA member (keyfile) or a `principal://`
  member (federation); that sameness is the convergence.
- **Verbs converge** to two axes: add/remove a person (identity lifecycle) and
  grant/revoke a capability for a person (membership) — plus rekey (keyfile-only
  key rotation) and roster (read). These map onto the credential-lifecycle split
  already underway: add = establish, grant/revoke = the binding lifecycle (revoke
  closing the never-wired teardown half, now generic across roles), rekey =
  key-only rotation, remove = teardown.
- **One mode-seam**: only "add person" (mint SA+key vs register a federated
  principal) and the token accessor (signed-JWT vs STS) ever branch on backing.
  Everything above is backing-blind.

## Why this is tractable (the load-bearing finding)

The token layer is already identity-based: a minted token asserts only "I am this
SA," and Google grants whatever that SA's IAM bindings allow — enforcement is
server-side and un-hackable. Role-typing is cosmetic, living only in the SA name
prefix and the per-role selection constants. So this is mostly removing a naming
convention atop an already-identity-based mechanism, not rebuilding enforcement.

## Cinched decisions

- Enforcement is server-side IAM. No file or client-side check is ever the
  boundary; any honor-system gate a malicious operator bypasses by not running
  our client.
- Roster derives from IAM (an `rbk-identity` label marks RBK-managed SAs once the
  role leaves the name), not a stored list — it cannot drift.
- Capability-sets become named code (lift the inline IAM-role lists out of the
  invest verbs); they are not a config file.
- The mode enum (keyfile vs federation) belongs in RBRD (depot-uniform,
  tripwire-protected) and is added only when federation lands — not in this heat.
- Payor is a capability on the grant/revoke axis like the others, but its
  identity lifecycle (install/refresh) branches because its backing is a human
  OAuth refresh token, not an SA key. RBRP already encodes this ("not every
  operator has a Payor identity").
- Migration grandfathers existing role-named SAs: backfill the label, read grants
  from IAM, let legacy `director-…`/`retriever-…` names age out. No flag day.

## Open forks (resolve within the heat)

- **Governor capability breadth.** Governor holds `roles/owner` today, so it can
  already mint peer admins. Keep it broad and catch dangerous unions by roster
  audit, or scope it so the rung is a hard IAM wall — the latter needs IAM
  Deny/Conditions because project `setIamPolicy` is not role-scoped. Same question
  federation will later ask of the governor principal.
- **Intended-roster.** Whether to store a declared roster (names → intended
  capabilities) alongside the IAM-derived actual, for legibility and an
  intended-vs-actual audit.

## Inherited concern — governor teardown leak

Governor teardown is part of this heat's generic-teardown convergence (mantle →
remove+add). It leaks today: `rbgp_governor_mantle` deletes each outgoing
`governor-*` SA without first revoking its `roles/owner` (+ establish grants), so
every re-mantle accrues a `deleted:…?uid=` project tombstone — H1's mechanism,
governor tier, the cousin rbk-08 deliberately scoped out (8 ghosts and counting on
the canonical depot). The convergence closes it for free — the generic
revoke-before-delete covers the governor like any role, and an idempotent governor
(rekey, not delete+recreate) drops the delete entirely — and must reuse the rbk-08
revoke layer + Class-C propagation tolerance, not reinvent them. If this heat is
deferred or descopes the governor, the standalone fix (lean project-scope
revoke-before-delete in the mantle delete loop — the Payor already owns the
project, so no Class-C) is captured in
`Memos/memo-20260605-governor-mantle-tombstone-leak.md`.

## What done looks like (the tectonic surface)

Every cult-verb surface migrated to the converged identity/verb vocabulary, not
just the bash that mints tokens:

- workstation bash (the ~30 role-keyed selection sites collapse to one identity);
- the governor/payor admin verbs (invest/divest/roster/mantle become
  add/grant/revoke/remove parameterized by capability);
- cloud-side bash wherever it names roles;
- the .adoc specs that voice the cult verbs;
- the Rust test cases (theurge) that drive them;
- the onboarding handbook tracks that teach them.

## Sources

The per-person design exploration in rbk-08-credential-repairs that birthed this
heat; memo-20260604-credential-churn-leak-and-propagation-races (H1/H2 lifecycle
split — the enabler); memo-20260527-operator-credential-models (the two-tier plan
and cult-verb shape); RBSHR "Operator federation".

aiTitle: Review and refine BZ idea for clean integration

content.text: This is a meaty architectural paddock. Now let me orient in the specs it leans on. I'll read the core ones directly and fan out a search agent for the "~30 role-keyed sites" claim in parallel, since that's a load-bearing assertion I want to verify rather than take on faith.

content.content:
1	[[rbshr_horizon_roadmap]]
2	==== Horizon Roadmap
3	
4	Defined-but-unscoped future work across Recipe Bottle.
5	Each item has a rationale (why it matters), a trigger (when to revisit), and a reference (where the evidence lives).
6	
7	Items here are not planned for any heat.
8	When an item becomes actionable, it moves into a heat as paces with proper dockets.
9	This is the single collection point for big changes — individual spec subdocuments should cross-reference here rather than maintaining local "Future Work" sections.
10	
11	**SA numeric ID for member references.**
12	Google recommends using a
13	{giam_service_account}'s
14	numeric ID rather than its email address when referencing it immediately after creation, to reduce propagation delay.
15	Recipe Bottle uses email addresses throughout, which is why propagation retry
16	(<<rbscip_trade_study>>
17	backoff profile) is necessary.
18	Switching to numeric ID could reduce retry frequency but would require threading the numeric ID from SA creation responses through all call chains that construct `serviceAccount:` member strings.
19	Requires verification that `setIamPolicy` accepts numeric ID in member strings.
20	_Trigger_: if propagation retries become a significant wall-clock cost in depot creation or governor reset.
21	_Reference_:
22	<<rbscig_iam_grant_contracts>>
23	(SA propagation section, evidence table).
24	
25	**Read-back verification universalization.**
26	Currently only
27	{rbtoe_iam_grant_project}
28	performs post-write verification (re-reading policy to confirm the granted role is observable).
29	{rbtoe_iam_grant_repo},
30	{rbtoe_iam_grant_sa},
31	and
32	{rbtoe_iam_grant_bucket}
33	trust the 200 from `setIamPolicy`.
34	Adding verification universally would provide stronger guarantees but adds an HTTP round-trip and a new timeout window per grant.
35	_Trigger_: after etag convergence (\u20a2AsAAF) has been validated in production and we have confidence in the corrected grant contracts.
36	_Reference_:
37	<<rbscig_iam_grant_contracts>>
38	(Convergence Analysis section).
39	
40	**SA preflight universalization.**
41	Currently only
42	{rbtoe_iam_grant_sa}
43	performs a pre-flight existence check on the target
44	{giam_service_account}.
45	Applying it to all grant types when the member is a service account requires conditional logic and changes behavior for non-SA members.
46	_Trigger_: if grant failures due to non-existent members become a diagnostic problem.
47	_Reference_:
48	<<rbscig_iam_grant_contracts>>
49	(Convergence Analysis section).
50	
51	**Podman as first-class deployment target.**
52	Recipe Bottle currently deploys to Podman on workstations via the bottle lifecycle (RBSBS, RBSBR, RBSBL).
53	Podman support is functional but not hardened — the sentry container, network isolation, and eBPF enforcement assume Linux Podman with rootful access.
54	Expanding to rootless Podman, Podman on macOS (via podman-machine), or alternative container runtimes would require revisiting the security model and network architecture.
55	_Trigger_: user demand for rootless deployment or macOS-native workflows beyond the current Podman VM approach.
56	_Reference_: RBSPV-PodmanVmSupplyChain.adoc (current VM supply chain);
57	RBSBS, RBSBR, RBSBL (bottle lifecycle specs).
58	
59	**`podman-compose` lacks `--wait` for `compose up`.**
60	Docker Compose `--wait` (v2.1.1+) blocks until all container health checks pass, which `rbob_charge` relies on to guarantee crucible readiness before returning.
61	`podman-compose` does not implement `--wait` — the command returns immediately after scheduling containers, creating a race between charge completion and the active-check assertion in theurge.
62	Without `--wait`, Podman integration will need an alternative readiness gate (polling loop, explicit health-check query, or `podman wait --condition=healthy`).
63	_Trigger_: Podman first-class deployment (above).
64	_Reference_: containers/podman-compose#710 (June 2023, open);
65	containers/podman-compose#1329 (October 2025, open).
66	
67	**Revised enshrine — universal capture verb under Lode recipe noun.**
68	Today's narrow `enshrine` mirrors only upstream OCI base images.
69	Three additional capture needs share the same gesture (pull from upstream, SLSA-wrap, store in GAR): build-time tool images (today's `mirror`/`reliquary` machinery), the WSL substrate distro that hosts the Windows workload, and podman VM disk images.
70	The revision generalizes `enshrine` into a universal verb operating on a single recipe noun — **Lode**, parallel to {rbtga_vessel} on the built side — with a kind enum that gates pipeline behavior: `base`, `tool`, `reliquary`, `wsl`, `podvm-wsl`, `podvm-native`.
71	Today's `reliquary` demotes from top-level noun to a kind enum slot — the date-cohort semantic survives, the cult-vocabulary weight drops.
72	Today's `mirror` verb retires entirely;
73	absorbed into enshrine on the `tool` and `reliquary` kinds.
74	Lode .env carries author intent only — no resolved FQIN field — mirroring RBRV's ORIGIN/ANCHOR pattern (kindle code computes upstream coordinates from kind primitives at module-startup).
75	The `wsl` kind delivers a project-controlled WSL rootfs tar that replaces the Microsoft-Store-appx-mutable distribution today's caparison-w depends on;
76	the `podvm-*` kinds collapse `rbv_PodmanVM.sh`'s existing GHCR mirror into GAR under the unified verb.
77	_Trigger_: when Windows host workload requires a project-controlled WSL distro, when podman VM supply chain needs project-owned bytes in GAR, or when the `mirror`/`reliquary` machinery accumulates enough friction to motivate consolidation.
78	_Reference_: RBSAE-ark_enshrine.adoc (current narrow-scope spec);
79	RBSRV-RegimeVessel.adoc (ORIGIN/ANCHOR precedent, kind-enum-gated variable groups pattern);
80	`Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/FUTURE/rbv_PodmanVM.sh` (existing podman-vm fan-out machinery).
81	
82	**Hairpin peer access between crucibles.**
83	Bottled containers cannot currently reach each other's published entry ports.
84	Each bottle is isolated behind its own sentry on a separate enclave network.
85	The traffic path is feasible: Bottle A egresses through Sentry A's transit bridge, the host kernel routes to Sentry B's transit bridge, and Sentry B's existing entry path (DNAT + RBM-FORWARD) accepts the connection unchanged.
86	The security model is preserved — the sentry remains the sole enforcement point, with peer access as a new category of allowed destination alongside entry (inbound) and uplink (outbound internet).
87	Nameplate model: two new variables (`RBRN_PEER_ACCESS_MODE` disabled|allowlist, gated `RBRN_PEER_ALLOWED_MONIKERS` as comma-separated moniker list).
88	No global mode — peer access must always be explicit.
89	Implementation touches three areas: nameplate kindle/validate/render, a peer-access iptables step in security_config, and host-side peer IP discovery via container inspection.
90	Audit gains a cross-instance check: each named peer must exist and have entry enabled.
91	_Trigger_: when a use case requires bottle-to-bottle communication (e.g., microservice composition, service mesh patterns).
92	_Reference_: prior design conversation capture (paddock).
93	
94	**Hoard: external artifact mirror in retention-locked GCS.**
95	A
96	{rbtgi_depot}
97	citizen alongside the
98	{rbtga_ark}
99	that mirrors external content-addressed corpora — canonical case: Hugging Face model checkpoints.
100	GCS archive is ~80× cheaper per byte than GAR and supports bucket-level retention locks for compliance-grade immutability that GAR does not offer.
101	Ingress reuses upstream SHA256 addressing (shallow git + LFS pointer resolution, SHA256-keyed GCS objects with natural dedup across revisions);
102	Cloud Build streams so egress stays Google-side;
103	auth path reuses the existing JWT/OAuth bearer curl stack — no new tooling dependency.
104	+
105	Intended consumption: just-in-time workstation mount from the retention-locked bucket into the
106	{at_bottle_container}
107	at charge time, rather than baking models into
108	{at_bottle_image_s}.
109	Keeps images lean, enables model swap without rebuild, keeps the retention property where it naturally lives.
110	Tradeoff: provenance becomes a
111	{at_nameplate}-declared
112	hoard digest recorded at charge time rather than a SLSA-attested image layer — weaker by one link.
113	_Trigger_: first concrete need to run LM inference inside a
114	{at_bottle_container}
115	against a specific HF checkpoint with retention guarantees.
116	_Reference_: this roadmap entry;
117	design conversation pending heat.
118	
119	**CIDR allowlist granularity vs CDN-hosted domains.**
120	The sentry's iptables CIDR rules accept all protocols and all destination ports to allowed IP ranges.
121	This is intentional: the spec defines
122	{rbrn_uplink_access_mode_allowlist}
123	as "IP access restricted to allowed CIDR ranges" — not port-restricted.
124	The DNS allowlist
125	({rbrn_uplink_allowed_domains})
126	provides name-level precision;
127	the CIDR allowlist provides IP-level gating.
128	These two layers are independent and reinforce each other.
129	+
130	The weakness emerges when allowed domains are CDN-hosted.
131	IANA's example.com (RFC 2606) migrated to Cloudflare, requiring CIDR expansion from `93.184.216.0/24` to include `104.16.0.0/12` — a /12 covering ~1M IPs shared with a large fraction of Cloudflare-fronted sites.
132	The DNS allowlist remains precise (two domains), but the CIDR backstop is now porous: any destination within the Cloudflare range is reachable regardless of domain.
133	A compromised or attacker-controlled site sharing the CDN range would be reachable from the bottle.
134	+
135	Three potential mitigations, in order of increasing ambition:
136	+
137	. **Accept as-is with documentation.**
138	  The DNS allowlist is the primary gate;
139	  the CIDR is a coarse backstop.
140	  Document the known weakness for broad CIDRs.
141	  Suitable when the allowed domains are trusted and the CDN range is acceptable collateral exposure.
142	. **Dynamic CIDR from DNS resolution.**
143	  Have dnsmasq feed resolved IPs into iptables at resolution time, so the allowed set tracks actual DNS responses rather than static configuration.
144	  Eliminates CDN range sprawl.
145	  Requires new sentry infrastructure (dnsmasq hook scripts or ipset integration).
146	. **TLS SNI enforcement.**
147	  Inspect TLS ClientHello for the SNI field and enforce that it matches the DNS allowlist.
148	  Closes the CDN gap at the application layer without dynamic iptables.
149	  Requires a transparent TLS proxy or eBPF-based SNI extraction in the sentry — a different order of complexity.
150	+
151	_Trigger_: when allowed domains require CIDRs broader than /24, or when the threat model requires preventing lateral reachability within a CDN range.
152	_Reference_: rbjs_sentry.sh (Phase 3 CIDR rules);
153	the tadmor nameplate's {rbrn_regime} assignment file (current CIDR configuration).
154	
155	**Encrypted tunnel conduit (WireGuard in sentry).**
156	Add WireGuard to the sentry image so the sentry terminates an encrypted tunnel directly to a VPC hosting PrivateLink endpoints for cloud services.
157	The bottle's traffic routes through a `wg0` interface on the sentry — one stable VPC CIDR replaces floating cloud-provider IP ranges.
158	WireGuard is ~400KB on Alpine, uses static keypair exchange (no PKI/CA), and fits naturally into the sentry's existing privileged-container, explicit-routing architecture.
159	New nameplate fields: tunnel mode, peer endpoint, public keys, allowed IPs.
160	VPC side requires a small WireGuard peer instance and PrivateLink endpoints for target services.
161	_Trigger_: when a bottle needs to reach a cloud service that supports PrivateLink (AWS Bedrock is the known first case; survey should include Vertex AI, Azure OpenAI, GitLab Dedicated, SageMaker, and others as they emerge).
162	_Reference_: `Memos/memo-20260415-crucible-conduit-architecture.md` (full design exploration, approach comparison, service compatibility survey).
163	
164	**Cloud service conduit nameplate.**
165	A nameplate configuration pattern for bottles that access cloud services at runtime.
166	Near-term: allowlist-only nameplate targeting specific service CIDRs and domains (works today with existing sentry machinery, no development needed).
167	Longer-term: tunnel-mode nameplate using WireGuard conduit (above).
168	AWS Bedrock is the known requirement;
169	the pattern should be researched broadly across cloud AI inference services, managed Git hosting, and other PrivateLink-capable endpoints.
170	_Trigger_: immediate — Bedrock access is a current need.
171	Allowlist-only nameplate can be created now;
172	tunnel-mode nameplate follows WireGuard sentry work.
173	_Reference_: `Memos/memo-20260415-crucible-conduit-architecture.md` (approach A for allowlist-only, approach C for tunnel mode).
174	
175	**Bottle credential custody.**
176	Move the service secrets a bottle workload uses — cloud API keys, IAM keys, SSH keys — off the operator's workstation and into the bottle itself, injected at charge time via nameplate/regime configuration rather than baked into the bottle image (a published image must never carry a live secret).
177	The workstation starts the crucible but never holds the service secret; its only credential becomes permission to charge.
178	This blocks credential theft from a compromised workstation — the one threat the sentry allowlist and the encrypted tunnel do not already close — by shrinking the secret's resting place to the contained bottle.
179	The existing RBRA file regime-path pattern provides the model.
180	Orthogonal to but naturally paired with the encrypted tunnel conduit: the tunnel grants the bottle network reach to a cloud service and the sentry custodies the tunnel key, while this item custodies the service secret inside the bottle — network access and the credential to use it configured together.
181	_Trigger_: alongside the conduit nameplate work.
182	_Reference_: `Memos/memo-20260415-crucible-conduit-architecture.md` (Credential Confinement section; the `Credential theft from workstation` row of the threat-model table).
183	
184	NOTE: For SaaS services without PrivateLink (GitHub.com, GitLab.com), the sentry CIDR/domain allowlist + TLS remains the realistic ceiling.
185	The sentry still provides the core value: the bottle cannot reach anything the sentry doesn't allow, and the workstation is cut out of the data path.
186	The tunnel adds defense-in-depth only for PrivateLink-capable services.
187	Cross-reference: the existing "CIDR allowlist granularity vs CDN-hosted domains" item (Network Security Model) remains relevant for SaaS-only services where the tunnel cannot help.
188	
189	**Cross-cloud architectural stance: asymmetric sovereignty.**
190	Recipe Bottle governs identities and infrastructure only in GCP.
191	AWS-side presence is shaped as delivered artifacts and policy templates, not owned principals — no AWS-Director, no AWS-Mason, no AWS-Retriever.
192	If Recipe Bottle ever needs to *build* in AWS rather than deliver to it, that would be a deliberate architectural decision triggering a full parallel role hierarchy, not an incremental drift.
193	Vocabulary used in related entries — embassy, envoy, bullion, entrust, accredit, recall, chantry — is tentative working language from a design conversation; concepts are firmer than the words.
194	_Trigger_: when AWS-side work moves into a heat with real scope.
195	_Reference_: this roadmap entry; design conversation pending heat.
196	
197	**Embassy — AWS-side delivery premises.**
198	An S3 bucket (with Object Lock in Compliance mode for retention), an IAM role with a trust policy accepting the envoy's federated identity, and Recipe-Bottle-shipped IAM policy shapes that the AWS consumer attaches to whatever principal consumes delivered artifacts (EC2 role, ECS task role, Bedrock execution role, etc.).
199	Provisioned by the payor via operations congruent with
200	{rbtgi_depot}
201	creation — the AWS consumer's embassy is the sovereign-setup equivalent of the Manor's depot.
202	Recipe Bottle ships provisioning templates;
203	the AWS consumer owns the resulting resources.
204	_Trigger_: first concrete need to deliver artifacts to an AWS workload.
205	_Reference_: this roadmap entry;
206	design conversation pending heat.
207	
208	**Envoy — GCP-side cloud-native cross-post actor.**
209	A
210	{giam_service_account}
211	in the depot, sibling to existing cloud-native SAs, whose sole purpose is executing cross-cloud pushes to an accredited embassy.
212	The envoy holds no long-lived AWS credentials;
213	it federates to AWS via OIDC Workload Identity Federation, exchanging short-lived GCP tokens for short-lived AWS STS credentials at operation time.
214	Preserves credential locality: Director holds only GCP credentials on the workstation, envoy holds only federation configuration in GCP, and AWS admin credentials never enter Recipe Bottle's custody.
215	_Trigger_: embassy entry above.
216	_Reference_: this roadmap entry.
217	
218	**Bullion — AWS-side content-addressed artifact.**
219	Recipe Bottle's delivered artifact in an embassy bucket: a content-addressed filesystem image (squashfs/erofs, optionally with dm-verity) with Object Lock applied for compliance-grade retention.
220	A bullion may carry a vessel image payload (the "Director pushes a vessel to AWS" motivator), model weights, or any other content-addressed corpus.
221	Sibling-but-distinct from the hoard: hoards stay GCS-native and depot-resident, preserving the existing provenance story;
222	bullions are the new AWS-side citizen with S3-native retention semantics.
223	Provenance transports as naming-convention sibling objects (attestation JSON adjacent to the image blob), not as OCI referring artifacts, because the bullion is not an OCI artifact.
224	_Trigger_: embassy entry above.
225	_Reference_: this roadmap entry;
226	Artifact Preservation section (hoard entry) for the GCP sibling concept.
227	
228	**Accredit / Recall — governor operations for embassy binding lifecycle.**
229	Accredit: governor configures the envoy's OIDC federation against an embassy's IAM role trust policy, and records the binding (embassy name, AWS account, region, role ARN, bucket name) in depot state.
230	Recall: governor removes the federation configuration and prunes the binding.
231	Follows the existing governor verb register (mantles, charters, knights, forfeits) with diplomatic-register verbs appropriate for foreign-court relationships.
232	A single depot may be accredited to multiple embassies.
233	_Trigger_: embassy entry above.
234	_Reference_: this roadmap entry.
235	
236	**Entrust — director-triggered cross-post operation.**
237	Director invokes entrust on a specific hallmark (or other deliverable) with a target embassy name.
238	The envoy executes the cross-post from within Cloud Build: federates to the embassy's IAM role, streams bytes to S3, applies Object Lock retention.
239	Director holds no AWS credentials;
240	failure modes surface through the existing Cloud Build event stream.
241	_Trigger_: embassy entry above.
242	_Reference_: this roadmap entry.
243	
244	**Chantry — Bedrock-hosted inference endpoint.**
245	Architecturally distinct from the embassy/bullion pattern.
246	A chantry is a Bedrock-registered custom model (or a native Bedrock foundation model) invoked by a
247	{at_bottle_container}
248	over the crucible conduit architecture — not an artifact Recipe Bottle delivers, but a foreign-sovereign service a bottle consumes.
249	Weights may originate as a bullion (entrusted to an embassy, then consumed by Bedrock Custom Model Import on the AWS side), but the chantry itself is the registered-and-invocable endpoint, not the weight storage.
250	Pairs with the "Cloud service conduit nameplate" entry for the invocation path.
251	_Trigger_: first concrete Bedrock inference requirement;
252	near-term per the Crucible Conduit Architecture section above.
253	_Reference_: this roadmap entry;
254	`Memos/memo-20260415-crucible-conduit-architecture.md`;
255	Crucible Conduit Architecture section above.
256	
257	**Egress lockdown — IMPLEMENTED.**
258	Dual-pool architecture: tether pool (public egress) and airgap pool (`NO_PUBLIC_EGRESS`).
259	Operation-level routing: inscribe/enshrine use tether, about/vouch always airgap, conjure/bind per vessel `RBRV_EGRESS_MODE`.
260	Reliquaries (tool images) and anchors (base images) provide universal GAR-sourced dependencies.
261	_Reference_: RBSCB-CloudBuildPosture.adoc (Implemented: Egress Lockdown via Dual Pools).
262	
263	**VPC Service Controls perimeter.**
264	Wrap depot project in VPC-SC perimeter restricting API access.
265	Requires egress lockdown (now implemented) as prerequisite, org-level admin for perimeter creation, and verification of every GCP API call in the pipeline.
266	_Trigger_: organizational security policy mandating VPC-SC, or data exfiltration risk assessment.
267	_Reference_: RBSCB-CloudBuildPosture.adoc (Potential Hardening: VPC Service Controls).
268	Cross-reference: if a VPC is stood up for the crucible conduit architecture (above), design the VPC-SC perimeter to serve both Cloud Build egress and bottle conduit consumers.
269	
270	**Cosign image signing.**
271	Cryptographic signatures on images, verifiable by downstream consumers.
272	KMS-backed keyed signing preferred for airgap-pool compatibility.
273	Currently redundant given CB-native SLSA v1.0 Build Level 3 attestation.
274	_Trigger_: external image distribution, trust verification needed outside GCP, or builder strategy expanding beyond Google Cloud Build.
275	_Reference_: RBSCB-CloudBuildPosture.adoc (Deferred Items: Cosign).
276	
277	**Binary Authorization.**
278	Deploy-time policy enforcement (only attested images can be deployed).
279	Technically achievable now with CB-native SLSA Level 3.
280	Not beneficial until deployment targets include GKE or Cloud Run.
281	_Trigger_: deployment targets change from Podman workstations to managed container platforms.
282	_Reference_: RBSCB-CloudBuildPosture.adoc (Deferred Items: Binary Authorization).
283	
284	**Dockerfile base image digest validation.**
285	Builder step images are digest-pinned, but user-authored Dockerfiles in vessel build contexts are not validated.
286	A vessel with `FROM ubuntu:22.04` uses a mutable tag.
287	Constrains vessel authors (policy decision).
288	_Trigger_: vessel ecosystem matures beyond internal-only use.
289	_Reference_: RBSCB-CloudBuildPosture.adoc (Deferred Items: Dockerfile base image).
290	
291	**Reproducibility verification.**
292	Periodic independent rebuilds compared by digest.
293	Requires reproducible toolchains — a vessel-author discipline, not a pipeline control.
294	_Trigger_: vessel ecosystem maturity and external distribution.
295	_Reference_: RBSCB-CloudBuildPosture.adoc (Deferred Items: Reproducibility).
296	
297	**Operator federation — external-IdP identity for depot participants.**
298	Recipe Bottle grants
299	{rbtgi_depot}
300	capabilities to identities the depot's IAM recognizes.
301	The shipping model is the keyfile tier: each operator role holds a service-account key file (the
302	{rbtgi_rbra_file})
303	on disk, possession of the key is the identity, and no GCP organization is required.
304	Operator federation is the heavyweight successor tier for installations that cannot accept an operator secret on disk.
305	The depot owner configures the
306	{rbtgi_depot}
307	to trust an external OIDC/SAML identity provider (any compliant issuer — self-hosted Dex/Keycloak, or GCP Identity Platform as an in-house IdP), then grants capabilities to those federated `principal://` subjects: keyless, no Google accounts for participants, revocation central at the depot.
308	The
309	{rbtgi_depot}
310	remains the home of the participant roster (the role-to-identity mapping); the IdP owns only identity proofing.
311	Org-only — federation pools are organization-level (Workforce Identity Federation, the human-identity product), so a no-org
312	{rbtr_payor}
313	cannot use this tier; the organization requirement is the line between the free keyfile tier and the federation tier.
314	A qualifying organization is free via Cloud Identity once its owner verifies control of a DNS domain, so the true prerequisite is that the operator controls a domain — not that they pay Google.
315	The Google-side credential path is curl-native — an STS token exchange (RFC 8693), no gcloud and no added stack dependency; only the IdP-auth leg (device flow where the issuer supports it) varies by issuer.
316	Distinct from the envoy's egress federation (GCP-to-AWS delivery, the machine-identity Workload product): this is
317	{at_operator}
318	*access* identity arriving the other way.
319	_Trigger_: first concrete need from an organization that cannot accept on-disk operator keyfiles, or when the keyfile tier's secret-distribution and rotation burden materially blocks adoption rather than being theoretically frictionful.
320	_Reference_: `Memos/memo-20260527-operator-credential-models.md` (the source document for this entry — the committed two-tier plan: keyfile (free, no-org) and Workforce federation (paid, org-required), the curl-native two-leg flow, device-flow / 12-hour session cap, the per-depot mode enum homed in RBRD, and the cult-verb shape);
321	`Memos/memo-20260522-org-affiliated-credential-reorientation.md` (R1/R2/R3 credential model and three-tier ladder, plus R4/R5 test-rig and gauntlet resolutions);
322	`Memos/memo-20260427-google-native-human-auth.md` (impersonation mechanism detail — its human-auth *framing* is superseded by R2);
323	Google docs: Workforce Identity Federation overview (`https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/workforce-identity-federation`) and short-lived token exchange (`https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/workforce-obtaining-short-lived-credentials`).
324

[[rbshr_horizon_roadmap]]
==== Horizon Roadmap

Defined-but-unscoped future work across Recipe Bottle.
Each item has a rationale (why it matters), a trigger (when to revisit), and a reference (where the evidence lives).

Items here are not planned for any heat.
When an item becomes actionable, it moves into a heat as paces with proper dockets.
This is the single collection point for big changes — individual spec subdocuments should cross-reference here rather than maintaining local "Future Work" sections.

**SA numeric ID for member references.**
Google recommends using a
{giam_service_account}'s
numeric ID rather than its email address when referencing it immediately after creation, to reduce propagation delay.
Recipe Bottle uses email addresses throughout, which is why propagation retry
(<<rbscip_trade_study>>
backoff profile) is necessary.
Switching to numeric ID could reduce retry frequency but would require threading the numeric ID from SA creation responses through all call chains that construct `serviceAccount:` member strings.
Requires verification that `setIamPolicy` accepts numeric ID in member strings.
_Trigger_: if propagation retries become a significant wall-clock cost in depot creation or governor reset.
_Reference_:
<<rbscig_iam_grant_contracts>>
(SA propagation section, evidence table).

**Read-back verification universalization.**
Currently only
{rbtoe_iam_grant_project}
performs post-write verification (re-reading policy to confirm the granted role is observable).
{rbtoe_iam_grant_repo},
{rbtoe_iam_grant_sa},
and
{rbtoe_iam_grant_bucket}
trust the 200 from `setIamPolicy`.
Adding verification universally would provide stronger guarantees but adds an HTTP round-trip and a new timeout window per grant.
_Trigger_: after etag convergence (\u20a2AsAAF) has been validated in production and we have confidence in the corrected grant contracts.
_Reference_:
<<rbscig_iam_grant_contracts>>
(Convergence Analysis section).

**SA preflight universalization.**
Currently only
{rbtoe_iam_grant_sa}
performs a pre-flight existence check on the target
{giam_service_account}.
Applying it to all grant types when the member is a service account requires conditional logic and changes behavior for non-SA members.
_Trigger_: if grant failures due to non-existent members become a diagnostic problem.
_Reference_:
<<rbscig_iam_grant_contracts>>
(Convergence Analysis section).

**Podman as first-class deployment target.**
Recipe Bottle currently deploys to Podman on workstations via the bottle lifecycle (RBSBS, RBSBR, RBSBL).
Podman support is functional but not hardened — the sentry container, network isolation, and eBPF enforcement assume Linux Podman with rootful access.
Expanding to rootless Podman, Podman on macOS (via podman-machine), or alternative container runtimes would require revisiting the security model and network architecture.
_Trigger_: user demand for rootless deployment or macOS-native workflows beyond the current Podman VM approach.
_Reference_: RBSPV-PodmanVmSupplyChain.adoc (current VM supply chain);
RBSBS, RBSBR, RBSBL (bottle lifecycle specs).

**`podman-compose` lacks `--wait` for `compose up`.**
Docker Compose `--wait` (v2.1.1+) blocks until all container health checks pass, which `rbob_charge` relies on to guarantee crucible readiness before returning.
`podman-compose` does not implement `--wait` — the command returns immediately after scheduling containers, creating a race between charge completion and the active-check assertion in theurge.
Without `--wait`, Podman integration will need an alternative readiness gate (polling loop, explicit health-check query, or `podman wait --condition=healthy`).
_Trigger_: Podman first-class deployment (above).
_Reference_: containers/podman-compose#710 (June 2023, open);
containers/podman-compose#1329 (October 2025, open).

**Revised enshrine — universal capture verb under Lode recipe noun.**
Today's narrow `enshrine` mirrors only upstream OCI base images.
Three additional capture needs share the same gesture (pull from upstream, SLSA-wrap, store in GAR): build-time tool images (today's `mirror`/`reliquary` machinery), the WSL substrate distro that hosts the Windows workload, and podman VM disk images.
The revision generalizes `enshrine` into a universal verb operating on a single recipe noun — **Lode**, parallel to {rbtga_vessel} on the built side — with a kind enum that gates pipeline behavior: `base`, `tool`, `reliquary`, `wsl`, `podvm-wsl`, `podvm-native`.
Today's `reliquary` demotes from top-level noun to a kind enum slot — the date-cohort semantic survives, the cult-vocabulary weight drops.
Today's `mirror` verb retires entirely;
absorbed into enshrine on the `tool` and `reliquary` kinds.
Lode .env carries author intent only — no resolved FQIN field — mirroring RBRV's ORIGIN/ANCHOR pattern (kindle code computes upstream coordinates from kind primitives at module-startup).
The `wsl` kind delivers a project-controlled WSL rootfs tar that replaces the Microsoft-Store-appx-mutable distribution today's caparison-w depends on;
the `podvm-*` kinds collapse `rbv_PodmanVM.sh`'s existing GHCR mirror into GAR under the unified verb.
_Trigger_: when Windows host workload requires a project-controlled WSL distro, when podman VM supply chain needs project-owned bytes in GAR, or when the `mirror`/`reliquary` machinery accumulates enough friction to motivate consolidation.
_Reference_: RBSAE-ark_enshrine.adoc (current narrow-scope spec);
RBSRV-RegimeVessel.adoc (ORIGIN/ANCHOR precedent, kind-enum-gated variable groups pattern);
`Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/FUTURE/rbv_PodmanVM.sh` (existing podman-vm fan-out machinery).

**Hairpin peer access between crucibles.**
Bottled containers cannot currently reach each other's published entry ports.
Each bottle is isolated behind its own sentry on a separate enclave network.
The traffic path is feasible: Bottle A egresses through Sentry A's transit bridge, the host kernel routes to Sentry B's transit bridge, and Sentry B's existing entry path (DNAT + RBM-FORWARD) accepts the connection unchanged.
The security model is preserved — the sentry remains the sole enforcement point, with peer access as a new category of allowed destination alongside entry (inbound) and uplink (outbound internet).
Nameplate model: two new variables (`RBRN_PEER_ACCESS_MODE` disabled|allowlist, gated `RBRN_PEER_ALLOWED_MONIKERS` as comma-separated moniker list).
No global mode — peer access must always be explicit.
Implementation touches three areas: nameplate kindle/validate/render, a peer-access iptables step in security_config, and host-side peer IP discovery via container inspection.
Audit gains a cross-instance check: each named peer must exist and have entry enabled.
_Trigger_: when a use case requires bottle-to-bottle communication (e.g., microservice composition, service mesh patterns).
_Reference_: prior design conversation capture (paddock).

**Hoard: external artifact mirror in retention-locked GCS.**
A
{rbtgi_depot}
citizen alongside the
{rbtga_ark}
that mirrors external content-addressed corpora — canonical case: Hugging Face model checkpoints.
GCS archive is ~80× cheaper per byte than GAR and supports bucket-level retention locks for compliance-grade immutability that GAR does not offer.
Ingress reuses upstream SHA256 addressing (shallow git + LFS pointer resolution, SHA256-keyed GCS objects with natural dedup across revisions);
Cloud Build streams so egress stays Google-side;
auth path reuses the existing JWT/OAuth bearer curl stack — no new tooling dependency.
+
Intended consumption: just-in-time workstation mount from the retention-locked bucket into the
{at_bottle_container}
at charge time, rather than baking models into
{at_bottle_image_s}.
Keeps images lean, enables model swap without rebuild, keeps the retention property where it naturally lives.
Tradeoff: provenance becomes a
{at_nameplate}-declared
hoard digest recorded at charge time rather than a SLSA-attested image layer — weaker by one link.
_Trigger_: first concrete need to run LM inference inside a
{at_bottle_container}
against a specific HF checkpoint with retention guarantees.
_Reference_: this roadmap entry;
design conversation pending heat.

**CIDR allowlist granularity vs CDN-hosted domains.**
The sentry's iptables CIDR rules accept all protocols and all destination ports to allowed IP ranges.
This is intentional: the spec defines
{rbrn_uplink_access_mode_allowlist}
as "IP access restricted to allowed CIDR ranges" — not port-restricted.
The DNS allowlist
({rbrn_uplink_allowed_domains})
provides name-level precision;
the CIDR allowlist provides IP-level gating.
These two layers are independent and reinforce each other.
+
The weakness emerges when allowed domains are CDN-hosted.
IANA's example.com (RFC 2606) migrated to Cloudflare, requiring CIDR expansion from `93.184.216.0/24` to include `104.16.0.0/12` — a /12 covering ~1M IPs shared with a large fraction of Cloudflare-fronted sites.
The DNS allowlist remains precise (two domains), but the CIDR backstop is now porous: any destination within the Cloudflare range is reachable regardless of domain.
A compromised or attacker-controlled site sharing the CDN range would be reachable from the bottle.
+
Three potential mitigations, in order of increasing ambition:
+
. **Accept as-is with documentation.**
  The DNS allowlist is the primary gate;
  the CIDR is a coarse backstop.
  Document the known weakness for broad CIDRs.
  Suitable when the allowed domains are trusted and the CDN range is acceptable collateral exposure.
. **Dynamic CIDR from DNS resolution.**
  Have dnsmasq feed resolved IPs into iptables at resolution time, so the allowed set tracks actual DNS responses rather than static configuration.
  Eliminates CDN range sprawl.
  Requires new sentry infrastructure (dnsmasq hook scripts or ipset integration).
. **TLS SNI enforcement.**
  Inspect TLS ClientHello for the SNI field and enforce that it matches the DNS allowlist.
  Closes the CDN gap at the application layer without dynamic iptables.
  Requires a transparent TLS proxy or eBPF-based SNI extraction in the sentry — a different order of complexity.
+
_Trigger_: when allowed domains require CIDRs broader than /24, or when the threat model requires preventing lateral reachability within a CDN range.
_Reference_: rbjs_sentry.sh (Phase 3 CIDR rules);
the tadmor nameplate's {rbrn_regime} assignment file (current CIDR configuration).

**Encrypted tunnel conduit (WireGuard in sentry).**
Add WireGuard to the sentry image so the sentry terminates an encrypted tunnel directly to a VPC hosting PrivateLink endpoints for cloud services.
The bottle's traffic routes through a `wg0` interface on the sentry — one stable VPC CIDR replaces floating cloud-provider IP ranges.
WireGuard is ~400KB on Alpine, uses static keypair exchange (no PKI/CA), and fits naturally into the sentry's existing privileged-container, explicit-routing architecture.
New nameplate fields: tunnel mode, peer endpoint, public keys, allowed IPs.
VPC side requires a small WireGuard peer instance and PrivateLink endpoints for target services.
_Trigger_: when a bottle needs to reach a cloud service that supports PrivateLink (AWS Bedrock is the known first case; survey should include Vertex AI, Azure OpenAI, GitLab Dedicated, SageMaker, and others as they emerge).
_Reference_: `Memos/memo-20260415-crucible-conduit-architecture.md` (full design exploration, approach comparison, service compatibility survey).

**Cloud service conduit nameplate.**
A nameplate configuration pattern for bottles that access cloud services at runtime.
Near-term: allowlist-only nameplate targeting specific service CIDRs and domains (works today with existing sentry machinery, no development needed).
Longer-term: tunnel-mode nameplate using WireGuard conduit (above).
AWS Bedrock is the known requirement;
the pattern should be researched broadly across cloud AI inference services, managed Git hosting, and other PrivateLink-capable endpoints.
_Trigger_: immediate — Bedrock access is a current need.
Allowlist-only nameplate can be created now;
tunnel-mode nameplate follows WireGuard sentry work.
_Reference_: `Memos/memo-20260415-crucible-conduit-architecture.md` (approach A for allowlist-only, approach C for tunnel mode).

**Bottle credential custody.**
Move the service secrets a bottle workload uses — cloud API keys, IAM keys, SSH keys — off the operator's workstation and into the bottle itself, injected at charge time via nameplate/regime configuration rather than baked into the bottle image (a published image must never carry a live secret).
The workstation starts the crucible but never holds the service secret; its only credential becomes permission to charge.
This blocks credential theft from a compromised workstation — the one threat the sentry allowlist and the encrypted tunnel do not already close — by shrinking the secret's resting place to the contained bottle.
The existing RBRA file regime-path pattern provides the model.
Orthogonal to but naturally paired with the encrypted tunnel conduit: the tunnel grants the bottle network reach to a cloud service and the sentry custodies the tunnel key, while this item custodies the service secret inside the bottle — network access and the credential to use it configured together.
_Trigger_: alongside the conduit nameplate work.
_Reference_: `Memos/memo-20260415-crucible-conduit-architecture.md` (Credential Confinement section; the `Credential theft from workstation` row of the threat-model table).

NOTE: For SaaS services without PrivateLink (GitHub.com, GitLab.com), the sentry CIDR/domain allowlist + TLS remains the realistic ceiling.
The sentry still provides the core value: the bottle cannot reach anything the sentry doesn't allow, and the workstation is cut out of the data path.
The tunnel adds defense-in-depth only for PrivateLink-capable services.
Cross-reference: the existing "CIDR allowlist granularity vs CDN-hosted domains" item (Network Security Model) remains relevant for SaaS-only services where the tunnel cannot help.

**Cross-cloud architectural stance: asymmetric sovereignty.**
Recipe Bottle governs identities and infrastructure only in GCP.
AWS-side presence is shaped as delivered artifacts and policy templates, not owned principals — no AWS-Director, no AWS-Mason, no AWS-Retriever.
If Recipe Bottle ever needs to *build* in AWS rather than deliver to it, that would be a deliberate architectural decision triggering a full parallel role hierarchy, not an incremental drift.
Vocabulary used in related entries — embassy, envoy, bullion, entrust, accredit, recall, chantry — is tentative working language from a design conversation; concepts are firmer than the words.
_Trigger_: when AWS-side work moves into a heat with real scope.
_Reference_: this roadmap entry; design conversation pending heat.

**Embassy — AWS-side delivery premises.**
An S3 bucket (with Object Lock in Compliance mode for retention), an IAM role with a trust policy accepting the envoy's federated identity, and Recipe-Bottle-shipped IAM policy shapes that the AWS consumer attaches to whatever principal consumes delivered artifacts (EC2 role, ECS task role, Bedrock execution role, etc.).
Provisioned by the payor via operations congruent with
{rbtgi_depot}
creation — the AWS consumer's embassy is the sovereign-setup equivalent of the Manor's depot.
Recipe Bottle ships provisioning templates;
the AWS consumer owns the resulting resources.
_Trigger_: first concrete need to deliver artifacts to an AWS workload.
_Reference_: this roadmap entry;
design conversation pending heat.

**Envoy — GCP-side cloud-native cross-post actor.**
A
{giam_service_account}
in the depot, sibling to existing cloud-native SAs, whose sole purpose is executing cross-cloud pushes to an accredited embassy.
The envoy holds no long-lived AWS credentials;
it federates to AWS via OIDC Workload Identity Federation, exchanging short-lived GCP tokens for short-lived AWS STS credentials at operation time.
Preserves credential locality: Director holds only GCP credentials on the workstation, envoy holds only federation configuration in GCP, and AWS admin credentials never enter Recipe Bottle's custody.
_Trigger_: embassy entry above.
_Reference_: this roadmap entry.

**Bullion — AWS-side content-addressed artifact.**
Recipe Bottle's delivered artifact in an embassy bucket: a content-addressed filesystem image (squashfs/erofs, optionally with dm-verity) with Object Lock applied for compliance-grade retention.
A bullion may carry a vessel image payload (the "Director pushes a vessel to AWS" motivator), model weights, or any other content-addressed corpus.
Sibling-but-distinct from the hoard: hoards stay GCS-native and depot-resident, preserving the existing provenance story;
bullions are the new AWS-side citizen with S3-native retention semantics.
Provenance transports as naming-convention sibling objects (attestation JSON adjacent to the image blob), not as OCI referring artifacts, because the bullion is not an OCI artifact.
_Trigger_: embassy entry above.
_Reference_: this roadmap entry;
Artifact Preservation section (hoard entry) for the GCP sibling concept.

**Accredit / Recall — governor operations for embassy binding lifecycle.**
Accredit: governor configures the envoy's OIDC federation against an embassy's IAM role trust policy, and records the binding (embassy name, AWS account, region, role ARN, bucket name) in depot state.
Recall: governor removes the federation configuration and prunes the binding.
Follows the existing governor verb register (mantles, charters, knights, forfeits) with diplomatic-register verbs appropriate for foreign-court relationships.
A single depot may be accredited to multiple embassies.
_Trigger_: embassy entry above.
_Reference_: this roadmap entry.

**Entrust — director-triggered cross-post operation.**
Director invokes entrust on a specific hallmark (or other deliverable) with a target embassy name.
The envoy executes the cross-post from within Cloud Build: federates to the embassy's IAM role, streams bytes to S3, applies Object Lock retention.
Director holds no AWS credentials;
failure modes surface through the existing Cloud Build event stream.
_Trigger_: embassy entry above.
_Reference_: this roadmap entry.

**Chantry — Bedrock-hosted inference endpoint.**
Architecturally distinct from the embassy/bullion pattern.
A chantry is a Bedrock-registered custom model (or a native Bedrock foundation model) invoked by a
{at_bottle_container}
over the crucible conduit architecture — not an artifact Recipe Bottle delivers, but a foreign-sovereign service a bottle consumes.
Weights may originate as a bullion (entrusted to an embassy, then consumed by Bedrock Custom Model Import on the AWS side), but the chantry itself is the registered-and-invocable endpoint, not the weight storage.
Pairs with the "Cloud service conduit nameplate" entry for the invocation path.
_Trigger_: first concrete Bedrock inference requirement;
near-term per the Crucible Conduit Architecture section above.
_Reference_: this roadmap entry;
`Memos/memo-20260415-crucible-conduit-architecture.md`;
Crucible Conduit Architecture section above.

**Egress lockdown — IMPLEMENTED.**
Dual-pool architecture: tether pool (public egress) and airgap pool (`NO_PUBLIC_EGRESS`).
Operation-level routing: inscribe/enshrine use tether, about/vouch always airgap, conjure/bind per vessel `RBRV_EGRESS_MODE`.
Reliquaries (tool images) and anchors (base images) provide universal GAR-sourced dependencies.
_Reference_: RBSCB-CloudBuildPosture.adoc (Implemented: Egress Lockdown via Dual Pools).

**VPC Service Controls perimeter.**
Wrap depot project in VPC-SC perimeter restricting API access.
Requires egress lockdown (now implemented) as prerequisite, org-level admin for perimeter creation, and verification of every GCP API call in the pipeline.
_Trigger_: organizational security policy mandating VPC-SC, or data exfiltration risk assessment.
_Reference_: RBSCB-CloudBuildPosture.adoc (Potential Hardening: VPC Service Controls).
Cross-reference: if a VPC is stood up for the crucible conduit architecture (above), design the VPC-SC perimeter to serve both Cloud Build egress and bottle conduit consumers.

**Cosign image signing.**
Cryptographic signatures on images, verifiable by downstream consumers.
KMS-backed keyed signing preferred for airgap-pool compatibility.
Currently redundant given CB-native SLSA v1.0 Build Level 3 attestation.
_Trigger_: external image distribution, trust verification needed outside GCP, or builder strategy expanding beyond Google Cloud Build.
_Reference_: RBSCB-CloudBuildPosture.adoc (Deferred Items: Cosign).

**Binary Authorization.**
Deploy-time policy enforcement (only attested images can be deployed).
Technically achievable now with CB-native SLSA Level 3.
Not beneficial until deployment targets include GKE or Cloud Run.
_Trigger_: deployment targets change from Podman workstations to managed container platforms.
_Reference_: RBSCB-CloudBuildPosture.adoc (Deferred Items: Binary Authorization).

**Dockerfile base image digest validation.**
Builder step images are digest-pinned, but user-authored Dockerfiles in vessel build contexts are not validated.
A vessel with `FROM ubuntu:22.04` uses a mutable tag.
Constrains vessel authors (policy decision).
_Trigger_: vessel ecosystem matures beyond internal-only use.
_Reference_: RBSCB-CloudBuildPosture.adoc (Deferred Items: Dockerfile base image).

**Reproducibility verification.**
Periodic independent rebuilds compared by digest.
Requires reproducible toolchains — a vessel-author discipline, not a pipeline control.
_Trigger_: vessel ecosystem maturity and external distribution.
_Reference_: RBSCB-CloudBuildPosture.adoc (Deferred Items: Reproducibility).

**Operator federation — external-IdP identity for depot participants.**
Recipe Bottle grants
{rbtgi_depot}
capabilities to identities the depot's IAM recognizes.
The shipping model is the keyfile tier: each operator role holds a service-account key file (the
{rbtgi_rbra_file})
on disk, possession of the key is the identity, and no GCP organization is required.
Operator federation is the heavyweight successor tier for installations that cannot accept an operator secret on disk.
The depot owner configures the
{rbtgi_depot}
to trust an external OIDC/SAML identity provider (any compliant issuer — self-hosted Dex/Keycloak, or GCP Identity Platform as an in-house IdP), then grants capabilities to those federated `principal://` subjects: keyless, no Google accounts for participants, revocation central at the depot.
The
{rbtgi_depot}
remains the home of the participant roster (the role-to-identity mapping); the IdP owns only identity proofing.
Org-only — federation pools are organization-level (Workforce Identity Federation, the human-identity product), so a no-org
{rbtr_payor}
cannot use this tier; the organization requirement is the line between the free keyfile tier and the federation tier.
A qualifying organization is free via Cloud Identity once its owner verifies control of a DNS domain, so the true prerequisite is that the operator controls a domain — not that they pay Google.
The Google-side credential path is curl-native — an STS token exchange (RFC 8693), no gcloud and no added stack dependency; only the IdP-auth leg (device flow where the issuer supports it) varies by issuer.
Distinct from the envoy's egress federation (GCP-to-AWS delivery, the machine-identity Workload product): this is
{at_operator}
*access* identity arriving the other way.
_Trigger_: first concrete need from an organization that cannot accept on-disk operator keyfiles, or when the keyfile tier's secret-distribution and rotation burden materially blocks adoption rather than being theoretically frictionful.
_Reference_: `Memos/memo-20260527-operator-credential-models.md` (the source document for this entry — the committed two-tier plan: keyfile (free, no-org) and Workforce federation (paid, org-required), the curl-native two-leg flow, device-flow / 12-hour session cap, the per-depot mode enum homed in RBRD, and the cult-verb shape);
`Memos/memo-20260522-org-affiliated-credential-reorientation.md` (R1/R2/R3 credential model and three-tier ladder, plus R4/R5 test-rig and gauntlet resolutions);
`Memos/memo-20260427-google-native-human-auth.md` (impersonation mechanism detail — its human-auth *framing* is superseded by R2);
Google docs: Workforce Identity Federation overview (`https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/workforce-identity-federation`) and short-lived token exchange (`https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/workforce-obtaining-short-lived-credentials`).

13	Idempotent establish: a pre-existing SA is not drift — its identity (email, `uniqueId`) and IAM bindings are preserved and only the credential key is rotated; an absent SA is created first. Either path ends with exactly one fresh USER_MANAGED key and the role's bindings in place.

46	Service Account* (when `«SA_EXISTS»` is false):

64	.. {rbbc_poll}
65	with backoff until HTTP 200 (newly-created SA may not be immediately resolvable by email)
66	.. NOTE: Poll must use the email address, not the numeric ID. The email-based lookup path has independent propagation from the UID-based path, and subsequent steps (keys.list, keys.create) all use the email address. Polling by UID can succeed while the email path still returns 404.
67	.. NOTE: Skipped when `«SA_EXISTS»` is true — a pre-existing SA is already propagated.

NOTE: Keys subresource read-path may lag SA email-path readiness — the SA GET probe (previous step) can return 200 while `keys.list` on the same email immediately returns 404, observed as a non-monotonic 200→200→404 flap on freshly-minted depots. See `Memos/memo-20260514-pristine-gauntlet-iam-flap.md` for transcript evidence and <<rbscip_trade_study>> for the doctrinal pattern.
77	.. {rbbc_store}

50	link:https://cloud.google.com/iam/docs/reference/rest/v1/projects.serviceAccounts/list[iam.serviceAccounts.list]
51	for
52	`«INPUT_DEPOT_PROJECT_ID»`
53	.. {rbbc_store}
54	`«EXISTING_GOVERNORS»` as service accounts matching pattern `governor-*`
55	.. For each service account in `«EXISTING_GOVERNORS»`:
56	... {rbbc_call}
57	link:https://cloud.google.com/iam/docs/reference/rest/v1/projects.serviceAccounts/delete[iam.serviceAccounts.delete]
58	to remove the service account
59	... {rbbc_require}
60	deletion successful (HTTP 200) or already deleted (HTTP 404)
61	.. {rbbc_show}
62	count of deleted governor service accounts
63	
64	//axhos_step
65	. *Generate Governor Timestamp*:
66	.. {rbbc_store}
67	`«TIMESTAMP»` as current time in YYYYMMDDHHmm format
68	.. {rbbc_store}
69	`«GOVERNOR_ACCOUNT_ID»` as `governor-«TIMESTAMP»`
70	
71	//axhos_step
72	. *Create the
73	{rbtr_governor}
74	{giam_service_account}*:
75	.. {rbbc_call}
76	link:https://cloud.google.com/iam/docs/reference/rest/v1/projects.serviceAccounts/create[iam.serviceAccounts.create]
77	to create service account in
78	`«INPUT_DEPOT_PROJECT_ID»`
79	.. Set `accountId` to `«GOVERNOR_ACCOUNT_ID»`
80	.. Use display name: `«RBGC_DEPOT_DISPLAY_PREFIX» governor «RBRD_DEPOT_MONIKER»`
81	.. {rbbc_store}
82	`«GOVERNOR_SERVICE_ACCOUNT»` from response `.email` field
83	.. {rbbc_require}
84	service account creation successful (HTTP 200)
85	
86	//axhos_step
87	. *Verify
88	{giam_service_account}
89	Propagation*:
90	.. {rbbc_call}
91	link:https://cloud.google.com/iam/docs/reference/rest/v1/projects.serviceAccounts/get[iam.serviceAccounts.get]
92	to query service account using
93	`«GOVERNOR_SERVICE_ACCOUNT»`
94	.. {rbbc_poll}
95	with 3-5 second intervals for up to 30 seconds if service account not immediately available
96	.. {rbbc_require}
97	service account accessible before proceeding
98	
99	//axhos_step
100	. *Grant
101	{gcp_project_id}
102	-level Permissions*:
103	.. {rbtoe_iam_grant_project}
104	to update IAM policy on
105	`«INPUT_DEPOT_PROJECT_ID»`
106	.. {rbbc_use}
107	`«GOVERNOR_SERVICE_ACCOUNT»` as principal
108	.. Add role: `roles/owner` (excluding billing permissions maintained by
109	{rbtr_payor})
110	// roles/cloudbuild.connectionViewer eliminated — CB v2 connections no longer used
111	.. Set policy version: 3 for conditional binding support
112	.. {rbbc_require}
113	IAM policy update successful
114	.. NOTE: The `roles/owner` grant propagates to project-scope and SA-scope IAM caches effectively immediately, but resource-scope IAM caches (AR repo, bucket, secret) may lag.  Downstream operations that target resource-scope endpoints (notably {rbtgo_director_invest}'s AR repo IAM step) absorb this lag via the class C propagation tolerance in the grant harness.  See <<rbscig_iam_grant_contracts>> propagation catalog.
115	
116	//axhos_step
117	. *Generate
118	{rbtgi_rbra_file}*:
119	.. {rbbc_call}
120	link:https://cloud.google.com/iam/docs/reference/rest/v1/projects.serviceAccounts.keys/create[serviceAccounts.keys.create]
121	to create service account key for
122	`«GOVERNOR_SERVICE_ACCOUNT»`
123	.. Set keyAlgorithm: `KEY_ALG_RSA_2048`, privateKeyType: `TYPE_GOOGLE_CREDENTIALS_FILE`
124	.. Retry up to `RBGC_SA_KEY_CREATE_RETRY_MAX` times on HTTP 404 with `RBGC_SA_KEY_CREATE_RETRY_DELAY_SEC` delay between attempts
125	.. NOTE: SA read operations (GET by email, keys.list) may succeed while keys.create POST returns 404 "does not exist" due to write-path propagation delay. Google link:https://cloud.google.com/iam/docs/service-accounts-create[documents]: "you might need to wait 60 seconds or more before you use the service account." The 404 confirms no key was created, making retry safe. See <<rbscig_iam_grant_contracts>>.
126	.. {rbbc_store}
127	`«GOVERNOR_PRIVATE_KEY_DATA»` from response `.privateKeyData` field
128	.. {rbtoe_rbra_generate}
129	converts
130	`«GOVERNOR_PRIVATE_KEY_DATA»`
131	to
132	`{rbrr_governor_rbra_file}`
133	.. NOTE: Script outputs copy command for RBRA file installation
134	.. {rbbc_warn}
135	if
136	{rbrr_governor_rbra_file}
137	already exists (will be overwritten)
138	.. {rbbc_require}
139	RBRA file creation successful
140	
141	// CB v2 Connection Viewer verification step eliminated — CB v2 connections no longer used
142	
143	//axhoot_typed_output
144	{rbtr_governor}
145	. *Display Result*:
146	.. {rbbc_show}
147	governor reset successful
148	.. {rbbc_show}
149	governor service account: `«GOVERNOR_SERVICE_ACCOUNT»`
150	.. {rbbc_show}
151	RBRA file path:
152	{rbrr_governor_rbra_file}
153	
154	//axhoc_completion
155	Successful execution resets governor state.
