aiTitle: Review and refine BZ idea for clean integration

message.content: Yes, dig in further- lets see if we can make deft repairs that genuinely improve our target model.

content.text:
Let me drill into the roster reverse-mapping, because the deeper I push the more it turns into the keystone — and it cracks a cinched decision that I think is actually wrong under this model.

## The keystone repair: the "no stored list" cinch is self-defeating under decoupling

The paddock cinches: *"Roster derives from IAM (an `rbk-identity` label…), not a stored list — it cannot drift."* That was a **good** cinch in the fused world and a **broken** one in the decoupled world. Here's the mechanism.

**Why "derive from IAM" worked before:** the SA name carried the role (`director-alice`). Deriving the roster was a name regex — cheap *and* lossless, because the role was written on the identity.

**Why it breaks now:** drop the role prefix and a director capability-set is ~7 bindings smeared across **four** policies (project IAM, the Mason SA, the repo, the build bucket). To roster you'd read all four, reassemble per-identity, and reverse-match the binding-set against the named sets. That reverse-match is:

- **Lossy** — IAM bindings carry no "granted as part of the director set" tag. You're inferring intent from effect.
- **Ambiguous** — once two capability-sets share any binding, holding that binding alone doesn't classify. It's set-cover with ties.
- **Blind to partials** — a half-failed grant (4 of 7 bindings) is now *representable*, and reverse-match can only call it "not a director," silently dropping a drifting identity instead of flagging it.

And here's the part that retires the cinch outright: **you cannot audit IAM against itself.** If "intended" is *defined as* "whatever IAM currently says," then intended-vs-actual is a tautology that always passes. The cinch's stated virtue — "it cannot drift" — is really "it cannot *detect* drift," because it threw away the intent that drift is measured against. Under decoupling, drift between *what we meant to grant* and *what IAM enforces* is exactly the signal you want (a half-failed grant, a manual console edit, a revoke that didn't land). The cinch makes that signal unobservable.

The cinch quietly conflated two things:
- *"No **enforcement** state outside IAM"* — **true, keep it.** Enforcement is server-side IAM; nothing client-side is ever the boundary.
- *"No **intent** state may exist"* — **false corollary, drop it.** Intent is advisory/audit, never an enforcement gate, so it doesn't violate the real invariant.

**Deft repair: revise the cinch to "IAM is ground truth for *enforcement*; a depot-resident declared roster is ground truth for *intent*; routine roster reads intent, and the audit reports declared-vs-IAM drift as a feature, not a hazard."**

## Why the declared roster unifies the tiers (and is load-bearing, not optional)

The thing that makes this *deft* rather than just *more state*: **federation forces a declared roster anyway, so adopting it in keyfile is paid-once for both tiers.** A Workforce principal is `principal://…/subject/<opaque IdP claim>` — no name prefix is ever possible, so federation roster is *pure* reverse-mapping with zero name help. The memo already concedes this: *"the depot remains the home of the participant roster."* So:

- Federation **must** have a depot-resident roster.
- Keyfile, post-decoupling, **needs** one too (reverse-mapping is lossy and unauditable).
- Give them the **same** one → roster becomes "read the declared roster, optionally audit against IAM," **identical in both tiers, O(1) for the routine path.** That's another piece of structural coherence the model gets for free.

This promotes the paddock's "intended-roster" from *open fork* to *the mechanism the model runs on*. It was never optional legibility flavor — it's the instrument decoupling requires.

A clean separation falls out, and it respects the other cinch ("capability-sets are named *code*, not config"):
- **Code** defines the sets: `director := {7 bindings across 4 scopes}`.
- **Data** (the declared roster) records membership: `alice := {director, retriever}`.
- **Audit** = for each `(identity, declared-set)`, are the code-defined bindings actually present in IAM? Drift is the diff.

## A factual catch underneath the cinch — verify before relying on it

The cinch's fallback marker is *"an `rbk-identity` label marks RBK-managed SAs."* I'm fairly confident **service accounts don't support a labels map** — the `projects.serviceAccounts` resource has `displayName`, `description`, `disabled`, `uniqueId`… but no `labels` field (longstanding GCP gap). If that holds, the label mechanism the cinch leans on **doesn't exist** — the marker would have to live in `description`, which is editable free text and a weak anchor.

Worth a 5-minute verify before it's load-bearing anywhere — but note the repair **doesn't depend on the answer**: the depot-resident declared roster subsumes the "which SAs are RBK-managed" need (managed-set = the roster's keys) *and* beats the label on coherence even if labels did exist. So this catch only removes a fallback, it doesn't threaten the recommendation.

## Three more repairs that fell out while I was in there

**1. Mantle isn't a special verb — it dissolves.** Once add/grant are generic, `invest` = add-person + grant(set), and `mantle` = add-person + grant(**governor**-set), run by the **payor** instead of the governor. The only differences are *parameters*: the **actor** (payor vs governor) and the **capability-set** (owner-minus-billing vs director). So the whole role-verb table — invest / divest / mantle / roster — collapses to **add / remove / grant / revoke / rekey + read-roster**, parameterized by `(actor, capability-set, identity)`. The paddock gestures at this; the deft version is realizing mantle stops *existing* as a distinct concept. The authority hierarchy (payor→governor→dir/ret) survives intact as a policy question: *who may grant which set* — which is also your governor-breadth fork, now expressible as an audit query (*"which identities hold owner?"*) rather than a structural special case.

**2. Grant/revoke operate only on named capability-sets — never raw bindings.** This is the Interface-Contamination-flavored guard that keeps the roster auditable: if you allowed raw-binding grants, the declared roster would have to go binding-valued and the named-set audit would collapse back into reverse-mapping. Forbid raw grants; the only grant vocabulary is the named set. Clean, ROE-aligned, and cheap to enforce.

**3. The declared roster doubles as the transactionality anchor.** A capability-set spanning four policies is a non-atomic grant — partial failure is possible. Repair: **write intent first** (roster: `alice += director`), *then* converge IAM to match. If binding application half-fails, the very next audit shows `alice: declared director, missing repoAdmin` and a re-run of the idempotent grant heals it. This is exactly the rbk-08 idempotent-invest discipline (declare goal → converge → re-runnable), generalized — so it's reuse, not new machinery.

## And a fairer read on the blast-radius tradeoff I raised last turn

I called the union key (multi-role on one SA) a blast-radius *widening*, and the resting exposure genuinely is larger. But decoupling **also improves the response**, and I undersold that: in the fused model your only incident lever is delete-and-recreate the whole SA. Decoupled, the levers are granular and orthogonal — suspected director-capability misuse → `revoke director from alice` (she keeps retriever + her identity + her key); suspected key compromise → `rekey alice` (capabilities untouched). You can sever *what she can do* without touching *who she is*, and vice versa. So the honest net is: **larger resting target, finer incident response** — and the finer response exists *only because* of the decoupling. That's a real merit, not just a cost to swallow.

---

Where this leaves the model: the decoupling holds up under hard pushing and actually got *cleaner* — the roster problem I flagged dissolves once you accept that intent must be stored (federation proves it), the verb table collapses further than the paddock claimed, and the blast-radius story is more favorable than I first said. The one thing that *changed*, not just clarified, is the cinch: "no stored list" has to go.

Two places I'd drill next, your pick:
- **The audit's exact comparison semantics** — what counts as "drift" when IAM holds *more* than declared (someone granted out-of-band) vs *less* (a failed grant), and whether the audit auto-heals or only reports. This is where the model meets the messy Pale of manual console edits.
- **The actor/authority axis** — "who may grant which capability-set" is now the only real seam left with policy content (it's where the governor-breadth fork actually lives), and it's the same question federation will ask of the governor principal.

Want me to take one of those, or consolidate everything so far into a revised model statement you can react to?

lastPrompt: Yes, dig in further- lets see if we can make deft repairs that genuinely improve our target model.

aiTitle: Review and refine BZ idea for clean integration

message.content: Drill into both of those.

content.text:
Both drills turn out to interlock — the value of the audit (drill 1) depends on the authority topology (drill 2). Let me take them in order and then show the seam between them.

# Drill 1 — Audit comparison semantics

The audit compares three things: **declared roster** (intent: `alice := {director, retriever}`), **capability-set code** (`director := {7 bindings across 4 scopes}`), and **IAM** (actual bindings). For one identity, reduce the declared sets to a binding-set `D` and her actual RBK-relevant bindings to `A`. The comparison is not binary — enumerate the real state space:

| State | Meaning | Likely cause |
|---|---|---|
| `D = A` | clean | — |
| **deficit-whole** (`D` has a set wholly absent in `A`) | declared director, holds nothing director-ish | grant never ran / errant revoke |
| **deficit-fragment** (`D`'s set partly present) | holds 4 of director's 7 bindings | the half-failed multi-scope grant |
| **surplus-complete** (`A` has a whole *named* set not in `D`) | effectively a retriever, undeclared | out-of-band grant |
| **surplus-fragment** (`A` has bindings belonging to no whole set) | rogue/stranded bindings | console tampering, or a capability-set definition that shrank |

### The keystone: healing must be **asymmetric**, and the asymmetry *is* the Pale test

The tempting design — "drift is drift, converge it" — is a trap, and the two directions fail in opposite, instructive ways:

- **Auto-heal surplus by *revoking*** → you've built a tool that strips a working operator's privileges from a stale text file. One bookkeeping lag = an outage you *caused*.
- **Auto-heal surplus by *backfilling intent*** (roster learns to accept whatever IAM says) → the audit becomes a tautology again. This is the "can't audit IAM against itself" failure re-entering through the back door.

So surplus has no safe auto-heal. Deficit does. The split lands **exactly on the realm boundary**, which is why it's principled rather than arbitrary:

- **Deficit is *ours*** — our own grant mechanism failed to complete. Full sovereignty: **auto-converge up to `D`** (re-run the idempotent grant), *logged, never silent*. This is literally the transactionality recovery from last turn.
- **Surplus crossed the *Pale*** — someone touched IAM outside our verbs. We **characterize** it precisely (which bindings, whole set or fragment), **contain** it (report), **never paper over** (no auto-backfill), and a **human adjudicates** ("legitimize → declare it" or "reject → revoke it"). Detective, not preventive — because that's the only honest posture toward a boundary we don't own.

Concretely that means: **audit** is a pure read verb that reports drift categorized; **converge** is a separate verb (or flag) that heals *deficits only*; **no verb ever auto-revokes** — the destructive operation is always human-driven. The asymmetry isn't a policy choice bolted on; it's the recognition test ("can I edit the source of the failure?") applied per drift category.

### Three repairs that fall out of the asymmetry

**Intent-first makes surplus a clean bypass signal.** Recall the repair that grant writes the roster *first*, then converges IAM. Consequence: our own tooling can essentially never *produce* a surplus (it always declares before it grants). So **a surplus-complete is near-proof that someone went around the verbs** — console grant, manual escalation. The intent-first discipline turns surplus from ambiguous noise into a sharp "Pale was crossed here" diagnostic.

**Capability-set versioning rides the same asymmetry, for free.** When you edit a set's definition in code (it's code, per the cinch), every identity granted under the old shape drifts. Additions show up as **deficit** (auto-converge: re-run grant across the roster — idempotent, safe). Removals show up as **surplus-fragment** (report: a human confirms the now-orphaned binding should be revoked, because auto-revoking on a definition edit could just be propagating a mistake in the edit). Definition changes need no special machinery — they decompose into the two categories the audit already handles.

**The orphan-identity hole — and a one-job repair (keyfile only).** Here's a snag the audit exposes: with no name prefix *and* (per last turn's catch) no SA labels, **nothing on an SA marks it as RBK-managed.** The roster becomes the *only* record of which SAs are ours. So a failed `add-person` (SA + key minted, roster write failed) leaves **a live key for an SA nobody tracks** — a credential leak you can't even find to clean up. Two ways out: (a) define RBK-ownership *as* roster membership (clean ontology, but the leaked key is now "not ours" by definition and stays lost — unacceptable for a key), or (b) put a **faint sentinel in the SA `description`** (`rbk:…`) used *only* for an orphan-sweep (list SAs, filter by sentinel, diff against roster keys). I'd take (b): the description earns *exactly one* job — orphan detection — while the roster owns capability intent. Note this hazard is **keyfile-specific**: federation has no key to leak, and a stray `principal://` binding is visible in the IAM policy itself, so it's just a surplus the audit already catches. Keyfile carries key-custody concerns federation doesn't — consistent with the tier split.

**Audit domain boundary.** `A` is scoped to *roster identities × bindings named in some capability-set definition*. A roster identity holding a role outside every definition = surplus-fragment (flag it). A *non*-roster principal (payor, Google service agents) holding roles = not ours, not audited. Clean edge.

# Drill 2 — The actor/authority axis

The only seam left with real policy content is "who may grant which capability-set." Intended DAG: **payor → grants governor-set; governor → grants director/retriever-sets.** The governor-breadth fork asks whether that second arrow is a real wall.

### The governing fact: authority boundaries are real *only* where they fall on resource-scope splits

The payor/governor boundary **is** enforced — but examine *why*: billing lives at the **billing-account** resource, a different scope than the project, and governor's `roles/owner` excludes it. The wall is real because it coincides with a resource boundary, not because of any role-scoping cleverness.

The governor/director boundary has **no such resource split** — both live in the project IAM policy, and governor holds owner over the whole thing. So the intended "governor may grant *only* director/retriever" is **honor-system**: a governor with owner can `setIamPolicy` to grant any role to anyone, including **minting peer governors** or escalating itself.

### Correcting the fork: IAM cannot scope grants by role

The paddock frames horn (b) as "scope governor so the rung is a hard IAM wall — needs IAM Deny/Conditions." I believe that's **largely infeasible**, and it's worth knowing before betting on it:

- IAM **Conditions** evaluate request/resource *attributes* (resource name, type, time…). They have **no access to the *payload* of a `setIamPolicy` call** — there is no condition attribute for "which roles are in this policy." So "governor may setIamPolicy *only* to grant director/retriever" is **inexpressible**.
- IAM **Deny** matches on permission + resource + principal, not payload either. It can deny governor `setIamPolicy` *entirely* or *on a resource* — but a blanket deny also stops governor from doing its legitimate granting.

So GCP offers no "may grant only role X." (I'm confident here, but flag it as **verify-before-building** — it's the fact that decides whether role-scoped (b) is even on the table.) The honest conclusion: **horn (b) as IAM-scoping doesn't exist. The only lever is topology — *where the grant authority lives*, not *what a standing owner is allowed to grant*.**

### The model's own cinch already decides the fork

Here's the internal-consistency finding. The paddock cinches: *"any honor-system gate a malicious operator bypasses by not running our client."* Horn (a) — "keep governor broad, catch dangerous unions by roster audit" — **is** an honor-system/detective gate against a malicious governor. By the model's own stated principle, that's not a real boundary. So the fork was never balanced: **the model's cinch condemns horn (a)**, and drill-1 confirms why — surplus-detection against the governor is *detective-only*, and the governor is the very authority you'd be detecting after-the-fact (keys exfiltrated, images poisoned before the next audit run).

### The feasible resolution is topological — and tier-dependent

Since you can't scope a standing owner, the move is to ask whether the standing owner should *exist*. Apply the ROE load-bearing test to the governor itself: **the governor's entire reason for being is delegation** — it lets a *non-payor* admin run day-to-day SA management without billing power. So:

- **Keyfile tier (no-org, realistically solo or tiny — your case):** the payor and the would-be governor-admin are *the same human*. A standing SA with project owner buys nothing here and is pure attack surface (a resting key with owner). The deft move: **pull grant authority up to the payor** — payor grants director/retriever directly — and either drop the standing governor or demote it to non-privileged. The governor-escalation threat doesn't get *detected*; it gets *structurally removed* (no second omnipotent identity to compromise).
- **Federation tier (org-required, multi-admin plausible):** delegation has real value, *and* the governor principal has no exfiltrable key (compromise requires IdP-session theft, centrally revocable). So a standing delegated governor is far more tolerable. The answer legitimately differs.

So: **the governor-breadth answer is tier-dependent, not a single global cinch.** Keyfile wants minimal/no standing governor; federation may keep a real one. The model should *let it differ* — which it can, because the capability-set and actor are already parameters.

(If you do want a delegated governor in keyfile, the consolation prize the decoupling unlocks is an **ephemeral governor**: grant the governor-set to a short-lived identity for one admin op, then revoke — cheap now that grant/revoke are generic. It shrinks resting owner-exposure from "always" to "during admin ops." Probably over-engineered for solo keyfile, but it's a real option that *only exists because of* the decoupling.)

# The seam between the two drills

They're coupled, and naming the coupling is itself a model improvement:

> **The security value of the audit's surplus-detection is a function of the authority topology.** With a broad standing governor (horn a), surplus-detection is your *only* governor-escalation tripwire — load-bearing, and per the cinch, inadequate against a malicious actor. With grant authority pulled to the payor (keyfile), the malicious-escalation path is closed *structurally*, and surplus-detection demotes to hygiene (catching honest console slips on a surface you already trust as root).

The principle, stated generally: **don't lean on detective audit to contain a threat you can close structurally by topology.** Drill 2 closes it structurally where it matters (keyfile); drill 1's audit then does the lower-stakes hygiene job it's actually good at. That division — structural closure for the malicious case, detective audit for the honest-mistake case — is the clean resting state for both.

---

We've done substantial design across four turns now, and a few cinches have genuinely *moved* (the "no stored list" cinch is out; the governor-breadth fork is resolved tier-dependently; the audit's heal-asymmetry, the orphan sentinel, and grant-authority topology are new). I think the highest-value next step is to **consolidate all of this into a revised model statement** — a clean restatement of the target model with the repairs folded in and the retired cinches marked — that you can react to as a whole rather than holding five turns of deltas in your head. Want me to write that up (I can stage it as the revised paddock, or just as a memo/scratch you review first)? Or is there another seam you want to pressure-test before we freeze it?

lastPrompt: Drill into both of those.

input.content:
# jjezs_paddock ₣BZ

## Boundary — shape, not mechanism (cinched)

This heat adopts the federation *shape* in keyfile backing — without building the
federation *mechanism*. The backing stays keyfile (service-account keys); a no-org
operator runs it unchanged. Explicitly OUT of scope: Workforce pool/provider setup,
STS token exchange, IdP device flow, and the RBRD mode-enum activation — those belong
to the federation tier's own future work (RBSHR "Operator federation"; memo-20260527).

The payoff is sharper than "the vocabulary stays the same." The decoupling below makes
keyfile's *capability layer structurally identical* to federation's — same gesture, same
GCP IAM mechanism — so a later federation switch reshapes nothing in that layer; it adds
one identity-backing and one token path. This is structural isomorphism, not nominal
vocabulary stability, and it is the real reason the switch is non-disruptive.

NOTE — supersedes the source memo on one point. memo-20260527 says the operator verbs
"keep their names, only their bodies differ," and that keyfile holds "one SA key file each
*per role*." This heat goes further: the verb *bodies* converge in shape (not just names),
and keyfile becomes one SA *per person*, not per role. memo-20260527 is to be updated to
record this divergence when the model is frozen.

## The target model — identity decoupled from capability

The spine: **an identity's lifecycle is independent of its capability set**, exactly as in
federation. This is native to the GCP primitive — a service account can exist with zero IAM
bindings, and bindings are added/removed per-binding. Today's `invest` *fuses*
identity-creation with capability-granting; the model unfuses them. (Tractability holds: the
token layer is already identity-based — a minted token asserts only "I am this SA" and Google
enforces that SA's bindings server-side — so what is being removed is a *selection
convention*, not enforcement.)

- **Identity** = one principal per person, named for the person (no `director-` prefix). In
  keyfile backing it is a service account holding one RBRA key; in federation a `principal://`
  subject. Multi-role is first-class: one identity holds several capability sets at once.
- **Capability** = a *named set* of IAM grants (the role's binding list, lifted out of the
  invest bodies into code). The same set applies identically to an SA member (keyfile) or a
  `principal://` member (federation) — that sameness is the convergence.
- **The verbs dissolve** into a generic register parameterized by (actor, capability-set,
  identity): **add-person / remove-person / grant / revoke / rekey** + **read-roster**.
  `invest` = add-person + grant. `mantle` = add-person + grant(governor-set), run by the payor.
  `divest` = revoke + remove-person. `roster` = read the declared roster. Mantle stops existing
  as a distinct concept — it is the generic add+grant with the governor set and the payor as
  actor.
- **Capability verbs are backing-blind; only identity verbs branch** — and they branch *three*
  ways, not two. grant/revoke never branch (both tiers are an IAM binding on a pre-existing
  identity). add-person/remove-person/rekey branch on identity-backing: SA-key (keyfile),
  IdP-principal (federation), or OAuth-human (payor). rekey is keyfile-only (federation owns
  rotation at the IdP). The capability layer sits above all three, identical.
- **Grant/revoke operate only on named capability-sets, never raw bindings** — the
  interface-contamination guard that keeps the declared roster set-valued and auditable; a
  raw-binding grant would collapse the audit back into lossy reverse-mapping.

## The declared roster — intent ground-truth, distinct from enforcement

Enforcement is server-side IAM and nothing else. But *intent* — who is meant to hold which
capability — must be stored, for two reasons the decoupled model forces:

- Without a name prefix, deriving the roster from IAM is lossy reverse-mapping (a director's
  set is several bindings smeared across project + Mason SA + repo + bucket; partial and
  ambiguous matches become representable).
- You cannot audit IAM against itself. "Intent = whatever IAM says" makes intended-vs-actual a
  tautology that always passes, discarding the very drift signal the audit exists to find.

So a **depot-resident declared roster** records intent (`alice := {director, retriever}`); IAM
records enforcement; capability-set *code* defines what each set contains. Federation *forces*
this roster anyway — a `principal://` subject carries no role marker at all, and the memo
already concedes "the depot remains the home of the roster" — so adopting it in keyfile is paid
once for both tiers and makes **routine roster identical and O(1) across tiers**: read the
declared roster; touch IAM only when auditing drift. The intended-roster is therefore part of
the model, not an optional fork.

## The audit — drift as signal, healing asymmetric across the Pale

The audit diffs declared intent against IAM. Drift is not binary; the state space:
*deficit-whole* (a declared set wholly absent), *deficit-fragment* (a set partly present — the
half-failed multi-scope grant), *surplus-complete* (a whole undeclared set present), and
*surplus-fragment* (bindings belonging to no whole set — tampering, or a definition that
shrank).

Healing is **asymmetric**, and the asymmetry is the Pale recognition test applied:

- **Deficit is ours** — our own grant failed to complete. Auto-converge up to intent (re-run
  the idempotent grant), logged, never silent.
- **Surplus crossed the Pale** — IAM was touched outside our verbs. Characterize, report, and
  let a human adjudicate (legitimize → declare it, or reject → revoke it). Never auto-revoke (a
  stale roster would deprovision a working operator) and never auto-backfill intent (that
  re-enters the audit-against-itself tautology). No verb auto-revokes; the destructive
  operation is always human-driven.

Consequences: because grant writes intent *first*, our tooling cannot produce a surplus, so a
surplus is a clean "someone bypassed the verbs" signal. A capability-set definition change
decomposes into deficit-on-additions (auto-heal) + surplus-on-removals (report) — no special
versioning machinery. The audit's domain is roster-identities × set-defined bindings;
non-roster principals are out of scope.

**Orphan hazard (keyfile only).** With no name prefix and (apparently) no SA labels, nothing on
an SA marks it RBK-managed, so a failed add-person can strand a live key for an untracked SA.
Repair: a faint sentinel in the SA `description`, used *only* for an orphan-sweep — the roster
owns capability, the description earns exactly that one job. Federation has no key to leak and a
stray `principal://` binding is visible in the policy, so this hazard is keyfile-specific.

## Authority topology — the one seam with policy content

"Who may grant which capability-set" is the last real policy seam. Intended: payor →
governor-set; governor → director/retriever-sets.

- **Authority boundaries are real only where they fall on resource-scope splits.** The
  payor/governor wall holds *because billing is a separate resource* that governor's
  `roles/owner` excludes. The governor/director wall has no such split — both live in project
  IAM and governor has owner over it — so "governor may grant only director/retriever" is
  honor-system.
- **IAM cannot scope grants by role** (verify): Conditions cannot read a setIamPolicy payload,
  and Deny matches permission/resource/principal, not payload — so a "hard IAM wall scoping the
  governor's grants" is largely infeasible.
- **The model's own cinch decides the fork.** "Honor-system gates a malicious operator
  bypasses" condemns the "keep governor broad, catch unions by audit" horn — that audit is
  detective-only against the very authority it watches.
- **Resolution is topological and tier-dependent.** Since a standing owner cannot be scoped,
  ask whether it should exist. The governor exists for *delegation* (a non-payor admin). In the
  keyfile tier (no-org, solo/tiny — payor and admin are the same human) a standing owner-holding
  SA buys nothing and is pure attack surface → pull grant authority up to the payor; drop or
  demote the standing governor. In the federation tier (org, multi-admin plausible, no
  exfiltrable key) a delegated governor is tolerable. The answer legitimately differs by tier,
  and the model allows it since actor and capability-set are already parameters. (Decoupling also
  unlocks an *ephemeral* governor — grant the governor-set for one op, then revoke — if a keyfile
  delegated governor is ever wanted.)

**The seam between audit and topology:** the security value of surplus-detection depends on the
topology. A broad standing governor makes surplus-detection the *only* escalation tripwire (and,
per the cinch, inadequate against malice); grant authority at the payor closes the escalation
path *structurally* and demotes the audit to honest-mistake hygiene. Do not substitute detective
audit for structural closure.

## Blast radius — larger resting target, finer response

One SA per person carrying the union of granted capabilities widens the keyfile resting target
(a leaked key carries more than a per-role key did). Accept this explicitly. It is bounded: the
widening lands only in the free keyfile tier, where the operator already holds an on-disk secret;
federation has no key at all. And the decoupling *improves incident response* in a way the fused
model cannot — revoke a capability without touching the identity/key, or rekey without touching
capabilities, where the old model's only lever was delete-and-recreate.

## Inherited concern — governor teardown leak

`rbgp_governor_mantle` deletes outgoing `governor-*` SAs without first revoking their grants,
minting a `deleted:…?uid=` project tombstone per re-mantle (several ghosts and counting on the
canonical depot). The convergence closes it for free: the generic revoke-before-delete covers the
governor like any identity, and an idempotent governor (rekey, not delete+recreate) drops the
delete entirely. Must reuse the rbk-08 revoke layer + Class-C propagation tolerance, not reinvent
them. If this heat defers or descopes the governor, the standalone fix (lean project-scope
revoke-before-delete in the mantle delete loop) is captured in
memo-20260605-governor-mantle-tombstone-leak.md.

## What done looks like

Every cult-verb surface migrated to the converged identity/capability vocabulary: workstation
bash (the role-keyed selection collapses to one identity accessor), the governor/payor admin
verbs (invest/divest/roster/mantle become add/grant/revoke/remove + read parameterized by
capability-set), cloud-side bash wherever it names roles, the .adoc specs that voice the cult
verbs, the Rust theurge cases that drive them, and the onboarding handbook tracks that teach them.

The load-bearing federation-enabling core within that surface is narrow and shippable with no
behavior change: route the role/file-path-keyed token-accessor calls through one identity-keyed
accessor (memo-20260527 "single code seam"). The vocabulary convergence is the larger,
motivated-but-not-required remainder. Scoping/heat-chopping is deferred — recorded here as shape
only.

## Cinched decisions

- Enforcement is server-side IAM. No file or client-side check is ever the boundary.
- Identity lifecycle is decoupled from capability lifecycle (the spine). Multi-role is
  first-class: one identity, several capability-sets.
- Keyfile is one SA per person (union key), not one per role — blast-radius tradeoff accepted
  (bounded; finer incident response in return).
- Capability-sets are named code (the IAM-role lists lifted out of the invest bodies); grant and
  revoke operate only on named sets, never raw bindings.
- The declared roster (intent) is part of the model; IAM is enforcement; the audit diffs them.
- Audit healing is asymmetric: auto-converge deficits (ours), report surplus (Pale); no verb
  auto-revokes.
- Governor breadth is resolved per tier, not globally: keyfile pulls grant authority to the payor
  / minimizes the standing governor; federation may keep a delegated governor.
- The mode enum (keyfile vs federation) belongs in RBRD and is added only when federation lands —
  not in this heat.
- Migration grandfathers existing role-named SAs: read grants from IAM, register them in the
  declared roster, let legacy `director-…`/`retriever-…` names age out. No flag day.
- The convergence covers *operator* identities; cloud-native identities (Mason, later Envoy)
  share the capability-set abstraction but not the person-shaped identity lifecycle.

## Retired cinches

- "Roster derives from IAM (an `rbk-identity` label), not a stored list — it cannot drift."
  Retired: under decoupling, deriving from IAM is lossy and makes the intended-vs-actual audit a
  tautology, and SA labels appear not to exist (verify). Replaced by the declared roster +
  asymmetric audit above. The *true* invariant it reached for — no enforcement state outside IAM —
  is preserved; only the false corollary (no intent state may exist) is dropped.

## Open — resolve within the heat

- **Where the declared roster physically lives** (GCS object in the depot bucket, GAR artifact,
  …) and its writer/etag discipline. It is mutable, high-churn depot state — unlike
  tripwire-protected RBRD.
- **Whether keyfile drops the standing governor entirely or demotes it** to non-privileged.
- **Verify the two Pale facts before building on them**: SA labels absent; IAM Conditions/Deny
  cannot scope a grant by the roles in its payload.
- **Operator add-person UX**: add-person yields a powerless credential; a separate grant makes it
  useful — confirm the two-gesture flow is the intended operator experience.

## Sources

The per-person design exploration in rbk-08-credential-repairs that birthed this heat;
memo-20260604-credential-churn-leak-and-propagation-races (the H1/H2 lifecycle split — the
enabler); memo-20260527-operator-credential-models (the two-tier plan and cult-verb shape — to be
updated per the divergence noted above); memo-20260605-governor-mantle-tombstone-leak (standalone
governor-leak fix); RBSHR "Operator federation". This revision also folds in the four-turn design
conversation of 260605 (identity/capability decoupling, the declared roster, asymmetric audit,
authority topology).

Done — paddock for ₣BZ rewritten and committed (`21cb5fb3`). Here's what moved, and where I'd most want your critical eye next turn.

## Structural changes from the original paddock

- **Reframed the boundary.** The payoff is now stated as *structural isomorphism* (keyfile's capability layer becomes byte-identical to federation's), not the weaker "vocabulary stays the same." Added an explicit **"supersedes the source memo"** note — memo-20260527 says verbs keep names / keyfile is one-key-*per-role*; this model converges verb *bodies* and goes one-SA-*per-person*.
- **New "target model" section** built on the decoupling spine: identity lifecycle independent of capability; verbs dissolve to add/remove/grant/revoke/rekey + read-roster; mantle stops being special; capability verbs backing-blind while identity verbs branch *three* ways (SA-key / IdP / OAuth-human); grant/revoke only on named sets.
- **New "declared roster" section** — the replacement for the retired cinch; federation forces it anyway, so it's paid-once for both tiers.
- **New "audit" section** — the four-way drift taxonomy and the keystone: *asymmetric healing as the Pale test* (auto-converge deficits, report surplus, never auto-revoke), plus the keyfile-only orphan sentinel.
- **New "authority topology" section** — boundaries real only at resource-scope splits; IAM can't scope grants by payload; the model's own cinch condemns the broad-governor+audit horn; tier-dependent topological resolution; and the audit↔topology seam.
- **New "blast radius" section**, reorganized **cinched / retired / open**, and "what done looks like" now separates the narrow token-accessor core from the larger convergence.

## Where I made judgment calls you should challenge

1. **I retired one of your cinches** ("roster derives from IAM, no stored list"). The covenant says cinches change only in conversation — we did discuss it, and you asked me to fold the work in, but retiring it is the single most consequential edit. Confirm or veto.
2. **I cinched "one SA per person, union key, tradeoff accepted."** You said you *want* multi-role + decoupled, which implies it — but I inferred your acceptance of the keyfile blast-radius widening. That inference is mine, not your stated words.
3. **The paddock got long.** It's richer than the original by a good margin, and a couple of sections (audit taxonomy, orphan sentinel) lean toward mechanism. By JJK posture that's a smell — tell me if it's over-filled and should be thinned back to pure shape, with the detail spun to a memo.
4. **Two unverified Pale facts** are load-bearing (SAs lack labels; IAM can't scope grants by policy payload). I flagged them as Open/verify, but the model *leans* on both — I have not run a check.
5. **"Convergence covers operators, not cloud-native (Mason/Envoy)"** is a boundary I added on my own read.
6. The heat is still named **rbk-14-mvp-prepare-federation-idea**, but the model has outgrown "prepare federation" — it's now an identity/capability model federation later plugs into. Relabel is your call.

It's staged for the critical review you asked for. I'd suggest we start next turn on items 1 and 2 — they're the load-bearing ones, and your answer reshapes the cinched list.

Links: [{"title":"Overview of IAM Conditions | Identity and Access Management (IAM) | Google Cloud Documentation","url":"https://cloud.google.com/iam/docs/conditions-overview"},{"title":"Set limits on granting roles | Identity and Access Management (IAM) | Google Cloud","url":"https://cloud.google.com/iam/docs/setting-limits-on-granting-roles"},{"title":"How to Create IAM Deny Policies to Enforce Security Guardrails in Google Cloud","url":"https://oneuptime.com/blog/post/2026-02-17-how-to-create-iam-deny-policies-to-enforce-security-guardrails-in-google-cloud/view"},{"title":"Use custom organization policies for allow policies | Identity and Access Management (IAM) | Google Cloud Documentation","url":"https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/org-policy-custom-constraints"},{"title":"Policies and permissions in AWS Identity and Access Management - AWS Identity and Access Management","url":"https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/access_policies.html"},{"title":"Control access to AWS resources using policies - AWS Identity and Access Management","url":"https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/access_controlling.html"},{"title":"IAM JSON policy elements: Condition - AWS Identity and Access Management","url":"https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/reference_policies_elements_condition.html"},{"title":"Preventing prod access with IAM Conditions | by Neil Kolban | Google Cloud - Community | Medium","url":"https://medium.com/google-cloud/preventing-prod-access-with-iam-conditions-c0d709a5cadc"}]

Based on the search results, here's information about using IAM conditions to restrict which roles can be granted with setIamPolicy in deny policies:

## Key Information

In a role binding for a role with the iam.projects.setIamPolicy permission, you can use a condition expression like `api.getAttribute('iam.googleapis.com/modifiedGrantsByRole', []).hasOnly(['roles/billing.admin'])` to allow a user to grant and revoke only the Billing Account Administrator role on the project.

Roles with permissions to grant and revoke IAM roles (those with permission names ending in setIamPolicy) can be restricted using conditions in allow policies.

## Important Limitations

However, there's a significant limitation when using deny policies: Deny policies cannot inspect the members being added to an allow policy, so they cannot directly block only external identities.

For deny policies specifically, while IAM Allow Policy takes Roles (collections of permissions) to allow, the IAM Deny Policy takes raw permissions for its denial specification. If you want to "cancel" a role for a group, you would have to unpack the role to its constituent permissions and then create a Deny policy for that set of permissions.

## Best Practice

Only projects, folders, and organizations accept conditions to limit role granting in their allow policies.

The API attribute `iam.googleapis.com/modifiedGrantsByRole` is the mechanism designed specifically for restricting which roles can be granted through conditions on principals with setIamPolicy permissions.

content.content:
# Google Cloud Service Account Tags Support

Based on the documentation provided:

## (1) Attaching Tags to Service Accounts
**Yes**, you can attach key-value tags to individual service accounts. The guide explicitly states: "This guide describes how to create and manage tags for service accounts."

## (2) Readability, Listability, and IAM Conditions Use
**Yes** on all counts:
- Tags are listable via `gcloud resource-manager tags bindings list`
- They can be used with IAM: "You can use tags to conditionally allow or deny policies based on whether a resource has a specific tag"
- The documentation mentions "Tags and access control" for using tags with IAM conditions

## (3) Tags vs. Labels Distinction
The documentation **does not mention resource labels** for service accounts or distinguish between tags and labels. It focuses exclusively on tags as "key-value pair[s]" attached via tag bindings.

## (4) Limits on Tags
The document **does not specify** any numeric limits on tag quantity, attachment methods, or other constraints beyond the requirement to "create a tag and configure its value" beforehand.

The feature is currently in **Preview** status, meaning it has limited support and may change.

content.content:
# Restricting IAM Role Granting via Conditions

## (1) Condition Attribute

The attribute is **`iam.googleapis.com/modifiedGrantsByRole`**, which contains the role names from bindings that a request modifies.

## (2) Example Expression Syntax

The basic format is:

```
api.getAttribute('iam.googleapis.com/modifiedGrantsByRole', []).hasOnly(['roles/pubsub.editor', 'roles/pubsub.publisher'])
```

This allows granting/revoking only the specified roles. You can include up to 10 roles in the list.

## (3) Resource Level Restrictions

**Key limitation:** The document explicitly states: "only projects, folders, and organizations accept conditions to limit role granting in their allow policies."

While other resource types recognize the `modifiedGrantsByRole` attribute, individual resources like buckets, Artifact Registry repos, or service accounts **do not support this restriction mechanism** in their policies. BigQuery datasets are a notable exception—they don't recognize the attribute but still allow limited admins to grant roles if permissions exist.

## (4) Other Key Limitations

- **Service recognition:** "Some Google Cloud services don't recognize limits on role granting," causing requests to fail
- **Avoid dangerous roles:** Don't allow principals to grant roles with `setIamPolicy` permissions or custom roles they can modify, as they could escalate privileges
- **CEL limitations:** Don't use `&&` or `||` operators joining multiple `hasOnly()` statements, as requests granting multiple roles may fail unexpectedly

content.text:
I checked. One of my two "confident" facts was wrong — surfacing that first.

## #4 — Verified, and I owe you a correction

**SA labels: confirmed.** Service accounts don't support resource labels. (They *do* support **tags** — key-value, listable, condition-usable — which is a cleaner home for the "RBK-managed" orphan marker than stuffing the `description`. Caveat: SA tags are currently **Preview**, so the description-sentinel stays as the stable fallback.)

**Scoping grants by role: I was wrong.** IAM *can* do this. There's a purpose-built condition attribute, `iam.googleapis.com/modifiedGrantsByRole`, that limits which roles a principal may grant or revoke:

```
api.getAttribute('iam.googleapis.com/modifiedGrantsByRole', []).hasOnly(['roles/foo','roles/bar'])
```

It's literally the "let this admin grant only these roles" feature — and the docs even warn about the exact escalation we feared (don't let a limited admin grant roles that themselves carry `setIamPolicy`; our director/retriever sets don't, so we're clear).

This **materially corrects the authority section.** The real picture:
- Horn (b) — structurally limit the governor to granting only director/retriever — is **feasible, not infeasible.**
- **But with real limits:** it works only on **project / folder / org** policies, *not* on resource-level policies (the AR repo, the build bucket, the Mason SA). A director's set spans all of those, so only its *project-scoped* part is IAM-limitable; the repo/bucket/SA grants are not. Max 10 roles per condition. And you'd have to stop granting the governor plain `roles/owner` (unconditional `setIamPolicy`) and give it a *conditioned* `setIamPolicy` instead.

So the corrected resolution is a **composition**, not "topology only":
- **Project-scoped grants** → IAM-limit them on the governor (preventive, server-side).
- **Resource-scoped grants** (repo/bucket/SA) → can't be IAM-limited → detective audit (drill 1) covers that residual.
- **Topology** (pull authority to the payor in keyfile) stays the cleanest lever where the governor buys nothing.

Still tier-shaped: keyfile/solo → topology; federation/multi-admin → a *condition-limited* governor is now a genuinely good answer (it's what `modifiedGrantsByRole` exists for). I'll carry this fix into the memo/paddock refactor.

Sources: [Set limits on granting roles](https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/setting-limits-on-granting-roles) · [Tags for service accounts](https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/service-accounts-tags) · [Service accounts overview](https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/service-account-overview)

## #2 — Blast radius, plainly (and it's not a separate choice)

The key thing I muddled: **the "union key" isn't a decision you make separately — it's the same decision as "one identity per person."**

Today, if Alice both builds images and pulls them, she has **two** service accounts, each with its **own** key file: a director key (can build) and a retriever key (can only pull).

The new model makes Alice **one** identity = **one** service account = **one** key, holding **both** powers.

"Blast radius" = how much a thief gets if that key leaks (committed to git, copied off a laptop):
- **Today:** if her *retriever* key leaks, the thief can only pull — can't poison builds. The director key is a separate file and didn't leak. One stolen key = one power.
- **New model:** her one key does everything she can do. One stolen key = *all* her powers.

So the single key is a juicier target. That's the cost — and here's why it's forced: in keyfile, an identity *is* a service account, and an SA's key always carries that account's **full** set of permissions. You can't make one key wield a subset. So "Alice is one identity with two roles" *requires* "Alice's one key carries both." The only escape is two separate accounts again — which is the old model, and throws away the "one identity per person, like federation" goal you want. The two are inseparable.

Why it's acceptable:
- It only exists in the **keyfile** tier (there's a file to steal). **Federation has no key file at all** — Alice logs in fresh each time — so the concern vanishes in the tier that cares most about security.
- Most people wear one hat, so for them one-per-person already *equals* one-per-role — no change at all.
- Recovery is actually *better*: revoke just her build power (she keeps her identity + pull), or rotate her key without touching her powers. The old model's only move was delete-and-recreate the whole account.

So: a bigger thing to steal, but only on the tier that already keeps a secret on disk, only for the rare multi-hat person, and with finer cleanup. Does that land — and are you OK accepting it, given it's the unavoidable flip side of the multi-role you want?

## #5 — Envoy (and yes, mason + envoy excluded)

**Envoy** is a *future, not-yet-built* robot account from the AWS-delivery roadmap. Plain version: a service account living in your GCP depot whose only job is to push artifacts *out to AWS* for you. It uses machine federation (Workload Identity Federation) so it never holds AWS passwords — it swaps a short-lived Google token for a short-lived AWS one at push time. It's a **machine, not a person**, and it doesn't exist yet.

So both **Mason** (today's build-runtime robot) and **Envoy** (tomorrow's AWS-push robot) are **excluded** from this convergence — machine identities, not people. They can reuse the *capability-set* idea (a named bundle of IAM grants), but they don't get the person-shaped lifecycle (add-person, rekey, device-flow, an RBRA on a workstation). Confirmed and cinched.

## #6 — Better silks

The heat outgrew "prepare-federation-idea" — it's now the converged identity/capability model itself, which federation later plugs into. Keeping the `rbk-14-` prefix:

- **`rbk-14-identity-capability-decoupling`** ← my pick — names the load-bearing spine you zeroed in on.
- `rbk-14-converged-identity-model` — names the outcome.
- `rbk-14-operator-identity-convergence` — emphasizes the operator-only scope (matches the mason/envoy exclusion).

One question that affects this: you called it **"this RBSA model"** earlier. If RBSA is a name you're minting for it (a sibling to RBRA?), say so and I'll thread it through both the silks and the vocabulary. Tell me which silks (or your own) and I'll `jjx_alter` it.

---

#1 (retire the cinch) and #3 (paddock → pure shape, mechanism → a pointed-to memo) are both noted. Once you've settled #2 and #6, I'll do the refactor — and fold the #4 correction into the authority section as I go.

aiTitle: Review and refine BZ idea for clean integration

message.content: Actually, lets first pick a better name for what I called 'current RBRA': I certainly have frequently reached for a term to be clear here and I've never reached resolve.  Recommend a few, name your favorite before we go into refactor mode.

content.text:
Before words, let me pin the *referent* — because your two reaches, "RBSA model" and "current RBRA," circle one gap: **a noun for an operator's identity in the depot that is neutral to rank and neutral to backing.** Today that noun is fractured — sometimes the role (`director`), sometimes the mechanism (`service account`), sometimes the credential (`RBRA`). The new model needs *one* word covering all four operators (payor, governor, director, retriever), whether backed by a keyfile SA or a federation principal, regardless of which capabilities they hold. That's the thing you keep reaching for. (If you actually meant a name for the whole *model*, or for the keyfile *credential bytes* specifically, redirect me — but I'm fairly sure it's the identity-noun.)

It has to read cleanly in every verb: "add a ___", "grant director to a ___", "roster the ___s", "rekey a ___", "the ___'s RBRA."

Three filters I held the candidates to:
- **Rank-neutral** — must fit the sovereign payor and the humble retriever equally. (This kills the feudal options — *retainer, vassal, liegeman* — they imply a station, and station is exactly what we moved *into* the capability-set.)
- **Backing-neutral** — true whether keyfile SA or federation principal.
- **Monosemic in your codebase** — no collision with a load-bearing word.

## Recommendations

1. **Citizen** — *my favorite.* Civic register, matching the depot's existing offices (payor and governor are civic, not feudal). Rank-neutral by construction: a sovereign and a laborer are both citizens — rank lives in what they're *enfranchised* to do, which maps the capability-set onto "the rights of citizenship" cleanly. It already drifts through your depot vocabulary loosely (RBSHR calls arks/bullions depot "citizens"); I'd formalize it the *other* way — **citizens are the identities; arks/hoards/bullions are holdings (property, not citizens)** — giving the depot a clean civic ontology: *citizens + holdings.* Reads everywhere: "add a citizen", "grant director to the citizen", "roster the citizens", "the citizen's RBRA", "rekey the citizen." And the model names itself for free: *the citizen model.*

2. **Principal** — maximal coherence with federation, because it's federation's *own* word (`principal://…`). Keyfile and federation would then share vocabulary literally — the whole coherence thesis made lexical. The cost: borrowed platform jargon, heavily overloaded, and against your usual grain (you mint `hallmark`, not "image"; `crucible`, not "container").

3. **Member** — coheres with the paddock's existing *membership* axis (grant/revoke = membership), and IAM itself calls binding subjects "members." Plain and apt, but overloaded — IAM "member" means *any* principal, not just operators.

4. **Patentee** — the heraldic deep cut: one holding *letters patent*, a grant of rank and rights. Maps the capability-set onto the patent itself; exact and rank-neutral. But archaic enough to tax every reader.

**Rejected, though it reads best of all — Bearer.** "The bearer's key," "grant to the bearer" is lovely, and possession-is-identity is exactly the keyfile semantics. But it collides head-on with "bearer token," already meaningful in your code (`rbw-rdi … bearer token via positional`). Monosemy says no.

## My vote

**Citizen.** It's civic like your offices, rank-neutral by design, already half-living in the depot, and it hands you both a model name (*the citizen model*) and a clean ontology (citizens hold capabilities; holdings are property). The code prefix can fall out in refactor mode.

Want me to run with citizen, or riff on it?

[Request interrupted by user]

message.content: Hold on! Are we replacing 'federation model' with 'citizen model'?  I rather thought you were proposing 'citizen' as the _current_ version, not the future one.

content.text:
Good stop — and you're right, that was my error. I let "citizen" balloon from "the current identity" into the name of the *whole cross-tier model*, which stepped right on "federation." That's not what you asked for. Let me lay out the corrected framing and confirm before I write anything.

## What went wrong

You asked me to name **the current version** ("current RBRA"). I instead made "citizen" a *backing-neutral umbrella* spanning keyfile **and** federation, then called the whole converged thing "the citizen model" — which reads as replacing "federation." Wrong scope. We are **not** replacing "federation."

## The corrected framing

There are two *tiers*, and they keep **distinct identity-kinds** — because the identity is the one thing that genuinely differs between them:

- **Citizen** = the **current / keyfile** operator identity. The depot issues the papers directly (the RBRA key); possession is identity; no external power needed. ← the no-org tier.
- **Federation** = the **future** tier. Identity comes from an external nation (your org's IdP); the depot recognizes it by *treaty*, not by issuing it. (That's literally what "federate" means — join by treaty.) Its identities are *federated principals*, not citizens.

The civic metaphor actually makes your instinct sing: a **citizen** is recognized natively by the depot; a **federate** is foreign-vouched. "Federation" was always the right word for the future tier, and "citizen" is its natural counterpart for the current one. The depot's civic ontology becomes: **citizens + federates** (people), and **holdings** (arks/hoards/bullions — property).

What this fixes: the thing that **converges and is tier-blind** is *not* the identity — it's the **capability layer** (capability-sets, grant/revoke, the declared roster, the audit). The identity *differs* by tier (citizen vs federate); that difference **is** the one real seam. So:

> The capability layer is identical whether the identities are **citizens** or **federates**; only the identity-kind and the token mint (signed-JWT vs STS) differ.

That's the same "structural isomorphism" payoff, but now honestly scoped — citizen names *one tier's* identity, federation names the other, and they share the capability machinery.

(Payor stays outside both — the founding human, neither citizen nor federate, per memo-20260527.)

## What this means for the work

- This heat builds the **citizen tier** (keyfile) with the decoupled capability layer, *shaped* so federation plugs in later by swapping citizen→federate and signed-JWT→STS. We do not touch federation in this heat.
- The relabel `rbk-14-citizen-model` still reads true under this scoping (it builds the citizen tier) — but tell me if you'd rather sharpen it (e.g. `rbk-14-citizen-tier` or `rbk-14-citizen-capability`).
- Minor open: do you want a peer noun for the federation-tier identity (**federate**?), or just "federated principal" when we get there? Not needed for this heat; flagging it so the metaphor stays consistent.

I've only done the relabel so far — no memo, no paddock yet. If this corrected framing is what you meant, I'll write the mechanics memo and the pure-shape paddock now with **citizen scoped to the keyfile tier** and the capability layer as the tier-blind shared part. Confirm and I'll go.

input.content:
# Citizen Tier — Identity / Capability Mechanics

Date: 2026-06-05

Status: Design captured during the 260605 grooming conversation for heat ₣BZ
(`rbk-14-citizen-model`). The *shape* lives in the ₣BZ paddock; this memo holds the
*mechanism* the paddock points to. Nothing here is built yet.

## Scope and vocabulary

This heat builds the **citizen tier** — the current, keyfile, no-org operator-credential
tier — restructured so its capability layer is identical to the future federation tier's, so
that federation plugs in later by swapping identity-kind and token mint and nothing else.

- **Citizen** — a keyfile-tier operator identity: a depot-issued service account holding one
  RBRA key. Rank-neutral (governor, director, retriever are all citizens); the home of the
  identity, not the role. Possession of the key is the identity.
- **Federate** (future, not this heat) — a federation-tier operator identity: an external-IdP
  `principal://` subject the depot recognizes by treaty. A federate holds no key.
- **Payor** — the founding human (OAuth refresh token), outside both tiers; neither citizen nor
  federate (memo-20260527).
- **Capability-set** — a named bundle of IAM grants, defined in code (the role's binding list,
  lifted out of today's invest bodies). Tier-blind.
- **Declared roster** — depot-resident data mapping identity → {capability-sets held}.
  Tier-blind.
- **Holdings** — the depot's artifacts (arks/hoards/bullions). Property, not identities. The
  depot's civic ontology is citizens/federates (people) + holdings (property).

The convergence: the **capability layer** (capability-sets, grant/revoke, the declared roster,
the audit) is tier-blind and shared. The **identity** differs by tier (citizen vs federate) —
that difference is the one real seam. "Structural isomorphism" means the capability layer is
byte-identical across tiers; only identity-kind and token mint (signed-JWT vs STS) differ.

## Verb dissolution

Today's role-keyed verbs decompose into a generic register parameterized by
(actor, capability-set, identity):

| Today | Decomposes to | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| invest (director/retriever) | add-citizen + grant(set) | governor is actor |
| mantle (governor) | add-citizen + grant(governor-set) | payor is actor; mantle is not special |
| divest | revoke(all held) + remove-citizen | |
| roster | read the declared roster | |
| — | grant(set) / revoke(set) | the capability axis, tier-blind |
| — | rekey | keyfile-only identity-backing maintenance |

Actor and capability-set are parameters, not verb variants. Federation reuses the capability
half (grant/revoke/roster) verbatim and swaps the identity half (add-federate, no rekey).

## Identity verbs branch; capability verbs do not

- **Capability verbs (grant/revoke)** never branch on tier — both are an IAM binding on a
  pre-existing identity.
- **Identity verbs** are tier-specific (this is the seam):
  - *citizen* (this heat): add = mint SA + key + deliver RBRA; rekey = rotate the key, bindings
    untouched; remove = revoke-all then delete the SA.
  - *federate* (future): add ≈ first grant (identity owned by the IdP, not minted here); no rekey
    (the IdP owns rotation); remove = de-register + revoke-all (the principal is not ours to
    delete).
  - *payor*: install / refresh a human OAuth refresh token.
- Token mint also branches (citizen: signed-JWT; federate: STS) and sits below everything.

## The declared roster

Why intent must be stored (the retired "derive from IAM" cinch was self-defeating once the role
prefix leaves the identity name):

1. Without a name prefix, deriving the roster from IAM is lossy reverse-mapping — a director set
   is several bindings smeared across project + Mason SA + repo + bucket; partial and ambiguous
   matches become representable.
2. You cannot audit IAM against itself — "intent = whatever IAM says" makes intended-vs-actual a
   tautology that always passes, discarding the drift signal.

Federation forces the same roster anyway (a federated principal carries no role marker;
memo-20260527: "the depot remains the home of the roster"), so the roster is paid once for both
tiers and makes routine roster identical and O(1) across tiers.

- **Code vs data.** Capability-set *definitions* are code; the roster is *data* (memberships).
  The audit compares: for each (identity, declared-set), are the code-defined bindings present in
  IAM?
- **Physical home (open).** A GCS object in the depot bucket — mutable, high-churn, etag-guarded
  read-modify-write (like IAM policies themselves) — is the natural fit, unlike tripwire-protected
  RBRD. A GAR artifact is an alternative. Decide in-heat.
- **Writers.** The governor writes director/retriever entries; the payor writes the governor
  entry — matching the authority hierarchy.

## The audit

Drift taxonomy (for one identity; D = union of declared sets' bindings, A = actual RBK-relevant
bindings):

| State | Meaning | Likely cause | Heal |
|---|---|---|---|
| D = A | clean | — | — |
| deficit-whole | a declared set wholly absent in A | grant never ran / errant revoke | auto-converge |
| deficit-fragment | a declared set partly present | half-failed multi-scope grant | auto-converge |
| surplus-complete | a whole undeclared named set in A | out-of-band grant | report |
| surplus-fragment | bindings belonging to no whole set | tampering / shrunk definition | report |

**Asymmetric healing = the Pale recognition test:**

- **Deficit is ours** (our own grant failed to complete) → auto-converge up to D (re-run the
  idempotent grant), logged, never silent.
- **Surplus crossed the Pale** (IAM touched outside our verbs) → characterize, report, let a human
  adjudicate (legitimize → declare it, or reject → revoke it). Never auto-revoke (a stale roster
  would deprovision a working identity); never auto-backfill intent (re-enters the tautology). No
  verb auto-revokes; the destructive operation is always human-driven.

Consequences:

- Because grant writes intent *first*, our tooling cannot produce a surplus — so a surplus is a
  clean "someone bypassed the verbs" signal.
- A capability-set definition change decomposes into deficit-on-additions (auto-heal via
  roster-wide re-converge) + surplus-on-removals (report for human-confirmed revoke) — no special
  versioning machinery.
- Audit domain = roster-identities × set-defined bindings; non-roster principals are out of scope.

## Orphan hazard and the identity marker (citizen tier only)

With no name prefix and no SA labels, nothing marks an SA as RBK-managed — so a failed add-citizen
can strand a live key for an untracked SA (a credential leak you cannot find). Marker options:

- **SA tags** (tag bindings): key-value, listable, condition-usable — the proper metadata
  mechanism. Caveat: SA tags are **Preview** (may change).
- **SA `description` sentinel** (`rbk:…`): stable fallback, used only for the orphan-sweep; the
  roster owns capability, the description earns exactly this one job.

Federation has no key to leak and a stray principal binding is visible in the policy, so this
hazard is citizen-tier-specific.

## Authority — who may grant which capability-set

- **Resource-scope-split principle.** An authority boundary is IAM-real only where it coincides
  with a resource boundary. Payor/governor holds because billing is a separate resource that
  governor's `roles/owner` excludes. Governor/director does not — both live in project IAM and
  governor has owner over it — so "governor may grant only director/retriever" is, by itself,
  honor-system.
- **IAM can scope grants by role** (corrects the first paddock draft). The condition attribute
  `iam.googleapis.com/modifiedGrantsByRole` limits which roles a setIamPolicy-capable principal
  may grant/revoke:
  `api.getAttribute('iam.googleapis.com/modifiedGrantsByRole', []).hasOnly(['roles/x','roles/y'])`.
  Limits: works only on **project/folder/org** allow policies, **not** resource-level (AR repo,
  bucket, individual SA); ≤10 roles per condition; don't join `hasOnly` with `&&`/`||`; some
  services don't recognize the limit (request fails); the governor must NOT hold plain
  `roles/owner` (unconditional setIamPolicy) — give it a conditioned setIamPolicy instead.
  Google's own caveat — don't let a limited admin grant roles bearing setIamPolicy — is satisfied
  because director/retriever sets contain none.
- **Composed resolution.**
  - Project-scoped grants → IAM-limit on the governor (preventive, server-side).
  - Resource-scoped grants (repo/bucket/SA) → not IAM-limitable → detective audit covers the
    residual.
  - Topology → pull grant authority to the payor where a standing governor buys nothing.
- **Tier-dependent.** Keyfile/solo (payor == admin) → topology: a standing owner-holding citizen
  buys nothing and is pure attack surface; drop or demote it. Federation/multi-admin (no
  exfiltrable key) → a condition-limited governor is the right preventive tool.
- **Audit ↔ topology seam.** Surplus-detection's security value depends on topology: a broad
  standing governor makes surplus-detection the only escalation tripwire (detective-only, weak vs
  malice); authority at the payor closes escalation structurally and demotes the audit to
  honest-mistake hygiene. Do not substitute detective audit for structural closure.
- **Ephemeral governor** (decoupling-enabled option): grant the governor-set for one op, then
  revoke. Shrinks resting owner-exposure; relevant only if a keyfile delegated governor is ever
  wanted.

## Blast radius — full reasoning

One citizen per person = one SA = one key carrying the **union** of granted capabilities. This is
**forced** by "one identity per person": an SA's key always wields the SA's full bindings; you
cannot scope one key to a subset. The only escape — separate SAs per role — is the old model,
which discards the citizen goal. So accepting multi-role on one citizen *is* accepting the union
key; they are one decision.

- Cost: larger resting target (a leaked key carries more).
- Bounded: only in the citizen tier (a file to steal); federation has no key. Most citizens wear
  one hat (one-per-person == one-per-role).
- Upside: finer incident response — revoke a capability without touching the citizen/key, or rekey
  without touching capabilities. The old model's only lever was delete-and-recreate.

## Governor teardown leak (folds in)

`rbgp_governor_mantle` deletes outgoing `governor-*` SAs without first revoking their grants,
minting a `deleted:…?uid=` tombstone per re-mantle. The convergence closes it for free: the
generic revoke-before-delete covers the governor like any citizen, and an idempotent governor
(rekey, not delete+recreate) drops the delete entirely. Reuse the rbk-08 revoke layer + Class-C
propagation tolerance. Standalone fix (if the heat descopes the governor):
memo-20260605-governor-mantle-tombstone-leak.md.

## Verified facts (checked 260605)

- **Service accounts do not support resource labels.** They support **tags** (tag bindings),
  currently in Preview. So the orphan marker uses tags (Preview) or the description sentinel, not a
  label.
- **IAM can limit which roles a principal may grant** via `modifiedGrantsByRole`, but only on
  project/folder/org policies and ≤10 roles — not on resource-level policies.

Sources:
- Set limits on granting roles — https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/setting-limits-on-granting-roles
- Tags for service accounts — https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/service-accounts-tags
- Service accounts overview — https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/service-account-overview

## memo-20260527 divergence (record when the model is frozen)

memo-20260527 states the operator verbs "keep their names, only their bodies differ," and that
keyfile holds "one SA key file each per role." This heat supersedes both: the verb *bodies*
converge in shape (not just names), and the citizen tier is one SA *per person*, not per role.
Update memo-20260527 to record this when the model is frozen.

content.content: File created successfully at: /home/bhyslop/projects/rbm_alpha_recipemuster/Memos/memo-20260605-citizen-capability-model.md (file state is current in your context — no need to Read it back)
