  with a resource boundary. Payor/governor holds because billing is a separate resource that
  governor's `roles/owner` excludes. Governor/director does not — both live in project IAM and
  governor has owner over it — so "governor may grant only director/retriever" is, by itself,
  honor-system.
- **IAM can scope grants by role** (corrects the first paddock draft). The condition attribute
  `iam.googleapis.com/modifiedGrantsByRole` limits which roles a setIamPolicy-capable principal
  may grant/revoke:
  `api.getAttribute('iam.googleapis.com/modifiedGrantsByRole', []).hasOnly(['roles/x','roles/y'])`.
  Limits: works only on **project/folder/org** allow policies, **not** resource-level (AR repo,
  bucket, individual SA); ≤10 roles per condition; don't join `hasOnly` with `&&`/`||`; some
  services don't recognize the limit (request fails); the governor must NOT hold plain
  `roles/owner` (unconditional setIamPolicy) — give it a conditioned setIamPolicy instead.
  Google's own caveat — don't let a limited admin grant roles bearing setIamPolicy — is satisfied
  because director/retriever sets contain none.
- **Composed resolution.**
  - Project-scoped grants → IAM-limit on the governor (preventive, server-side).
  - Resource-scoped grants (repo/bucket/SA) → not IAM-limitable → detective audit covers the
    residual.
  - Topology → pull grant authority to the payor where a standing governor buys nothing.
- **Tier-dependent.** Keyfile/solo (payor == admin) → topology: a standing owner-holding citizen
  buys nothing and is pure attack surface; drop or demote it. Federation/multi-admin (no
  exfiltrable key) → a condition-limited governor is the right preventive tool.
- **Audit ↔ topology seam.** Surplus-detection's security value depends on topology: a broad
  standing governor makes surplus-detection the only escalation tripwire (detective-only, weak vs
  malice); authority at the payor closes escalation structurally and demotes the audit to
  honest-mistake hygiene. Do not substitute detective audit for structural closure.
- **Ephemeral governor** (decoupling-enabled option): grant the governor-set for one op, then
  revoke. Shrinks resting owner-exposure; relevant only if a keyfile delegated governor is ever
  wanted.

## Blast radius — full reasoning

One citizen per person = one SA = one key carrying the **union** of granted capabilities. This is
**forced** by "one identity per person": an SA's key always wields the SA's full bindings; you
cannot scope one key to a subset. The only escape — separate SAs per role — is the old model,
which discards the citizen goal. So accepting multi-role on one citizen *is* accepting the union
key; they are one decision.

- Cost: larger resting target (a leaked key carries more).
- Bounded: only in the citizen tier (a file to steal); federation has no key. Most citizens wear
  one hat (one-per-person == one-per-role).
- Upside: finer incident response — revoke a capability without touching the citizen/key, or rekey
  without touching capabilities. The old model's only lever was delete-and-recreate.

## Governor teardown leak (folds in)

`rbgp_governor_mantle` deletes outgoing `governor-*` SAs without first revoking their grants,
minting a `deleted:…?uid=` tombstone per re-mantle. The convergence closes it for free: the
generic revoke-before-delete covers the governor like any citizen, and an idempotent governor
(rekey, not delete+recreate) drops the delete entirely. Reuse the rbk-08 revoke layer + Class-C
propagation tolerance. Standalone fix (if the heat descopes the governor):
memo-20260605-governor-mantle-tombstone-leak.md.

## Verified facts (checked 260605)

- **Service accounts do not support resource labels.** They support **tags** (tag bindings),
  currently in Preview. So the orphan marker uses tags (Preview) or the description sentinel, not a
  label.
- **IAM can limit which roles a principal may grant** via `modifiedGrantsByRole`, but only on
  project/folder/org policies and ≤10 roles — not on resource-level policies.

Sources:
- Set limits on granting roles — https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/setting-limits-on-granting-roles
- Tags for service accounts — https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/service-accounts-tags
- Service accounts overview — https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/service-account-overview

## memo-20260527 divergence (record when the model is frozen)

memo-20260527 states the operator verbs "keep their names, only their bodies differ," and that
keyfile holds "one SA key file each per role." This heat supersedes both: the verb *bodies*
converge in shape (not just names), and the citizen tier is one SA *per person*, not per role.
Update memo-20260527 to record this when the model is frozen.

input.content:
# jjezs_paddock ₣BZ

## Boundary — shape, not mechanism (cinched)

This heat builds the **citizen tier** — the current keyfile, no-org operator-credential tier —
restructured so its capability layer is identical to the future federation tier's. The backing
stays keyfile; a no-org operator runs it unchanged. We do **not** touch federation here.
Explicitly OUT of scope: Workforce pool/provider setup, STS exchange, IdP device flow, and the
RBRD mode-enum activation — federation's own future work (RBSHR "Operator federation";
memo-20260527).

The payoff is structural, not nominal: the **capability layer** (capability-sets, grant/revoke,
the declared roster, the audit) becomes tier-blind, so a later federation switch reshapes nothing
in it — it swaps the identity-kind (citizen → federate) and the token mint (signed-JWT → STS) and
nothing else. That is what makes the switch non-disruptive.

Mechanism for everything below lives in `Memos/memo-20260605-citizen-capability-model.md`; this
paddock holds shape only.

NOTE — supersedes memo-20260527 on one point: it says operator verbs "keep their names, only
bodies differ" and keyfile is "one SA key file each per role." This heat converges the verb
*bodies* in shape and makes the citizen tier one SA *per person*. Update that memo when the model
is frozen.

## The citizen model — identity decoupled from capability

- **Citizen** = a keyfile-tier operator identity (governor/director/retriever), a depot-issued SA
  holding one RBRA key. Rank-neutral: rank is not a property of the citizen, it lives in the
  capability-sets the citizen holds. Multi-role is first-class — one citizen holds several
  capability-sets. (The federation tier's identity is a *federate*; the payor is the founder,
  outside both.)
- **Identity lifecycle is decoupled from capability lifecycle** — the spine. Adding/removing a
  citizen is independent of granting/revoking its capabilities; this is native to the GCP
  primitive (an SA may exist with zero bindings). Today's `invest` fuses the two; the model
  unfuses them.
- **Capability-set** = a named bundle of IAM grants, defined in code (lifted out of the invest
  bodies). Tier-blind.
- **The verbs dissolve** to add-citizen / remove-citizen / grant / revoke / rekey + read-roster,
  parameterized by (actor, capability-set, citizen). invest/divest/mantle/roster stop existing as
  distinct concepts.
- **Capability verbs are tier-blind; only identity verbs branch by tier** (and by the payor's
  OAuth backing). rekey is keyfile-only.
- **Grant/revoke operate only on named capability-sets, never raw bindings** — the contamination
  guard that keeps the roster auditable.
- Ontology: a depot has **citizens** (and later federates) — people who hold capabilities — and
  **holdings** (arks/hoards/bullions) — property.

## Intent vs enforcement — the declared roster (cinched)

Enforcement is server-side IAM, always. Intent — which citizen is meant to hold which capability —
is stored in a depot-resident **declared roster**, distinct from IAM. The audit diffs declared
intent against IAM; routine roster reads intent and is identical across tiers. Federation forces
the same roster anyway, so it is paid once for both. (Why it must be stored, and where it lives:
memo.)

## The audit — drift as signal, asymmetric healing (cinched)

The audit measures drift between declared intent and IAM. Healing is asymmetric, and the asymmetry
is the Pale recognition test:

- **Deficit** (IAM short of intent — our own grant failed) is ours → auto-converge, logged.
- **Surplus** (IAM beyond intent — touched outside our verbs) crossed the Pale → report; a human
  adjudicates. No verb auto-revokes; the destructive operation is always human-driven.

(Drift taxonomy, the intent-first bypass signal, definition-change versioning, and the orphan
marker: memo.)

## Authority topology — the one seam with policy content

"Who may grant which capability-set." Shape:

- Authority boundaries are IAM-real only where they fall on resource-scope splits (payor/governor
  holds because billing is a separate resource; governor/director does not).
- The governor's grant power **is** scopable by role at project scope (IAM `modifiedGrantsByRole`)
  but **not** at resource scope (repo/bucket/SA). So the resolution composes: IAM-limit the
  project-scoped grants, detective-audit the resource-scoped residual, and pull authority to the
  payor where a standing governor buys nothing.
- **Tier-dependent**: keyfile/solo → topology (no/minimal standing governor); federation/multi-admin
  → a condition-limited governor. The model allows the answer to differ (actor and capability-set
  are parameters).
- Surplus-detection's security value depends on the topology — do not substitute detective audit for
  structural closure. (modifiedGrantsByRole limits + the seam reasoning: memo.)

## Blast radius — larger resting target, finer response (cinched)

One citizen = one SA = one key carrying the union of held capabilities — forced by "one identity per
person," accepted as a tradeoff. Bounded to the citizen tier (federation has no key); most citizens
wear one hat; and decoupling buys finer incident response (revoke a capability without touching the
key; rekey without touching capabilities). (Reasoning: memo.)

## Inherited concern — governor teardown leak

`rbgp_governor_mantle` deletes outgoing `governor-*` SAs without revoking first, minting a
`deleted:…?uid=` tombstone per re-mantle. The convergence closes it for free (generic
revoke-before-delete + idempotent rekey-not-delete), reusing the rbk-08 revoke layer + Class-C
tolerance. Standalone fix if the governor is descoped:
memo-20260605-governor-mantle-tombstone-leak.md.

## What done looks like

Every cult-verb surface migrated to the citizen/capability vocabulary: workstation bash (role-keyed
selection collapses to one identity accessor), the governor/payor admin verbs (invest/divest/roster/
mantle → add/grant/revoke/remove + read, parameterized by capability-set), cloud-side bash wherever
it names roles, the .adoc specs that voice the cult verbs, the Rust theurge cases that drive them,
and the onboarding handbook tracks that teach them. "citizen" replaces the role-named identity
throughout.

The load-bearing federation-enabling core within that surface is narrow and shippable with no
behavior change: route the role/file-path-keyed token-accessor calls through one identity-keyed
accessor (memo-20260527 "single code seam"). The vocabulary convergence is the larger,
motivated-but-not-required remainder. Scoping/heat-chopping is deferred — recorded here as shape.

## Cinched decisions

- Enforcement is server-side IAM. No file or client-side check is ever the boundary.
- The identity is the **citizen** — rank-neutral, keyfile-backed (the federation tier's peer is a
  *federate*; the payor is the founder, outside both). Identity lifecycle is decoupled from
  capability lifecycle. Multi-role is first-class.
- The citizen tier is one SA (one key, union of capabilities) per person, not one per role —
  blast-radius tradeoff accepted.
- Capability-sets are named code; grant/revoke operate only on named sets, never raw bindings.
- The declared roster (intent) is part of the model; IAM is enforcement; the audit diffs them.
- Audit healing is asymmetric: auto-converge deficits, report surplus; no verb auto-revokes.
- Governor breadth is resolved per tier, not globally: keyfile pulls grant authority to the payor /
  minimizes the standing governor; federation may keep a condition-limited governor.
- The mode enum (keyfile vs federation) belongs in RBRD and is added only when federation lands —
  not in this heat.
- Migration grandfathers existing role-named SAs: read grants from IAM, register citizens in the
  declared roster, let legacy `director-…`/`retriever-…` names age out. No flag day.
- The convergence covers operator citizens; cloud-native identities (Mason, future Envoy) share the
  capability-set abstraction but not the citizen lifecycle.

## Retired cinches

- "Roster derives from IAM (an `rbk-identity` label), not a stored list — it cannot drift." Retired:
  under decoupling, deriving from IAM is lossy and makes the intended-vs-actual audit a tautology,
  and SA labels do not exist (SAs support tags, in Preview). Replaced by the declared roster +
  asymmetric audit. The true invariant it reached for — no enforcement state outside IAM — is
  preserved; the false corollary (no intent state may exist) is dropped.

## Open — resolve within the heat

- Where the declared roster physically lives (GCS object in the depot bucket vs GAR artifact) and
  its writer/etag discipline.
- Whether the keyfile tier drops the standing governor entirely or demotes it to non-privileged.
- The citizen orphan marker: SA tags (Preview) vs description sentinel.
- Operator add-citizen UX: add yields a powerless credential, a separate grant makes it useful —
  confirm the two-gesture flow is intended.

## Sources

The per-person design exploration in rbk-08-credential-repairs that birthed this heat;
memo-20260604-credential-churn-leak-and-propagation-races (the lifecycle split — the enabler);
memo-20260527-operator-credential-models (the two-tier plan and cult-verb shape — to be updated per
the divergence above); memo-20260605-governor-mantle-tombstone-leak (standalone governor-leak fix);
memo-20260605-citizen-capability-model (the mechanism this paddock points to); RBSHR "Operator
federation". This revision folds in the 260605 design conversation.

content.text:
Done. All three changes are committed and the tree is clean:

- `b69f46492` — relabel → `rbk-14-citizen-model`
- `1c0228d91` — paddock refactored to **pure shape**, citizen scoped to the keyfile tier (federate = the federation-tier peer), capability layer tier-blind, pointing at the memo
- `007497852` — **notch** of the mechanics memo `Memos/memo-20260605-citizen-capability-model.md`

The paddock now carries shape only; every mechanism (drift taxonomy, asymmetric-heal mechanics, `modifiedGrantsByRole` limits with sources, orphan marker, blast-radius reasoning, verb-dissolution table) lives in the memo it points to.

## Review-chat prompt

Here's a self-contained prompt for a fresh chat in this repo. It reviews the two artifacts, cross-checks them against the source docs, and emits a paste-back report. Copy from the block:

````text
You are critically reviewing a design heat's captured artifacts in this repo
(recipe-bottle / RBK). Do NOT implement or edit anything — read, cross-check, and
produce a written feedback report I will paste into the originating chat.

## What to review (two artifacts, both written 2026-06-05)

1. The paddock for heat ₣BZ (silks `rbk-14-citizen-model`). To read it:
   - Call the JJK MCP tool `jjx_open` ALONE first to get an officium id (☉…).
   - Then call `jjx_paddock` with params `{"firemark": "BZ"}` and immediately read
     `.claude/jjm/officia/<id>/gazette_out.md` (id = the officium with ☉ stripped).
     That file is deleted by the next jjx call, so read it right away.
2. The mechanics memo it points to:
   `Memos/memo-20260605-citizen-capability-model.md`.

## Context (verify, don't take on faith)

"Citizen" is a freshly-minted term for the KEYFILE-TIER operator identity (a
depot-issued service account holding one RBRA key). The future federation tier's
identity is a "federate." The paddock holds *shape*; the memo holds *mechanism*.
The model decouples identity lifecycle from capability lifecycle, stores intent in a
"declared roster," and audits declared-vs-IAM.

## Cross-check against these source docs

- `Memos/memo-20260527-operator-credential-models.md` — the committed two-tier plan.
  The new model claims to SUPERSEDE it on two points (verb *bodies* converge, not just
  names; keyfile is one SA per person, not per role). Verify that divergence is stated
  correctly AND that nothing else in the new model silently contradicts this memo.
- `Memos/memo-20260604-credential-churn-leak-and-propagation-races.md` — the lifecycle
  split the model builds on.
- `Memos/memo-20260605-governor-mantle-tombstone-leak.md` — the standalone governor-leak
  fix the model says it subsumes.
- `Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/RBSHR-HorizonRoadmap.adoc` — the "Operator federation" entry.
- Current cult-verb specs the model proposes to dissolve (all under
  `Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/`): RBSDK (director_invest), RBSDD (director_divest),
  RBSRK (retriever_invest), RBSDR (director_roster), RBSGM (governor_mantle),
  RBSRA (CredentialFormat). Check the model's description of CURRENT behavior matches.

## Review for

1. CONFLICT — contradictions within or between the paddock and memo; conflicts with the
   source docs (especially memo-20260527); claims about current behavior that don't match
   the cult-verb specs.
2. ERROR — factual errors, especially the GCP claims: SA resource labels absent; SA tags
   in Preview; `modifiedGrantsByRole` limits which roles a setIamPolicy-capable principal
   may grant, but only on project/folder/org policies (not resource-level) and ≤10 roles.
   (Optional: independently re-verify these via web search.) Also logical errors — the
   audit deficit/surplus asymmetry, the "union key is forced" blast-radius claim, the
   authority resolution.
3. CLARITY — is the citizen / federate / payor scoping crisp and consistent throughout?
   Any section unclear standing alone?
4. CONCISE — redundancy between paddock and memo (paddock should be pure shape, memo pure
   mechanism — flag overlap/duplication); bloat; ROE "load-bearing complexity" (anything
   that doesn't earn its place).
5. POSTURE — anything filed "cinched" that's actually unresolved, or "open" that's actually
   decided; scope creep beyond "build the citizen tier, shaped for federation."

## Output

A numbered feedback report I can paste back. Tag each finding [CONFLICT] / [ERROR] /
[CLARITY] / [CONCISE] / [POSTURE], with location (doc + section), the issue in 1–2
sentences, and a concrete suggested fix. End with a one-paragraph overall assessment.
Be a genuine critic — rubber-stamping helps no one; flag your own uncertainty; and it's
fine to say a section is solid.
````

Verified from my side — the memo is real and committed, not a phantom:

- `Memos/memo-20260605-citizen-capability-model.md` exists, 12 KB / 206 lines
- It's in HEAD as commit `007497852` (`1 file changed, 206 insertions`)
- `ls Memos/ | grep -i citizen` finds it

So your local working tree has it. The review chat is looking at a **different or stale view** — and there's a clean explanation:

**Your 7 commits are local only — not pushed** (`git status`: "ahead of origin/main by 7 commits"). Every change from this chat — the citizen memo, the relabeled silks, *and* both paddock refactors — lives only in this working copy. So any review session that isn't *this exact directory* (a cloud/web Claude session, or a separate clone synced from origin) will be missing all of it. That fully explains "the citizen memo doesn't exist at that path."

There's a second, sharper consequence: the review chat got far enough to read a gazette — if it's a separate/remote environment, the paddock it read is the **stale pre-refactor** one, so any feedback it produces would be reviewing the wrong artifact entirely. We need it on the current tree before its critique means anything.

One diagnostic question decides the fix — **how is the review chat running?**

- **(a) Another Claude Code in this same folder** → the file is right there; just resume/retry it (have it run `ls Memos/ | grep -i citizen` to confirm). It likely raced the file creation or fumbled a path.
- **(b) A cloud/web session, or a separate clone** → it's reading origin, which lacks all 7 commits. The fix is to **push first**, then re-run it so it pulls a current tree. I haven't pushed (I only push when you ask) — say the word and I'll `git push` origin/main, then you re-launch the review.

Which is it? If (b), just tell me to push and I'll do it.

40	## Working Preferences
41	- When user mentions an acronym, immediately navigate to the corresponding file
42	- Assume full edit permissions for all files in the three main directories
43	- For bash scripts, prefer functional programming style with clear error handling
44	- For .adoc files, maintain consistent AsciiDoc formatting
45	- For .claudex files, preserve the specific format requirements
46	
47	### Heredoc Delimiter Selection
48	
49	When generating heredocs for stdin content, the delimiter must not appear alone on any line within the content.
50	
51	- **Check content first**: If content includes `EOF` (e.g., code examples showing heredoc patterns), use a different delimiter
52	- **Safe alternatives**: `SPEC`, `CONTENT`, `DOC`, `PACESPEC`, `SLASHCMD`
53	- **Pattern**: `cat <<'DELIM' | command` (quoted delimiter prevents variable expansion)
54	
55	### AsciiDoc Linked Terms
56	When working with .adoc files using MCM patterns:
57	- **Linked Term**: Concept with three parts:
58	  - Attribute reference: `:prefix_snake_case:` (mapping section)
59	  - Replacement text: `<<anchor,Display Text>>` (what readers see)
60	  - Definition: `[[anchor]] {attribute}:: Definition text` (meaning)
61	- Definitions may be grouped in lists or dispersed through document
62	- Maintain consistent prefix categories (e.g., `mcm_`, `rbw_`, `gad_`)
63	- Use snake_case for anchors, match attribute to anchor
64	
65	### Rust Build Discipline
66	
67	Two Rust build targets. Always use the tabtarget, never raw cargo commands.
68	
69	**VOW pipeline** (vvk/jjk/cmk kits — parceled for delivery):
70	- `tt/vow-b.Build.sh` — build vvr binary and install to VVK bin
71	- `tt/vow-t.Test.sh` — run all kit crate tests
72	- `tt/vvw-r.RunVVX.sh <cmd>` — run vvx binary with arguments
73	
74	**Theurge** (rbk's own test infrastructure — dispatches through the unified rbw workbench):
75	- `tt/rbw-tb.Build.sh` — build theurge crate
76	- `tt/rbw-tt.Test.sh` — run theurge unit tests
77	
78	### Test Execution
79	
80	**Test suites** group fixtures by dependency tier. Run the broadest applicable suite:
81	
82	| Suite | Tabtarget | Dependencies | What it covers |
83	|-------|-----------|-------------|----------------|
84	| `fast` | `tt/rbw-ts.TestSuite.fast.sh` | None | enrollment-validation (47), regime-validation (21), regime-smoke (7) = 75 cases |
85	| `service` | `tt/rbw-ts.TestSuite.service.sh` | GCP credentials | fast + access-probe (4), hallmark-lifecycle (1), batch-vouch (1) = 81 cases |
86	| `crucible` | `tt/rbw-ts.TestSuite.crucible.sh` | Container runtime | fast + tadmor-security (34), srjcl-jupyter (3), pluml-diagram (5) = 117 cases |
87	| `complete` | `tt/rbw-ts.TestSuite.complete.sh` | All of the above | All 8 fixtures = 122 cases |
88	
89	**After code changes**, run the appropriate tier:
90	- Regime/validation changes → `fast`
91	- Foundry/credential changes → `service`
92	- Bottle/sentry/network changes → `crucible`
93	- Pre-release or decomposition sweep → `complete`
94	
95	**Single fixture**: `tt/rbw-tf.FixtureRun.sh <name>` (e.g., `tadmor`, `enrollment-validation`, `regime-smoke`)
96	
97	**Single case**: `tt/rbw-tc.FixtureCase.sh <fixture> [case-name]` — run one case against an already-charged crucible (no charge/quench). Omit case name to list all cases for the fixture; omit fixture to list all fixtures. Workflow for crucible debugging: charge via `tt/rbw-cC.Charge.{nameplate}.sh`, run individual cases, quench via `tt/rbw-cQ.Quench.{nameplate}.sh` when done.
98	
99	**BUK self-test**: `tt/buw-st.BukSelfTest.sh` — exercises BUK test framework (kick-tires + bure-tweak, 9 cases)
100	
101	**Sequential only**: Never run fixtures in parallel — they share regime state and container namespaces.
102	
103	## Prefix Naming Discipline ("mint")
104	
105	When asked to "mint" names, apply these rules. Full study: `Memos/memo-20260110-acronym-selection-study.md`
106	
107	### Two Universes
108	
109	**Primary Universe** — ANY persistent identifier, regardless of where it lives. This includes the obvious (code, docs, functions, variables, directories) AND the easy-to-miss: git refs, slash commands, environment variables, paths in target repos, configuration keys. **If it's a name that persists, it's in scope.** Prefixes must be globally unique and respect terminal exclusivity.
110	
111	**Tabtarget Universe** — launchers in `tt/`. These are *colophons* referencing the primary universe. `rbw-` points to the `rbw` workbench; it doesn't consume new prefix space.
112	
113	### Core Rules
114	
115	**Rule 1 - Project Prefix**: Names start with 2-4 char project ID:
116	`rb` (Recipe Bottle), `gad` (GAD), `bu` (BUK), `jj` (Job Jockey), `pb` (Paneboard), `mcm`/`axl` (CMK), `crg`, `wrs`, `hm` (HMK), `lmci`, `vsl` (VSLK)
117	
118	**Rule 2 - Terminal Exclusivity**: A prefix either IS a name or HAS children, never both.
119	- `rbg` has children (`rbga`, `rbgb`...) → `rbg` cannot name a thing
120	- `rbi` names Image module → `rbia`, `rbib` forbidden
121	
122	### Primary Universe Patterns
123	
124	| Domain | Pattern | Example |
125	|--------|---------|---------|
126	| Code files | `prefix_Word.ext` | `rbga_ArtifactRegistry.sh` |
127	| Doc files | `ACRONYM-Words.ext` | `RBS0-SpecTop.adoc` |
128	| Functions (public) | `prefix_name()` | `buc_log_args()` |
129	| Functions (private) | `zprefix_name()` | `zbuc_color()` |
130	| Variables | `PREFIX_NAME` | `BURC_PROJECT_ROOT` |
131	| AsciiDoc attributes | `:prefix_term:` | `:rbw_depot:` |
132	| AsciiDoc anchors | `[[prefix_term]]` | `[[rbw_depot]]` |
133	| Directories | `prefix/` | `Tools/buk/` |
134	
135	### Tabtarget Universe Pattern
136	
137	Tabtargets follow: `{colophon}.{frontispiece}[.{imprint}].sh`
138	
139	Colophons must reference valid Primary Universe prefixes. See **BUK Concepts** in the BUK include for terminology (colophon, frontispiece, imprint, workbench).
140	
141	Tabtargets dispatch through `tt/z-launcher.sh`, naming their launcher in the `BURD_LAUNCHER` config line (a bare `launcher.<id>_workbench.sh` basename) and exec'ing the trampoline with a byte-identical, token-free exec line. See BCG "Tabtarget Path Indirection" for the rationale.
142	
143	### Extended Namespace Checklist
144	
145	When minting, enumerate ALL namespaces the system touches:
146	
147	| Namespace | Pattern | Example |
148	|-----------|---------|---------|
149	| Git refs | `refs/{prefix}/...` | `refs/vvg/locks/*` |

content.text:
Done — conflict resolved, rebase finished, pushed, and local is back in sync with `origin/main`.

What happened:
- Both conflicts were the same kludge-churn on `rbmm_moorings/tadmor/rbrn.env` (two machines writing hallmark digests). Resolved latest-of-each; they converged cleanly to the **16:41 builds** for both artifacts — sentry `k260605164118-34cf36951`, bottle `k260605164118-1b3549cef`.
- The rebase replayed all 8 commits; your ₣BZ paddock/silks/memo commits applied with **no gallops conflict** (I was watching for one — I would've stopped rather than hand-edit `jjg_gallops.json`).
- `git push` fast-forwarded `2aa576c98..5b8b7b58c`. Tree clean, in sync.

The review-chat prompt from my previous message still stands whenever you want to run it — paste its report back here and I'll weigh each finding.

message.content: <local-command-caveat>Caveat: The messages below were generated by the user while running local commands. DO NOT respond to these messages or otherwise consider them in your response unless the user explicitly asks you to.</local-command-caveat>
<bash-input>git pull --rebase  &&  git push</bash-input>

<bash-stdout>Already up to date.
Everything up-to-date</bash-stdout><bash-stderr></bash-stderr>

Assess the merits of this independent review and make any amendments you strongly agree with.  Make a list of the remaining and lets talk about each after.


● I've now cross-checked everything: both review artifacts, the three source memos, all six cult-verb specs, and the
  roadmap entry — plus I web-verified the two time-sensitive GCP facts. Both check out as current (SA tags still Preview
  as of June 2026; modifiedGrantsByRole confirmed project/folder/org-only with the no-setIamPolicy-roles caveat). Here is
  the report.

  ---
  Feedback report — heat ₣BZ paddock + citizen-capability memo (2026-06-05)

  What's solid (so the critique is calibrated)

  - GCP facts are accurate and current. I independently verified the two load-bearing claims. Service accounts support
  tags, not labels, and SA tags remain in Preview as of June 2026 — the orphan-marker caveat is correctly hedged.
  modifiedGrantsByRole is confirmed project/folder/org-only, not resource-level, with Google's own "don't allow-list
  setIamPolicy-bearing roles" caveat. The memo's "Verified facts" section holds.
  - The memo-20260527 two-point divergence is correctly stated. Both quoted claims ("keep their names, only their bodies
  differ"; "one SA key file each per role") are faithful paraphrases of 20260527 §"Operator-admin verbs" and the tier
  table, and both are genuinely superseded.
  - Current-behavior descriptions match the specs. The governor-mantle leak (delete-without-revoke → tombstone) matches
  RBSGM §"Find and Delete Existing Governor Service Accounts" and the standalone memo. The model correctly builds on
  landed rbk-08 work (RBSDD already does revoke-before-delete; RBSDK/RBSRK are already idempotent-establish + rekey).
  - The audit's paddock/memo split is a clean instance of the intended division — paddock states the asymmetry + Pale
  test, memo holds the drift taxonomy. That is exactly the shape/mechanism boundary working.

  Findings

  1. [ERROR] Auto-converge-deficit safety silently depends on roster integrity, which is filed open. (memo §"The audit"
  lines 101–108; paddock §"The audit" + Open list) The asymmetry argument guards the surplus side ("a stale roster would
  deprovision a working identity") but never guards the symmetric deficit risk: auto-granting "up to D" treats the roster
  as authoritative intent, so anyone who can write the roster upward gets an auto-grant — a softer escalation path than
  setIamPolicy. Yet the roster's physical home and writer ACL are explicitly open (memo §"Physical home (open)"; paddock
  Open). A cinched behavior (auto-heal deficits) rests on an unresolved tamper-resistance decision. Fix: add a sentence
  making roster-write authority ≡ grant authority an invariant of the asymmetry ("auto-converge is safe only because
  roster-write is at least as protected as setIamPolicy"), and flag in the Open item that the roster home must satisfy
  that ACL constraint.

  2. [ERROR] "Director/retriever sets contain no setIamPolicy-bearing role" is wrong as written. (memo §"Authority" line
  148) roles/artifactregistry.repoAdmin — granted to the director in RBSDK §"Grant Repository Admin Access" — includes
  artifactregistry.repositories.setIamPolicy at repo scope. The claim is true only for the project-scoped portion of the
  sets. This doesn't break the argument — it's precisely the resource-scope residual the detective audit is meant to
  cover — but the blanket phrasing is a factual error. Fix: scope it: "the project-scoped director/retriever grants bear
  no setIamPolicy, satisfying Google's caveat at the level modifiedGrantsByRole applies; the repo-scoped repoAdmin does
  bear setIamPolicy, which is exactly why it falls to the detective half."

  3. [CONFLICT] The "civic ontology" (citizens/federates + holdings) omits cloud-native identities that the model itself
  says exist. (memo §"Scope and vocabulary" line 27; paddock §"The citizen model" line 46 vs Cinched line 131) The
  two-part ontology — "people (citizens/federates) + property (holdings)" — has no slot for Mason/Envoy, yet the
  migration cinch says cloud-native SAs "share the capability-set abstraction but not the citizen lifecycle." So an
  identity that holds capability-sets but is neither citizen, federate, nor holding is asserted to exist while the
  ontology denies it a place. Fix: either admit a third ontological category (cloud-native/system identities that hold
  capability-sets without the citizen lifecycle) or explicitly state that the civic ontology covers operator identities
  only and cloud-native SAs sit outside it.

  4. [CONFLICT] An undocumented third divergence from memo-20260527: the seam flips from role-keyed to identity-keyed.
  (paddock §"What done looks like" line 108–110; memo §"memo-20260527 divergence") The paddock invokes "memo-20260527
  'single code seam'" as the load-bearing core — but that memo's seam is explicitly role-keyed ("give me a token as
  director," 20260527 §"The single code seam"). Under one-SA-per-person, there is no "token as director" vs "as
  retriever" — one citizen key yields one token carrying the union, so the accessor is identity-keyed. The divergence
  section records only two points and misses this one, even though the paddock leans on that seam by name. Fix: add a
  third bullet to the divergence note: the single seam becomes identity-keyed (a consequence of one-SA-per-person),
  generalizing — not contradicting — 20260527's role-keyed accessor.

  5. [ERROR] "Surplus is a clean 'someone bypassed the verbs' signal" is internally contradicted. (memo §"The audit"
  lines 110–116) Line 110–111 claims our tooling cannot produce a surplus, so surplus ⟹ bypass. But lines 113–116
  immediately say a capability-set definition shrink produces surplus-on-removals — and that surplus came from our own
  tooling's earlier grant under the old definition, not from a bypass. The operational routing (report, don't
  auto-revoke) is correct for both cases; only the characterization overreaches. Fix: qualify — "absent a definition
  change, our tooling cannot produce a surplus; a definition shrink is the one tooling-side surplus source, which is why
  surplus is reported rather than auto-revoked regardless of origin."

  6. [POSTURE] "What done looks like" bundles a load-bearing core with a not-required rename and calls the whole thing
  "done." (paddock §"What done looks like" lines 99–111) It enumerates a codebase-wide vocabulary migration (workstation
  bash, admin verbs, cloud bash, .adoc specs, Rust theurge, handbook) — "citizen replaces the role-named identity
  throughout" — then concedes the rename is "motivated-but-not-required." Per the ROE load-bearing test, an optional
  rename sweep shouldn't sit inside the definition of done. Fix: make the narrow federation-enabling core (the
  identity-keyed token accessor + roster + audit) the "done," and move the throughout-the-codebase rename to an
  explicitly-optional/deferred remainder.

  7. [CONCISE] The governor-teardown-leak section is near-verbatim duplicated, mechanism and all. (paddock §"Inherited
  concern" lines 91–97 vs memo §"Governor teardown leak" lines 180–186) Both carry the same mechanism
  (revoke-before-delete, idempotent rekey, rbk-08 revoke layer, Class-C tolerance, standalone-fix cite). The paddock
  should hold only the shape — "governor teardown sits inside this heat's blast radius; standalone fallback exists if
  descoped" — and defer the mechanism to the memo. Fix: strip the paddock version to one shape sentence + the memo
  pointer.

  8. [CONCISE] The paddock's authority section leaks mechanism, including the literal IAM attribute name. (paddock
  §"Authority topology" line 74) Naming modifiedGrantsByRole in the paddock is mechanism in a shape document; the whole
  section largely restates the memo's authority reasoning. Fix: compress to the shape — "grant authority is preventively
  scopable at project scope but only detectively at resource scope; resolved per tier" — and let the memo own the
  attribute name and the project/resource/≤10 detail.

  9. [CLARITY] "Bounded to the citizen tier" conflates the resting key with the union-of-capability property. (memo
  §"Blast radius" line 175; paddock §"Blast radius" line 87) The resting key file is genuinely keyfile-only — but the
  union of capabilities on a single identity is tier-blind: a federate granted director+retriever sets also wields both,
  exposed via a stolen live session rather than a stolen file. The core claim (resting attack surface is
  keyfile-specific) is sound; the phrasing implies the whole blast property is keyfile-only. Fix: "the resting-key form
  of this exposure is citizen-only; the union-on-one-identity property itself is tier-blind (federation exposes it
  through a live session, not a file at rest)."

  10. [POSTURE] The keyfile tier's governor topology — this heat's own deliverable — is open, and that openness
  propagates into "done." (paddock Cinched line 124 "resolved per tier" vs Open line 144) "Governor breadth is resolved
  per tier" reads as settled, but the keyfile resolution (drop vs demote the standing governor) is open — and that choice
  determines whether any preventive modifiedGrantsByRole work is even in-heat. Leaving it open is defensible, but
  "resolved per tier" overstates settledness for the one tier being built. Fix: note that the keyfile governor decision
  gates part of the heat's scope, so "done" is conditional on resolving it.

  11. [CLARITY] "Closes it for free" undersells the idempotent-governor change. (paddock line 94; memo line 182) Making
  the governor idempotent means dropping the datestamped governor-YYYYMMDDHHmm naming (which exists precisely to dodge
  the delete→recreate flap, per RBSGM and memo-20260605-governor) for a stable governor name, plus migrating existing
  datestamped governors. It reuses rbk-08 machinery but isn't free. Fix: "closes it by reusing the rbk-08 revoke layer;
  idempotency additionally requires a stable governor name and a one-time migration off datestamped names."

  12. [CLARITY] "Byte-identical across tiers" slightly overclaims. (memo §"Scope and vocabulary" lines 30–32) The
  capability verbs receive a tier-dependent principal handle (serviceAccount:… vs principal://…). This is defensible if
  you treat the principal string as identity-layer output that the capability layer consumes opaquely — but
  "byte-identical" invites the objection. (Low confidence — flagging my own uncertainty; you may judge this fine as-is.)
  Fix (optional): "identical modulo the opaque principal handle the identity layer supplies."

  13. [CLARITY] The "two-gesture flow" Open item is nearly cinched-by-implication. (paddock Open line 147–148) "Confirm
  the two-gesture (add-then-grant) flow is intended" — but two gestures follow necessarily from the cinched
  identity/capability decoupling, so there's little left to confirm. If the real question is whether to offer a combined
  add+grant convenience verb for the common single-hat citizen, say that. Fix: rephrase to "whether to offer a combined
  add+grant convenience wrapper for the common one-hat case."

  Overall assessment

  This is strong, well-grounded design capture — the conceptual spine (decouple identity from capability; store intent in
  a declared roster; audit declared-vs-IAM; map healing asymmetry onto the Pale recognition test) is coherent and the
  GCP facts it rests on are accurate and current, which I confirmed independently. The most important findings are not
  conceptual refutations but scoping imprecisions that the same rigor elsewhere should reach: the auto-heal asymmetry
  leans on roster integrity that's filed open (#1); the setIamPolicy-free claim is wrong for resource-scope repoAdmin
  (#2) in a way that actually strengthens the composed-resolution argument once corrected; and the clean two-part
  ontology can't house the Mason/Envoy identities the model itself posits (#3). On posture, the paddock is carrying more
  mechanism than "pure shape" warrants (#7, #8) and "done" over-bundles an admittedly-optional rename (#6). None of these
  are fatal; most are one-or-two-sentence qualifiers that sharpen the model rather than overturn it. The single most
  consequential one to resolve before freezing is #1, because it's a security property currently asserted as cinched
  while its precondition sits in the Open list.

  Sources (GCP verification): Tags for service accounts (Preview) · Set limits on granting roles — modifiedGrantsByRole ·
  Service accounts overview

content.text:
This is a high-quality review — it verified my facts independently, calibrated with what's solid, and most findings are sharp. Let me give you my own verdict on each (I'm not rubber-stamping the reviewer either), apply the ones I strongly agree with, and tee up the rest.

My assessment, in short: findings **#1, #2, #5, #11** are real errors/contradictions I fully accept; **#7, #8** directly serve the pure-shape goal you set; **#3, #4, #9, #12, #13** are correct precision fixes. **#6, #10**, and a deeper version of **#4**, are scope/posture judgments that are yours — those I'm listing, not applying.

Applying the amendments now (rewriting both files with the fixes folded in).

input.content:
# Citizen Tier — Identity / Capability Mechanics

Date: 2026-06-05

Status: Design captured during the 260605 grooming conversation for heat ₣BZ
(`rbk-14-citizen-model`). The *shape* lives in the ₣BZ paddock; this memo holds the
*mechanism* the paddock points to. Nothing here is built yet. Amended 260605 after an
independent review pass (findings #1–#5, #9, #11, #12).

## Scope and vocabulary

This heat builds the **citizen tier** — the current, keyfile, no-org operator-credential
tier — restructured so its capability layer is identical to the future federation tier's, so
that federation plugs in later by swapping identity-kind and token mint and nothing else.

- **Citizen** — a keyfile-tier operator identity: a depot-issued service account holding one
  RBRA key. Rank-neutral (governor, director, retriever are all citizens); the home of the
  identity, not the role. Possession of the key is the identity.
- **Federate** (future, not this heat) — a federation-tier operator identity: an external-IdP
  `principal://` subject the depot recognizes by treaty. A federate holds no key.
- **Payor** — the founding human (OAuth refresh token), outside both tiers; neither citizen nor
  federate (memo-20260527).
- **Capability-set** — a named bundle of IAM grants, defined in code (the role's binding list,
  lifted out of today's invest bodies). Tier-blind.
- **Declared roster** — depot-resident data mapping identity → {capability-sets held}.
  Tier-blind.
- **Holdings** — the depot's artifacts (arks/hoards/bullions). Property, not identities. The
  depot's civic ontology — citizens/federates (people) + holdings (property) — covers *operator*
  identities; cloud-native system identities (Mason, future Envoy) sit outside it, reusing the
  capability-set abstraction without the citizen lifecycle (whether they deserve their own
  ontological category is deferred).

The convergence: the **capability layer** (capability-sets, grant/revoke, the declared roster,
the audit) is tier-blind and shared. The **identity** differs by tier (citizen vs federate) —
that difference is the one real seam. "Structural isomorphism" means the capability layer is
identical across tiers *modulo the opaque principal handle the identity layer supplies*
(`serviceAccount:…` vs `principal://…`); only identity-kind, that handle, and the token mint
(signed-JWT vs STS) differ.

## Verb dissolution

Today's role-keyed verbs decompose into a generic register parameterized by
(actor, capability-set, identity):

| Today | Decomposes to | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| invest (director/retriever) | add-citizen + grant(set) | governor is actor |
| mantle (governor) | add-citizen + grant(governor-set) | payor is actor; mantle is not special |
| divest | revoke(all held) + remove-citizen | |
| roster | read the declared roster | |
| — | grant(set) / revoke(set) | the capability axis, tier-blind |
| — | rekey | keyfile-only identity-backing maintenance |

Actor and capability-set are parameters, not verb variants. Federation reuses the capability
half (grant/revoke/roster) verbatim and swaps the identity half (add-federate, no rekey).

## Identity verbs branch; capability verbs do not

- **Capability verbs (grant/revoke)** never branch on tier — both are an IAM binding on a
  pre-existing identity.
- **Identity verbs** are tier-specific (this is the seam):
  - *citizen* (this heat): add = mint SA + key + deliver RBRA; rekey = rotate the key, bindings
    untouched; remove = revoke-all then delete the SA.
  - *federate* (future): add ≈ first grant (identity owned by the IdP, not minted here); no rekey
    (the IdP owns rotation); remove = de-register + revoke-all (the principal is not ours to
    delete).
  - *payor*: install / refresh a human OAuth refresh token.
- Token mint also branches (citizen: signed-JWT; federate: STS) and sits below everything.

## The declared roster

Why intent must be stored (the retired "derive from IAM" cinch was self-defeating once the role
prefix leaves the identity name):

1. Without a name prefix, deriving the roster from IAM is lossy reverse-mapping — a director set
   is several bindings smeared across project + Mason SA + repo + bucket; partial and ambiguous
   matches become representable.
2. You cannot audit IAM against itself — "intent = whatever IAM says" makes intended-vs-actual a
   tautology that always passes, discarding the drift signal.

Federation forces the same roster anyway (a federated principal carries no role marker;
memo-20260527: "the depot remains the home of the roster"), so the roster is paid once for both
tiers and makes routine roster identical and O(1) across tiers.

- **Code vs data.** Capability-set *definitions* are code; the roster is *data* (memberships).
  The audit compares: for each (identity, declared-set), are the code-defined bindings present in
  IAM?
- **Physical home (open).** A GCS object in the depot bucket — mutable, high-churn, etag-guarded
  read-modify-write (like IAM policies themselves) — is the natural fit, unlike tripwire-protected
  RBRD. A GAR artifact is an alternative. Decide in-heat — but the home must place roster-write
  authority at least on par with grant authority (see the audit invariant below), so this is a
  security decision, not only storage.
- **Writers.** The governor writes director/retriever entries; the payor writes the governor
  entry — matching the authority hierarchy.

## The audit

Drift taxonomy (for one identity; D = union of declared sets' bindings, A = actual RBK-relevant
bindings):

| State | Meaning | Likely cause | Heal |
|---|---|---|---|
| D = A | clean | — | — |
| deficit-whole | a declared set wholly absent in A | grant never ran / errant revoke | auto-converge |
| deficit-fragment | a declared set partly present | half-failed multi-scope grant | auto-converge |
| surplus-complete | a whole undeclared named set in A | out-of-band grant | report |
| surplus-fragment | bindings belonging to no whole set | tampering / shrunk definition | report |

**Asymmetric healing = the Pale recognition test:**

- **Deficit is ours** (our own grant failed to complete) → auto-converge up to D (re-run the
  idempotent grant), logged, never silent.
- **Surplus crossed the Pale** (IAM touched outside our verbs) → characterize, report, let a human
  adjudicate (legitimize → declare it, or reject → revoke it). Never auto-revoke (a stale roster
  would deprovision a working identity); never auto-backfill intent (re-enters the tautology). No
  verb auto-revokes; the destructive operation is always human-driven.

**Invariant the asymmetry rests on.** Auto-converging deficits treats the declared roster as
authoritative intent, so writing the roster *upward* is itself a grant. The auto-heal is safe only
if roster-write authority is at least as protected as the grant verbs (setIamPolicy); otherwise it
is a softer escalation path than setIamPolicy. The roster's physical home (above) must satisfy that
ACL constraint — it is a security boundary, not a storage convenience.

Consequences:

- Because grant writes intent *first*, our tooling cannot produce a surplus *absent a definition
  change* — so a surplus is normally a clean "someone bypassed the verbs" signal. The one
  tooling-side surplus source is a capability-set definition *shrink* (next bullet), which strands a
  binding our own earlier grant placed — which is why surplus is reported, not auto-revoked,
  regardless of origin.
- A capability-set definition change decomposes into deficit-on-additions (auto-heal via
  roster-wide re-converge) + surplus-on-removals (report for human-confirmed revoke) — no special
  versioning machinery.
- Audit domain = roster-identities × set-defined bindings; non-roster principals are out of scope.

## Orphan hazard and the identity marker (citizen tier only)

With no name prefix and no SA labels, nothing marks an SA as RBK-managed — so a failed add-citizen
can strand a live key for an untracked SA (a credential leak you cannot find). Marker options:

- **SA tags** (tag bindings): key-value, listable, condition-usable — the proper metadata
  mechanism. Caveat: SA tags are **Preview** (may change).
- **SA `description` sentinel** (`rbk:…`): stable fallback, used only for the orphan-sweep; the
  roster owns capability, the description earns exactly this one job.

Federation has no key to leak and a stray principal binding is visible in the policy, so this
hazard is citizen-tier-specific.

## Authority — who may grant which capability-set

- **Resource-scope-split principle.** An authority boundary is IAM-real only where it coincides
  with a resource boundary. Payor/governor holds because billing is a separate resource that
  governor's `roles/owner` excludes. Governor/director does not — both live in project IAM and
  governor has owner over it — so "governor may grant only director/retriever" is, by itself,
  honor-system.
- **IAM can scope grants by role** (corrects the first paddock draft). The condition attribute
  `iam.googleapis.com/modifiedGrantsByRole` limits which roles a setIamPolicy-capable principal
  may grant/revoke:
  `api.getAttribute('iam.googleapis.com/modifiedGrantsByRole', []).hasOnly(['roles/x','roles/y'])`.
  Limits: works only on **project/folder/org** allow policies, **not** resource-level (AR repo,
  bucket, individual SA); ≤10 roles per condition; don't join `hasOnly` with `&&`/`||`; some
  services don't recognize the limit (request fails); the governor must NOT hold plain
  `roles/owner` (unconditional setIamPolicy) — give it a conditioned setIamPolicy instead.
  Google's own caveat — don't let a limited admin grant roles bearing setIamPolicy — is satisfied
  at *project* scope: the project-scoped director/retriever grants bear no setIamPolicy. The
  repo-scoped `roles/artifactregistry.repoAdmin` *does* carry
  `artifactregistry.repositories.setIamPolicy` — but that is exactly the resource-scope residual
  the detective audit covers, and modifiedGrantsByRole does not reach repo scope anyway.
- **Composed resolution.**
  - Project-scoped grants → IAM-limit on the governor (preventive, server-side).
  - Resource-scoped grants (repo/bucket/SA) → not IAM-limitable → detective audit covers the
    residual.
  - Topology → pull grant authority to the payor where a standing governor buys nothing.
- **Tier-dependent.** Keyfile/solo (payor == admin) → topology: a standing owner-holding citizen
  buys nothing and is pure attack surface; drop or demote it. Federation/multi-admin (no
  exfiltrable key) → a condition-limited governor is the right preventive tool.
- **Audit ↔ topology seam.** Surplus-detection's security value depends on topology: a broad
  standing governor makes surplus-detection the only escalation tripwire (detective-only, weak vs
  malice); authority at the payor closes escalation structurally and demotes the audit to
  honest-mistake hygiene. Do not substitute detective audit for structural closure.
- **Ephemeral governor** (decoupling-enabled option): grant the governor-set for one op, then
  revoke. Shrinks resting owner-exposure; relevant only if a keyfile delegated governor is ever
  wanted.

## Blast radius — full reasoning

One citizen per person = one SA = one key carrying the **union** of granted capabilities. This is
**forced** by "one identity per person": an SA's key always wields the SA's full bindings; you
cannot scope one key to a subset. The only escape — separate SAs per role — is the old model,
which discards the citizen goal. So accepting multi-role on one citizen *is* accepting the union
key; they are one decision.

- Cost: larger resting target (a leaked key carries more).
- Bounded: the *resting-key* form of this exposure is citizen-tier-only (a file to steal);
  federation has no key at rest. The union-of-capabilities-on-one-identity property is itself
  tier-blind — a federate granted director+retriever wields both too, exposed through a hijacked
  live session rather than a file at rest. Most citizens wear one hat (one-per-person ==
  one-per-role).
- Upside: finer incident response — revoke a capability without touching the citizen/key, or rekey
  without touching capabilities. The old model's only lever was delete-and-recreate.

## Governor teardown leak (folds in)

`rbgp_governor_mantle` deletes outgoing `governor-*` SAs without first revoking their grants,
minting a `deleted:…?uid=` tombstone per re-mantle. The leak closes cheaply: add the generic
revoke-before-delete (reusing the rbk-08 revoke layer + Class-C propagation tolerance) before the
existing delete — no tombstone, no naming change. The further idempotent-governor refinement (rekey
instead of delete+recreate, dropping the delete entirely) is *not* free: it needs a *stable*
governor name in place of today's datestamped `governor-YYYYMMDDHHmm` (which exists precisely to
dodge the delete→recreate flap), plus a one-time migration of existing datestamped governors.
Standalone leak fix (if the heat descopes the governor):
memo-20260605-governor-mantle-tombstone-leak.md.

## Verified facts (checked 260605)

- **Service accounts do not support resource labels.** They support **tags** (tag bindings),
  currently in Preview. So the orphan marker uses tags (Preview) or the description sentinel, not a
  label.
- **IAM can limit which roles a principal may grant** via `modifiedGrantsByRole`, but only on
  project/folder/org policies and ≤10 roles — not on resource-level policies.

Sources:
- Set limits on granting roles — https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/setting-limits-on-granting-roles
- Tags for service accounts — https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/service-accounts-tags
- Service accounts overview — https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/service-account-overview

## memo-20260527 divergence (record when the model is frozen)

memo-20260527 states the operator verbs "keep their names, only their bodies differ," and that
keyfile holds "one SA key file each per role." This heat supersedes both: the verb *bodies*
converge in shape (not just names), and the citizen tier is one SA *per person*, not per role.

A third divergence the paddock leans on by name: 20260527's "single code seam" is a *role-keyed*
token accessor ("a token as director"), shippable with no behavior change over today's per-role
SAs. Under one-SA-per-person the accessor becomes *identity-keyed* ("a token as alice", carrying the
union) — a generalization of that seam, but *not* itself no-behavior-change, since it presupposes
the per-person migration. (Whether the heat's "load-bearing core" is the role-keyed no-behavior
stepping-stone or the identity-keyed end state is an open scoping question — see the paddock.)

Update memo-20260527 to record these when the model is frozen.

input.content:
# jjezs_paddock ₣BZ

## Boundary — shape, not mechanism (cinched)

This heat builds the **citizen tier** — the current keyfile, no-org operator-credential tier —
restructured so its capability layer is identical to the future federation tier's. The backing
stays keyfile; a no-org operator runs it unchanged. We do **not** touch federation here.
Explicitly OUT of scope: Workforce pool/provider setup, STS exchange, IdP device flow, and the
RBRD mode-enum activation — federation's own future work (RBSHR "Operator federation";
memo-20260527).

The payoff is structural, not nominal: the **capability layer** (capability-sets, grant/revoke,
the declared roster, the audit) becomes tier-blind, so a later federation switch reshapes nothing
in it — it swaps the identity-kind (citizen → federate) and the token mint (signed-JWT → STS) and
nothing else. That is what makes the switch non-disruptive.

Mechanism for everything below lives in `Memos/memo-20260605-citizen-capability-model.md`; this
paddock holds shape only.

NOTE — supersedes memo-20260527: it says operator verbs "keep their names, only bodies differ" and
keyfile is "one SA key file each per role." This heat converges the verb *bodies* in shape, makes
the citizen tier one SA *per person*, and generalizes 20260527's role-keyed token seam to an
identity-keyed one. Update that memo when the model is frozen.

## The citizen model — identity decoupled from capability

- **Citizen** = a keyfile-tier operator identity (governor/director/retriever), a depot-issued SA
  holding one RBRA key. Rank-neutral: rank is not a property of the citizen, it lives in the
  capability-sets the citizen holds. Multi-role is first-class — one citizen holds several
  capability-sets. (The federation tier's identity is a *federate*; the payor is the founder,
  outside both.)
- **Identity lifecycle is decoupled from capability lifecycle** — the spine. Adding/removing a
  citizen is independent of granting/revoking its capabilities; this is native to the GCP
  primitive (an SA may exist with zero bindings). Today's `invest` fuses the two; the model
  unfuses them.
- **Capability-set** = a named bundle of IAM grants, defined in code (lifted out of the invest
  bodies). Tier-blind.
- **The verbs dissolve** to add-citizen / remove-citizen / grant / revoke / rekey + read-roster,
  parameterized by (actor, capability-set, citizen). invest/divest/mantle/roster stop existing as
  distinct concepts.
- **Capability verbs are tier-blind; only identity verbs branch by tier** (and by the payor's
  OAuth backing). rekey is keyfile-only.
- **Grant/revoke operate only on named capability-sets, never raw bindings** — the contamination
  guard that keeps the roster auditable.
- Ontology: the depot's civic ontology covers **operator** identities — citizens (and later
  federates), people who hold capabilities — and **holdings** (arks/hoards/bullions), property.
  Cloud-native system identities (Mason, future Envoy) sit outside it.

## Intent vs enforcement — the declared roster (cinched)

Enforcement is server-side IAM, always. Intent — which citizen is meant to hold which capability —
is stored in a depot-resident **declared roster**, distinct from IAM. The audit diffs declared
intent against IAM; routine roster reads intent and is identical across tiers. Federation forces
the same roster anyway, so it is paid once for both. (Why it must be stored, and where it lives:
memo.)

## The audit — drift as signal, asymmetric healing (cinched)

The audit measures drift between declared intent and IAM. Healing is asymmetric, and the asymmetry
is the Pale recognition test:

- **Deficit** (IAM short of intent — our own grant failed) is ours → auto-converge, logged.
- **Surplus** (IAM beyond intent — touched outside our verbs) crossed the Pale → report; a human
  adjudicates. No verb auto-revokes; the destructive operation is always human-driven.

Auto-converge presumes roster-write authority is at least as protected as the grant verbs (else it
is a softer escalation path) — a security constraint on where the roster lives; see Open. (Drift
taxonomy, the intent-first bypass signal, definition-change versioning, and the orphan marker:
memo.)

## Authority topology — the one seam with policy content

"Who may grant which capability-set." Shape:

- Authority boundaries are IAM-real only where they fall on resource-scope splits (payor/governor
  holds because billing is a separate resource; governor/director does not).
- Grant authority is preventively scopable at **project** scope but only **detectively** at
  **resource** scope — so the resolution composes: IAM-limit the project-scoped grants,
  detective-audit the resource-scoped residual, and pull authority to the payor where a standing
  governor buys nothing.
- **Tier-dependent**: keyfile/solo → topology (no/minimal standing governor); federation/multi-admin
  → a condition-limited governor. The model allows the answer to differ (actor and capability-set
  are parameters).
- Surplus-detection's security value depends on the topology — do not substitute detective audit for
  structural closure. (The IAM attribute, its scope/role limits, and the seam reasoning: memo.)

## Blast radius — larger resting target, finer response (cinched)

One citizen = one SA = one key carrying the union of held capabilities — forced by "one identity per
person," accepted as a tradeoff. The resting-key form of this exposure is citizen-tier-only
(federation has no key at rest; the union property itself is tier-blind); most citizens wear one
hat; and decoupling buys finer incident response (revoke a capability without touching the key;
rekey without touching capabilities). (Reasoning: memo.)

## Inherited concern — governor teardown leak

Governor teardown sits inside this heat's blast radius: today's mantle leaks a project tombstone
per re-mantle. The convergence closes it (cheap revoke-before-delete; an idempotent governor is a
further, not-free refinement). Mechanism, and the standalone fallback if the governor is descoped:
memo + memo-20260605-governor-mantle-tombstone-leak.md.

## What done looks like

Every cult-verb surface migrated to the citizen/capability vocabulary: workstation bash (role-keyed
selection collapses to one identity accessor), the governor/payor admin verbs (invest/divest/roster/
mantle → add/grant/revoke/remove + read, parameterized by capability-set), cloud-side bash wherever
it names roles, the .adoc specs that voice the cult verbs, the Rust theurge cases that drive them,
and the onboarding handbook tracks that teach them. "citizen" replaces the role-named identity
throughout.

The load-bearing federation-enabling core within that surface is narrow: route the
role/file-path-keyed token-accessor calls through one identity-keyed accessor (generalizing
memo-20260527's "single code seam"). The vocabulary convergence is the larger,
motivated-but-not-required remainder. Scoping/heat-chopping is deferred — recorded here as shape.

## Cinched decisions

- Enforcement is server-side IAM. No file or client-side check is ever the boundary.
- The identity is the **citizen** — rank-neutral, keyfile-backed (the federation tier's peer is a
  *federate*; the payor is the founder, outside both). Identity lifecycle is decoupled from
  capability lifecycle. Multi-role is first-class.
- The citizen tier is one SA (one key, union of capabilities) per person, not one per role —
  blast-radius tradeoff accepted.
- Capability-sets are named code; grant/revoke operate only on named sets, never raw bindings.
- The declared roster (intent) is part of the model; IAM is enforcement; the audit diffs them.
- Audit healing is asymmetric: auto-converge deficits, report surplus; no verb auto-revokes —
  with auto-converge presuming roster-write is at least as protected as grant.
- Governor breadth is resolved per tier, not globally: keyfile pulls grant authority to the payor /
  minimizes the standing governor; federation may keep a condition-limited governor.
- The mode enum (keyfile vs federation) belongs in RBRD and is added only when federation lands —
  not in this heat.
- Migration grandfathers existing role-named SAs: read grants from IAM, register citizens in the
  declared roster, let legacy `director-…`/`retriever-…` names age out. No flag day.
- The convergence covers operator citizens; cloud-native identities (Mason, future Envoy) share the
  capability-set abstraction but not the citizen lifecycle.

## Retired cinches

- "Roster derives from IAM (an `rbk-identity` label), not a stored list — it cannot drift." Retired:
  under decoupling, deriving from IAM is lossy and makes the intended-vs-actual audit a tautology,
  and SA labels do not exist (SAs support tags, in Preview). Replaced by the declared roster +
  asymmetric audit. The true invariant it reached for — no enforcement state outside IAM — is
  preserved; the false corollary (no intent state may exist) is dropped.

## Open — resolve within the heat

- Where the declared roster physically lives (GCS object in the depot bucket vs GAR artifact) and
  its writer/etag discipline — a security boundary, not just storage: roster-write authority must be
  at least as protected as the grant verbs (the auto-heal invariant).
- Whether the keyfile tier drops the standing governor entirely or demotes it to non-privileged —
  this choice gates whether project-scope grant-limiting is in-heat.
- The citizen orphan marker: SA tags (Preview) vs description sentinel.
- Whether to offer a combined add+grant convenience wrapper for the common one-hat citizen (the
  two-gesture add-then-grant flow itself follows from the decoupling).

## Sources

The per-person design exploration in rbk-08-credential-repairs that birthed this heat;
memo-20260604-credential-churn-leak-and-propagation-races (the lifecycle split — the enabler);
memo-20260527-operator-credential-models (the two-tier plan and cult-verb shape — to be updated per
the divergence above); memo-20260605-governor-mantle-tombstone-leak (standalone governor-leak fix);
memo-20260605-citizen-capability-model (the mechanism this paddock points to); RBSHR "Operator
federation". This revision folds in the 260605 design conversation and the 260605 review pass.
