input.content:
# Citizen Tier — Identity / Capability Mechanics

Date: 2026-06-05

Status: Design captured during the 260605 grooming conversation for heat ₣BZ
(`rbk-14-citizen-model`). The *shape* lives in the ₣BZ paddock; this memo holds the
*mechanism* the paddock points to. Nothing here is built yet. Amended 260605 after an
independent review pass (findings #1–#5, #9, #11, #12).

## Scope and vocabulary

This heat builds the **citizen tier** — the current, keyfile, no-org operator-credential
tier — restructured so its capability layer is identical to the future federation tier's, so
that federation plugs in later by swapping identity-kind and token mint and nothing else.

- **Citizen** — a keyfile-tier operator identity: a depot-issued service account holding one
  RBRA key. Rank-neutral (governor, director, retriever are all citizens); the home of the
  identity, not the role. Possession of the key is the identity.
- **Federate** (future, not this heat) — a federation-tier operator identity: an external-IdP
  `principal://` subject the depot recognizes by treaty. A federate holds no key.
- **Payor** — the founding human (OAuth refresh token), outside both tiers; neither citizen nor
  federate (memo-20260527).
- **Capability-set** — a named bundle of IAM grants, defined in code (the role's binding list,
  lifted out of today's invest bodies). Tier-blind.
- **Declared roster** — depot-resident data mapping identity → {capability-sets held}.
  Tier-blind.
- **Holdings** — the depot's artifacts (arks/hoards/bullions). Property, not identities. The
  depot's civic ontology — citizens/federates (people) + holdings (property) — covers *operator*
  identities; cloud-native system identities (Mason, future Envoy) sit outside it, reusing the
  capability-set abstraction without the citizen lifecycle (whether they deserve their own
  ontological category is deferred).

The convergence: the **capability layer** (capability-sets, grant/revoke, the declared roster,
the audit) is tier-blind and shared. The **identity** differs by tier (citizen vs federate) —
that difference is the one real seam. "Structural isomorphism" means the capability layer is
identical across tiers *modulo the opaque principal handle the identity layer supplies*
(`serviceAccount:…` vs `principal://…`); only identity-kind, that handle, and the token mint
(signed-JWT vs STS) differ.

## Verb dissolution

Today's role-keyed verbs decompose into a generic register parameterized by
(actor, capability-set, identity):

| Today | Decomposes to | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| invest (director/retriever) | add-citizen + grant(set) | governor is actor |
| mantle (governor) | add-citizen + grant(governor-set) | payor is actor; mantle is not special |
| divest | revoke(all held) + remove-citizen | |
| roster | read the declared roster | |
| — | grant(set) / revoke(set) | the capability axis, tier-blind |
| — | rekey | keyfile-only identity-backing maintenance |

Actor and capability-set are parameters, not verb variants. Federation reuses the capability
half (grant/revoke/roster) verbatim and swaps the identity half (add-federate, no rekey).

## Identity verbs branch; capability verbs do not

- **Capability verbs (grant/revoke)** never branch on tier — both are an IAM binding on a
  pre-existing identity.
- **Identity verbs** are tier-specific (this is the seam):
  - *citizen* (this heat): add = mint SA + key + deliver RBRA; rekey = rotate the key, bindings
    untouched; remove = revoke-all then delete the SA.
  - *federate* (future): add ≈ first grant (identity owned by the IdP, not minted here); no rekey
    (the IdP owns rotation); remove = de-register + revoke-all (the principal is not ours to
    delete).
  - *payor*: install / refresh a human OAuth refresh token.
- Token mint also branches (citizen: signed-JWT; federate: STS) and sits below everything.

## The declared roster

Why intent must be stored (the retired "derive from IAM" cinch was self-defeating once the role
prefix leaves the identity name):

1. Without a name prefix, deriving the roster from IAM is lossy reverse-mapping — a director set
   is several bindings smeared across project + Mason SA + repo + bucket; partial and ambiguous
   matches become representable.
2. You cannot audit IAM against itself — "intent = whatever IAM says" makes intended-vs-actual a
   tautology that always passes, discarding the drift signal.

Federation forces the same roster anyway (a federated principal carries no role marker;
memo-20260527: "the depot remains the home of the roster"), so the roster is paid once for both
tiers and makes routine roster identical and O(1) across tiers.

- **Code vs data.** Capability-set *definitions* are code; the roster is *data* (memberships).
  The audit compares: for each (identity, declared-set), are the code-defined bindings present in
  IAM?
- **Physical home (open).** A GCS object in the depot bucket — mutable, high-churn, etag-guarded
  read-modify-write (like IAM policies themselves) — is the natural fit, unlike tripwire-protected
  RBRD. A GAR artifact is an alternative. Decide in-heat — but the home must place roster-write
  authority at least on par with grant authority (see the audit invariant below), so this is a
  security decision, not only storage.
- **Writers.** The governor writes director/retriever entries; the payor writes the governor
  entry — matching the authority hierarchy.

## The audit

Drift taxonomy (for one identity; D = union of declared sets' bindings, A = actual RBK-relevant
bindings):

| State | Meaning | Likely cause | Heal |
|---|---|---|---|
| D = A | clean | — | — |
| deficit-whole | a declared set wholly absent in A | grant never ran / errant revoke | auto-converge |
| deficit-fragment | a declared set partly present | half-failed multi-scope grant | auto-converge |
| surplus-complete | a whole undeclared named set in A | out-of-band grant | report |
| surplus-fragment | bindings belonging to no whole set | tampering / shrunk definition | report |

**Asymmetric healing = the Pale recognition test:**

- **Deficit is ours** (our own grant failed to complete) → auto-converge up to D (re-run the
  idempotent grant), logged, never silent.
- **Surplus crossed the Pale** (IAM touched outside our verbs) → characterize, report, let a human
  adjudicate (legitimize → declare it, or reject → revoke it). Never auto-revoke (a stale roster
  would deprovision a working identity); never auto-backfill intent (re-enters the tautology). No
  verb auto-revokes; the destructive operation is always human-driven.

**Invariant the asymmetry rests on.** Auto-converging deficits treats the declared roster as
authoritative intent, so writing the roster *upward* is itself a grant. The auto-heal is safe only
if roster-write authority is at least as protected as the grant verbs (setIamPolicy); otherwise it
is a softer escalation path than setIamPolicy. The roster's physical home (above) must satisfy that
ACL constraint — it is a security boundary, not a storage convenience.

Consequences:

- Because grant writes intent *first*, our tooling cannot produce a surplus *absent a definition
  change* — so a surplus is normally a clean "someone bypassed the verbs" signal. The one
  tooling-side surplus source is a capability-set definition *shrink* (next bullet), which strands a
  binding our own earlier grant placed — which is why surplus is reported, not auto-revoked,
  regardless of origin.
- A capability-set definition change decomposes into deficit-on-additions (auto-heal via
  roster-wide re-converge) + surplus-on-removals (report for human-confirmed revoke) — no special
  versioning machinery.
- Audit domain = roster-identities × set-defined bindings; non-roster principals are out of scope.

## Orphan hazard and the identity marker (citizen tier only)

With no name prefix and no SA labels, nothing marks an SA as RBK-managed — so a failed add-citizen
can strand a live key for an untracked SA (a credential leak you cannot find). Marker options:

- **SA tags** (tag bindings): key-value, listable, condition-usable — the proper metadata
  mechanism. Caveat: SA tags are **Preview** (may change).
- **SA `description` sentinel** (`rbk:…`): stable fallback, used only for the orphan-sweep; the
  roster owns capability, the description earns exactly this one job.

Federation has no key to leak and a stray principal binding is visible in the policy, so this
hazard is citizen-tier-specific.

## Authority — who may grant which capability-set

- **Resource-scope-split principle.** An authority boundary is IAM-real only where it coincides
  with a resource boundary. Payor/governor holds because billing is a separate resource that
  governor's `roles/owner` excludes. Governor/director does not — both live in project IAM and
  governor has owner over it — so "governor may grant only director/retriever" is, by itself,
  honor-system.
- **IAM can scope grants by role** (corrects the first paddock draft). The condition attribute
  `iam.googleapis.com/modifiedGrantsByRole` limits which roles a setIamPolicy-capable principal
  may grant/revoke:
  `api.getAttribute('iam.googleapis.com/modifiedGrantsByRole', []).hasOnly(['roles/x','roles/y'])`.
  Limits: works only on **project/folder/org** allow policies, **not** resource-level (AR repo,
  bucket, individual SA); ≤10 roles per condition; don't join `hasOnly` with `&&`/`||`; some
  services don't recognize the limit (request fails); the governor must NOT hold plain
  `roles/owner` (unconditional setIamPolicy) — give it a conditioned setIamPolicy instead.
  Google's own caveat — don't let a limited admin grant roles bearing setIamPolicy — is satisfied
  at *project* scope: the project-scoped director/retriever grants bear no setIamPolicy. The
  repo-scoped `roles/artifactregistry.repoAdmin` *does* carry
  `artifactregistry.repositories.setIamPolicy` — but that is exactly the resource-scope residual
  the detective audit covers, and modifiedGrantsByRole does not reach repo scope anyway.
- **Composed resolution.**
  - Project-scoped grants → IAM-limit on the governor (preventive, server-side).
  - Resource-scoped grants (repo/bucket/SA) → not IAM-limitable → detective audit covers the
    residual.
  - Topology → pull grant authority to the payor where a standing governor buys nothing.
- **Tier-dependent.** Keyfile/solo (payor == admin) → topology: a standing owner-holding citizen
  buys nothing and is pure attack surface; drop or demote it. Federation/multi-admin (no
  exfiltrable key) → a condition-limited governor is the right preventive tool.
- **Audit ↔ topology seam.** Surplus-detection's security value depends on topology: a broad
  standing governor makes surplus-detection the only escalation tripwire (detective-only, weak vs
  malice); authority at the payor closes escalation structurally and demotes the audit to
  honest-mistake hygiene. Do not substitute detective audit for structural closure.
- **Ephemeral governor** (decoupling-enabled option): grant the governor-set for one op, then
  revoke. Shrinks resting owner-exposure; relevant only if a keyfile delegated governor is ever
  wanted.

## Blast radius — full reasoning

One citizen per person = one SA = one key carrying the **union** of granted capabilities. This is
**forced** by "one identity per person": an SA's key always wields the SA's full bindings; you
cannot scope one key to a subset. The only escape — separate SAs per role — is the old model,
which discards the citizen goal. So accepting multi-role on one citizen *is* accepting the union
key; they are one decision.

- Cost: larger resting target (a leaked key carries more).
- Bounded: the *resting-key* form of this exposure is citizen-tier-only (a file to steal);
  federation has no key at rest. The union-of-capabilities-on-one-identity property is itself
  tier-blind — a federate granted director+retriever wields both too, exposed through a hijacked
  live session rather than a file at rest. Most citizens wear one hat (one-per-person ==
  one-per-role).
- Upside: finer incident response — revoke a capability without touching the citizen/key, or rekey
  without touching capabilities. The old model's only lever was delete-and-recreate.

## Governor teardown leak (folds in)

`rbgp_governor_mantle` deletes outgoing `governor-*` SAs without first revoking their grants,
minting a `deleted:…?uid=` tombstone per re-mantle. The leak closes cheaply: add the generic
revoke-before-delete (reusing the rbk-08 revoke layer + Class-C propagation tolerance) before the
existing delete — no tombstone, no naming change. The further idempotent-governor refinement (rekey
instead of delete+recreate, dropping the delete entirely) is *not* free: it needs a *stable*
governor name in place of today's datestamped `governor-YYYYMMDDHHmm` (which exists precisely to
dodge the delete→recreate flap), plus a one-time migration of existing datestamped governors.
Standalone leak fix (if the heat descopes the governor):
memo-20260605-governor-mantle-tombstone-leak.md.

## Verified facts (checked 260605)

- **Service accounts do not support resource labels.** They support **tags** (tag bindings),
  currently in Preview. So the orphan marker uses tags (Preview) or the description sentinel, not a
  label.
- **IAM can limit which roles a principal may grant** via `modifiedGrantsByRole`, but only on
  project/folder/org policies and ≤10 roles — not on resource-level policies.

Sources:
- Set limits on granting roles — https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/setting-limits-on-granting-roles
- Tags for service accounts — https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/service-accounts-tags
- Service accounts overview — https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/service-account-overview

## memo-20260527 divergence (record when the model is frozen)

memo-20260527 states the operator verbs "keep their names, only their bodies differ," and that
keyfile holds "one SA key file each per role." This heat supersedes both: the verb *bodies*
converge in shape (not just names), and the citizen tier is one SA *per person*, not per role.

A third divergence the paddock leans on by name: 20260527's "single code seam" is a *role-keyed*
token accessor ("a token as director"), shippable with no behavior change over today's per-role
SAs. Under one-SA-per-person the accessor becomes *identity-keyed* ("a token as alice", carrying the
union) — a generalization of that seam, but *not* itself no-behavior-change, since it presupposes
the per-person migration. (Whether the heat's "load-bearing core" is the role-keyed no-behavior
stepping-stone or the identity-keyed end state is an open scoping question — see the paddock.)

Update memo-20260527 to record these when the model is frozen.

toolUseResult.content:
# Citizen Tier — Identity / Capability Mechanics

Date: 2026-06-05

Status: Design captured during the 260605 grooming conversation for heat ₣BZ
(`rbk-14-citizen-model`). The *shape* lives in the ₣BZ paddock; this memo holds the
*mechanism* the paddock points to. Nothing here is built yet. Amended 260605 after an
independent review pass (findings #1–#5, #9, #11, #12).

## Scope and vocabulary

This heat builds the **citizen tier** — the current, keyfile, no-org operator-credential
tier — restructured so its capability layer is identical to the future federation tier's, so
that federation plugs in later by swapping identity-kind and token mint and nothing else.

- **Citizen** — a keyfile-tier operator identity: a depot-issued service account holding one
  RBRA key. Rank-neutral (governor, director, retriever are all citizens); the home of the
  identity, not the role. Possession of the key is the identity.
- **Federate** (future, not this heat) — a federation-tier operator identity: an external-IdP
  `principal://` subject the depot recognizes by treaty. A federate holds no key.
- **Payor** — the founding human (OAuth refresh token), outside both tiers; neither citizen nor
  federate (memo-20260527).
- **Capability-set** — a named bundle of IAM grants, defined in code (the role's binding list,
  lifted out of today's invest bodies). Tier-blind.
- **Declared roster** — depot-resident data mapping identity → {capability-sets held}.
  Tier-blind.
- **Holdings** — the depot's artifacts (arks/hoards/bullions). Property, not identities. The
  depot's civic ontology — citizens/federates (people) + holdings (property) — covers *operator*
  identities; cloud-native system identities (Mason, future Envoy) sit outside it, reusing the
  capability-set abstraction without the citizen lifecycle (whether they deserve their own
  ontological category is deferred).

The convergence: the **capability layer** (capability-sets, grant/revoke, the declared roster,
the audit) is tier-blind and shared. The **identity** differs by tier (citizen vs federate) —
that difference is the one real seam. "Structural isomorphism" means the capability layer is
identical across tiers *modulo the opaque principal handle the identity layer supplies*
(`serviceAccount:…` vs `principal://…`); only identity-kind, that handle, and the token mint
(signed-JWT vs STS) differ.

## Verb dissolution

Today's role-keyed verbs decompose into a generic register parameterized by
(actor, capability-set, identity):

| Today | Decomposes to | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| invest (director/retriever) | add-citizen + grant(set) | governor is actor |
| mantle (governor) | add-citizen + grant(governor-set) | payor is actor; mantle is not special |
| divest | revoke(all held) + remove-citizen | |
| roster | read the declared roster | |
| — | grant(set) / revoke(set) | the capability axis, tier-blind |
| — | rekey | keyfile-only identity-backing maintenance |

Actor and capability-set are parameters, not verb variants. Federation reuses the capability
half (grant/revoke/roster) verbatim and swaps the identity half (add-federate, no rekey).

## Identity verbs branch; capability verbs do not

- **Capability verbs (grant/revoke)** never branch on tier — both are an IAM binding on a
  pre-existing identity.
- **Identity verbs** are tier-specific (this is the seam):
  - *citizen* (this heat): add = mint SA + key + deliver RBRA; rekey = rotate the key, bindings
    untouched; remove = revoke-all then delete the SA.
  - *federate* (future): add ≈ first grant (identity owned by the IdP, not minted here); no rekey
    (the IdP owns rotation); remove = de-register + revoke-all (the principal is not ours to
    delete).
  - *payor*: install / refresh a human OAuth refresh token.
- Token mint also branches (citizen: signed-JWT; federate: STS) and sits below everything.

## The declared roster

Why intent must be stored (the retired "derive from IAM" cinch was self-defeating once the role
prefix leaves the identity name):

1. Without a name prefix, deriving the roster from IAM is lossy reverse-mapping — a director set
   is several bindings smeared across project + Mason SA + repo + bucket; partial and ambiguous
   matches become representable.
2. You cannot audit IAM against itself — "intent = whatever IAM says" makes intended-vs-actual a
   tautology that always passes, discarding the drift signal.

Federation forces the same roster anyway (a federated principal carries no role marker;
memo-20260527: "the depot remains the home of the roster"), so the roster is paid once for both
tiers and makes routine roster identical and O(1) across tiers.

- **Code vs data.** Capability-set *definitions* are code; the roster is *data* (memberships).
  The audit compares: for each (identity, declared-set), are the code-defined bindings present in
  IAM?
- **Physical home (open).** A GCS object in the depot bucket — mutable, high-churn, etag-guarded
  read-modify-write (like IAM policies themselves) — is the natural fit, unlike tripwire-protected
  RBRD. A GAR artifact is an alternative. Decide in-heat — but the home must place roster-write
  authority at least on par with grant authority (see the audit invariant below), so this is a
  security decision, not only storage.
- **Writers.** The governor writes director/retriever entries; the payor writes the governor
  entry — matching the authority hierarchy.

## The audit

Drift taxonomy (for one identity; D = union of declared sets' bindings, A = actual RBK-relevant
bindings):

| State | Meaning | Likely cause | Heal |
|---|---|---|---|
| D = A | clean | — | — |
| deficit-whole | a declared set wholly absent in A | grant never ran / errant revoke | auto-converge |
| deficit-fragment | a declared set partly present | half-failed multi-scope grant | auto-converge |
| surplus-complete | a whole undeclared named set in A | out-of-band grant | report |
| surplus-fragment | bindings belonging to no whole set | tampering / shrunk definition | report |

**Asymmetric healing = the Pale recognition test:**

- **Deficit is ours** (our own grant failed to complete) → auto-converge up to D (re-run the
  idempotent grant), logged, never silent.
- **Surplus crossed the Pale** (IAM touched outside our verbs) → characterize, report, let a human
  adjudicate (legitimize → declare it, or reject → revoke it). Never auto-revoke (a stale roster
  would deprovision a working identity); never auto-backfill intent (re-enters the tautology). No
  verb auto-revokes; the destructive operation is always human-driven.

**Invariant the asymmetry rests on.** Auto-converging deficits treats the declared roster as
authoritative intent, so writing the roster *upward* is itself a grant. The auto-heal is safe only
if roster-write authority is at least as protected as the grant verbs (setIamPolicy); otherwise it
is a softer escalation path than setIamPolicy. The roster's physical home (above) must satisfy that
ACL constraint — it is a security boundary, not a storage convenience.

Consequences:

- Because grant writes intent *first*, our tooling cannot produce a surplus *absent a definition
  change* — so a surplus is normally a clean "someone bypassed the verbs" signal. The one
  tooling-side surplus source is a capability-set definition *shrink* (next bullet), which strands a
  binding our own earlier grant placed — which is why surplus is reported, not auto-revoked,
  regardless of origin.
- A capability-set definition change decomposes into deficit-on-additions (auto-heal via
  roster-wide re-converge) + surplus-on-removals (report for human-confirmed revoke) — no special
  versioning machinery.
- Audit domain = roster-identities × set-defined bindings; non-roster principals are out of scope.

## Orphan hazard and the identity marker (citizen tier only)

With no name prefix and no SA labels, nothing marks an SA as RBK-managed — so a failed add-citizen
can strand a live key for an untracked SA (a credential leak you cannot find). Marker options:

- **SA tags** (tag bindings): key-value, listable, condition-usable — the proper metadata
  mechanism. Caveat: SA tags are **Preview** (may change).
- **SA `description` sentinel** (`rbk:…`): stable fallback, used only for the orphan-sweep; the
  roster owns capability, the description earns exactly this one job.

Federation has no key to leak and a stray principal binding is visible in the policy, so this
hazard is citizen-tier-specific.

## Authority — who may grant which capability-set

- **Resource-scope-split principle.** An authority boundary is IAM-real only where it coincides
  with a resource boundary. Payor/governor holds because billing is a separate resource that
  governor's `roles/owner` excludes. Governor/director does not — both live in project IAM and
  governor has owner over it — so "governor may grant only director/retriever" is, by itself,
  honor-system.
- **IAM can scope grants by role** (corrects the first paddock draft). The condition attribute
  `iam.googleapis.com/modifiedGrantsByRole` limits which roles a setIamPolicy-capable principal
  may grant/revoke:
  `api.getAttribute('iam.googleapis.com/modifiedGrantsByRole', []).hasOnly(['roles/x','roles/y'])`.
  Limits: works only on **project/folder/org** allow policies, **not** resource-level (AR repo,
  bucket, individual SA); ≤10 roles per condition; don't join `hasOnly` with `&&`/`||`; some
  services don't recognize the limit (request fails); the governor must NOT hold plain
  `roles/owner` (unconditional setIamPolicy) — give it a conditioned setIamPolicy instead.
  Google's own caveat — don't let a limited admin grant roles bearing setIamPolicy — is satisfied
  at *project* scope: the project-scoped director/retriever grants bear no setIamPolicy. The
  repo-scoped `roles/artifactregistry.repoAdmin` *does* carry
  `artifactregistry.repositories.setIamPolicy` — but that is exactly the resource-scope residual
  the detective audit covers, and modifiedGrantsByRole does not reach repo scope anyway.
- **Composed resolution.**
  - Project-scoped grants → IAM-limit on the governor (preventive, server-side).
  - Resource-scoped grants (repo/bucket/SA) → not IAM-limitable → detective audit covers the
    residual.
  - Topology → pull grant authority to the payor where a standing governor buys nothing.
- **Tier-dependent.** Keyfile/solo (payor == admin) → topology: a standing owner-holding citizen
  buys nothing and is pure attack surface; drop or demote it. Federation/multi-admin (no
  exfiltrable key) → a condition-limited governor is the right preventive tool.
- **Audit ↔ topology seam.** Surplus-detection's security value depends on topology: a broad
  standing governor makes surplus-detection the only escalation tripwire (detective-only, weak vs
  malice); authority at the payor closes escalation structurally and demotes the audit to
  honest-mistake hygiene. Do not substitute detective audit for structural closure.
- **Ephemeral governor** (decoupling-enabled option): grant the governor-set for one op, then
  revoke. Shrinks resting owner-exposure; relevant only if a keyfile delegated governor is ever
  wanted.

## Blast radius — full reasoning

One citizen per person = one SA = one key carrying the **union** of granted capabilities. This is
**forced** by "one identity per person": an SA's key always wields the SA's full bindings; you
cannot scope one key to a subset. The only escape — separate SAs per role — is the old model,
which discards the citizen goal. So accepting multi-role on one citizen *is* accepting the union
key; they are one decision.

- Cost: larger resting target (a leaked key carries more).
- Bounded: the *resting-key* form of this exposure is citizen-tier-only (a file to steal);
  federation has no key at rest. The union-of-capabilities-on-one-identity property is itself
  tier-blind — a federate granted director+retriever wields both too, exposed through a hijacked
  live session rather than a file at rest. Most citizens wear one hat (one-per-person ==
  one-per-role).
- Upside: finer incident response — revoke a capability without touching the citizen/key, or rekey
  without touching capabilities. The old model's only lever was delete-and-recreate.

## Governor teardown leak (folds in)

`rbgp_governor_mantle` deletes outgoing `governor-*` SAs without first revoking their grants,
minting a `deleted:…?uid=` tombstone per re-mantle. The leak closes cheaply: add the generic
revoke-before-delete (reusing the rbk-08 revoke layer + Class-C propagation tolerance) before the
existing delete — no tombstone, no naming change. The further idempotent-governor refinement (rekey
instead of delete+recreate, dropping the delete entirely) is *not* free: it needs a *stable*
governor name in place of today's datestamped `governor-YYYYMMDDHHmm` (which exists precisely to
dodge the delete→recreate flap), plus a one-time migration of existing datestamped governors.
Standalone leak fix (if the heat descopes the governor):
memo-20260605-governor-mantle-tombstone-leak.md.

## Verified facts (checked 260605)

- **Service accounts do not support resource labels.** They support **tags** (tag bindings),
  currently in Preview. So the orphan marker uses tags (Preview) or the description sentinel, not a
  label.
- **IAM can limit which roles a principal may grant** via `modifiedGrantsByRole`, but only on
  project/folder/org policies and ≤10 roles — not on resource-level policies.

Sources:
- Set limits on granting roles — https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/setting-limits-on-granting-roles
- Tags for service accounts — https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/service-accounts-tags
- Service accounts overview — https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/service-account-overview

## memo-20260527 divergence (record when the model is frozen)

memo-20260527 states the operator verbs "keep their names, only their bodies differ," and that
keyfile holds "one SA key file each per role." This heat supersedes both: the verb *bodies*
converge in shape (not just names), and the citizen tier is one SA *per person*, not per role.

A third divergence the paddock leans on by name: 20260527's "single code seam" is a *role-keyed*
token accessor ("a token as director"), shippable with no behavior change over today's per-role
SAs. Under one-SA-per-person the accessor becomes *identity-keyed* ("a token as alice", carrying the
union) — a generalization of that seam, but *not* itself no-behavior-change, since it presupposes
the per-person migration. (Whether the heat's "load-bearing core" is the role-keyed no-behavior
stepping-stone or the identity-keyed end state is an open scoping question — see the paddock.)

Update memo-20260527 to record these when the model is frozen.

content.text:
Both committed, tree clean (paddock `552925625`, memo `a6a0eba72`).

## My verdict on the review

The reviewer earned its keep — it verified my GCP facts independently and most findings are real. Here's my own judgment on each, not a rubber-stamp.

**Applied (11 findings):**

| # | Type | My verdict | What I changed |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | ERROR | **Strong agree** — the sharpest catch | Added the invariant: auto-heal is safe only if roster-write authority ≥ grant authority; elevated the roster-home Open item to a security decision |
| 2 | ERROR | **Strong agree** — I overclaimed | Scoped it: project-scoped grants bear no setIamPolicy; repo-scoped `repoAdmin` *does* (`repositories.setIamPolicy`) — which is exactly the resource-scope residual the audit covers |
| 5 | ERROR | **Strong agree** — genuine self-contradiction | Qualified: tooling can't produce surplus *absent a definition shrink*, which is the one tooling-side source |
| 11 | CLARITY | **Strong agree** — "for free" oversold | Split it: the leak closes cheaply (revoke-before-delete); idempotent governor is a *further, not-free* step (stable name + migration off datestamps) |
| 7, 8 | CONCISE | **Strong agree** — serves the pure-shape goal you set | Compressed the paddock's governor + authority sections to shape; removed the `modifiedGrantsByRole` attribute name (now memo-only) |
| 3 | CONFLICT | Agree | Scoped the civic ontology to *operator* identities; cloud-native sit outside it (chose this over inventing a third category — that naming is deferrable) |
| 4 | CONFLICT | Agree (precise part) | Recorded the third divergence: 20260527's seam is role-keyed, the citizen model's is identity-keyed |
| 9 | CLARITY | Agree | Resting-key form is citizen-only; the union property itself is tier-blind (federate exposes it via live session) |
| 12 | CLARITY | Agree (reviewer's low-confidence flag was fair) | "byte-identical" → "identical *modulo the opaque principal handle*" |
| 13 | CLARITY | Agree | Open item rephrased to the real question: a combined add+grant *convenience wrapper* for one-hat citizens |

I also partially absorbed **#10**: I added to the Open item that the keyfile drop-vs-demote choice *gates whether project-scope grant-limiting is in-heat* — which is the reviewer's real concern.

## Remaining — let's talk (2 items)

**A. The scoping question (reviewer's #6 + the deeper half of #4).** These collapse into one real decision you've been deferring: *what is the heat's minimal "done"?* There are arguably two cores —
- **(a)** memo-20260527's *role-keyed* token-accessor refactor — ships now, **zero behavior change**, real federation-prep, needs none of the citizen migration. A clean de-risking first step.
- **(b)** the citizen *identity-keyed* accessor + one-SA-per-person — the actual model, which **is** a behavior change.

My recommendation: name both, and likely do (a) first as a no-risk stepping stone, then (b). But choosing this — and whether to demote the codebase-wide rename to an explicitly-optional follow-on — *is* the heat-chopping you deferred, so I left "what done looks like" describing the full surface and didn't cut it. Your call on whether to cut now.

**B. #10 residual — word choice only.** I kept the cinch "Governor breadth is **resolved** per tier." I partially disagree with the reviewer here: the cinch resolves the *approach* (keyfile = topological/minimize governor), while the Open holds the *specific* (drop vs demote) — that two-level split is legitimate, and I added the gating note. The only open question is whether "resolved" overstates it or you'd prefer "approached per tier." Minor; your preference.

Want to start with A? It's the one with real consequences for how this heat gets paced.

content.text: Ultracode's on, and "improvements throughout" is exactly the kind of exhaustive, adversarially-verified sweep a workflow is for. I'll fan out independent reviewers across nine lenses over the *current* (already-amended) paddock + memo — including agents that re-verify the GCP facts on the web and grep the actual RBK bash for current-behavior mismatches — then adversarially verify every candidate finding before synthesizing. I'm passing the live paddock text in as args so the agents review what's actually committed, not a stale copy.

input.args:
{"paddock":"# ₣BZ paddock — rbk-14-citizen-model (current committed text)

## Boundary — shape, not mechanism (cinched)
This heat builds the citizen tier — the current keyfile, no-org operator-credential tier — restructured so its capability layer is identical to the future federation tier's. The backing stays keyfile; a no-org operator runs it unchanged. We do NOT touch federation here. OUT of scope: Workforce pool/provider setup, STS exchange, IdP device flow, RBRD mode-enum activation — federation's own future work (RBSHR Operator federation; memo-20260527).
The payoff is structural, not nominal: the capability layer (capability-sets, grant/revoke, the declared roster, the audit) becomes tier-blind, so a later federation switch reshapes nothing in it — it swaps identity-kind (citizen->federate) and token mint (signed-JWT->STS) and nothing else.
Mechanism lives in Memos/memo-20260605-citizen-capability-model.md; this paddock holds shape only.
NOTE supersedes memo-20260527: verbs keep names/bodies differ + keyfile one SA key per role. This heat converges verb bodies in shape, citizen tier one SA per person, generalizes 20260527 role-keyed seam to identity-keyed.

## The citizen model — identity decoupled from capability
- Citizen = keyfile-tier operator identity (governor/director/retriever), depot-issued SA holding one RBRA key. Rank-neutral: rank lives in capability-sets held. Multi-role first-class. (Federation peer = federate; payor = founder, outside both.)
- Identity lifecycle decoupled from capability lifecycle — the spine. Native to GCP primitive (SA may exist with zero bindings). invest fuses the two; model unfuses.
- Capability-set = named bundle of IAM grants, defined in code. Tier-blind.
- Verbs dissolve to add-citizen/remove-citizen/grant/revoke/rekey + read-roster, parameterized by (actor, capability-set, citizen). invest/divest/mantle/roster stop existing as distinct concepts.
- Capability verbs tier-blind; only identity verbs branch by tier (and payor OAuth). rekey keyfile-only.
- Grant/revoke operate only on named capability-sets, never raw bindings.
- Ontology: civic ontology covers operator identities — citizens (later federates) hold capabilities — and holdings (arks/hoards/bullions) property. Cloud-native system identities (Mason, future Envoy) sit outside it.

## Intent vs enforcement — the declared roster (cinched)
Enforcement is server-side IAM, always. Intent stored in depot-resident declared roster, distinct from IAM. Audit diffs declared vs IAM; routine roster reads intent, identical across tiers. Federation forces same roster anyway. (Why/where: memo.)

## The audit — drift as signal, asymmetric healing (cinched)
Audit measures drift declared-vs-IAM. Healing asymmetric = Pale recognition test:
- Deficit (IAM short, our grant failed) is ours -> auto-converge, logged.
- Surplus (IAM beyond, touched outside our verbs) crossed Pale -> report; human adjudicates. No verb auto-revokes; destructive op human-driven.
Auto-converge presumes roster-write authority at least as protected as grant verbs (else softer escalation path) — security constraint on roster home; see Open. (Taxonomy, intent-first bypass signal, definition-change versioning, orphan marker: memo.)

## Authority topology — the one seam with policy content
- Authority boundaries IAM-real only at resource-scope splits (payor/governor holds because billing is a separate resource; governor/director does not).
- Grant authority preventively scopable at project scope but only detectively at resource scope -> resolution composes IAM-limited project grants, detective-audit resource residual, pull authority to payor where standing governor buys nothing.
- Tier-dependent: keyfile/solo -> topology (no/minimal standing governor); federation/multi-admin -> condition-limited governor.
- Surplus-detection security value depends on topology — don't substitute detective audit for structural closure. (Attribute, limits, seam: memo.)

## Blast radius — larger resting target, finer response (cinched)
One citizen = one SA = one key carrying union of held capabilities — forced by one-identity-per-person, tradeoff accepted. Resting-key form citizen-tier-only (federation no key at rest; union property tier-blind); most citizens one hat; decoupling buys finer incident response (revoke capability without touching key; rekey without touching capabilities). (Reasoning: memo.)

## Inherited concern — governor teardown leak
Governor teardown sits inside this heat's blast radius: mantle leaks a project tombstone per re-mantle. Convergence closes it (cheap revoke-before-delete; idempotent governor a further, not-free refinement). Mechanism + standalone fallback if descoped: memo + memo-20260605-governor-mantle-tombstone-leak.md.

## What done looks like
Every cult-verb surface migrated to citizen/capability vocabulary: workstation bash (role-keyed selection collapses to one identity accessor), governor/payor admin verbs (invest/divest/roster/mantle -> add/grant/revoke/remove + read parameterized by capability-set), cloud-side bash naming roles, .adoc specs voicing cult verbs, Rust theurge cases, onboarding handbook. citizen replaces role-named identity throughout.
Load-bearing federation-enabling core is narrow: route role/file-path-keyed token-accessor calls through one identity-keyed accessor (generalizing memo-20260527 single code seam). Vocabulary convergence is larger motivated-but-not-required remainder. Scoping/heat-chopping deferred.

## Cinched decisions
- Enforcement server-side IAM; no client-side check ever the boundary.
- Identity is the citizen — rank-neutral, keyfile-backed (federation peer federate; payor founder outside both). Identity lifecycle decoupled from capability. Multi-role first-class.
- Citizen tier one SA (one key, union) per person, not per role — blast-radius tradeoff accepted.
- Capability-sets named code; grant/revoke only on named sets, never raw bindings.
- Declared roster (intent) part of model; IAM enforcement; audit diffs.
- Audit healing asymmetric: auto-converge deficits, report surplus; no verb auto-revokes — auto-converge presumes roster-write at least as protected as grant.
- Governor breadth resolved per tier: keyfile pulls grant authority to payor/minimizes standing governor; federation may keep condition-limited governor.
- Mode enum (keyfile vs federation) belongs in RBRD, added only when federation lands.
- Migration grandfathers role-named SAs: read grants from IAM, register citizens in roster, legacy director-/retriever- names age out. No flag day.
- Convergence covers operator citizens; cloud-native (Mason, future Envoy) share capability-set abstraction not citizen lifecycle.

## Retired cinches
- Roster derives from IAM (rbk-identity label), not stored list, cannot drift. Retired: deriving from IAM lossy + tautological under decoupling; SA labels don't exist (SAs support tags, Preview). Replaced by declared roster + asymmetric audit. True invariant (no enforcement state outside IAM) preserved; false corollary (no intent state) dropped.

## Open — resolve within the heat
- Where declared roster physically lives (GCS object in depot bucket vs GAR artifact) + writer/etag discipline — security boundary not just storage: roster-write authority at least as protected as grant verbs (auto-heal invariant).
- Whether keyfile drops standing governor entirely or demotes to non-privileged — gates whether project-scope grant-limiting is in-heat.
- Citizen orphan marker: SA tags (Preview) vs description sentinel.
- Whether to offer combined add+grant convenience wrapper for common one-hat citizen (two-gesture flow follows from decoupling).

## Sources
rbk-08-credential-repairs; memo-20260604; memo-20260527 (to update); memo-20260605-governor-mantle-tombstone-leak; memo-20260605-citizen-capability-model (mechanism); RBSHR Operator federation."}

content.text:
The review is running in the background — **9 lenses** (internal coherence, source conflicts, spec-behavior fidelity, bash reality, web-verified GCP facts, adversarial security, vocabulary/minting, conciseness/shape-discipline, and a completeness critic), each producing candidate findings that then get **adversarially verified** against the real files, specs, and live Google Cloud docs before a synthesis pass dedups and ranks them.

It's reviewing the *currently committed* paddock + memo (passed in verbatim), and it's instructed not to re-surface the 11 fixes already applied — so what comes back should be genuinely new improvement signal, filtered through an adversarial pass to kill plausible-but-wrong findings.

I'll synthesize it into my own prioritized recommendations — separating safe apply-now improvements from the scope/judgment calls that are yours — as soon as it completes. You can watch live with `/workflows` if you like.

content: Auto mode lets Claude handle permission prompts automatically — Claude checks each tool call for risky actions and prompt injection before executing. Actions Claude identifies as safe are executed, while actions Claude identifies as risky are blocked and Claude may try a different approach. Ideal for long-running tasks. Sessions are slightly more expensive. Claude can make mistakes that allow harmful commands to run, it's recommended to only use in isolated environments. Shift+Tab to change mode.

We're refining heat ₣BZ, the "citizen model" for RBK operator credentials, and have committed a pure-shape paddock plus a mechanics memo. A 9-lens background review is running; next I'll turn its results into prioritized recommendations when it finishes. (disable recaps in /config)

The word now spans declared-intent, discovered-actual, and the producing verb across the memo, memo-20260527, and RBSHR.","fix":"Rename the intent store to a distinct noun (e.g. 'capability ledger' / 'declared ledger', patterned after the existing 'Holdings' civic noun on line 27); change line 50's RHS to 'read the declared ledger'; sweep the ~20 bare 'roster' uses to disambiguate ledger-data vs roster-verb. 161	          "issue": "The invariant 'roster-write authority >= grant verbs' appears three times in the paddock: in the audit prose (with rationale), in the Open item, and in the cinch bullet. One full statement plus pointers suffices.",
162	          "fix": "Keep the audit-prose statement intact — it carries the load-bearing rationale ('else it is a softer escalation path') and already ends with 'see Open.' Drop the trailing parenthetical from the cinch bullet, and in the Open item shorten the restated invariant to a back-reference (keep 'a security boundary, not just storage'; replace the restatement with '(the auto-heal invariant; see The audit)').",
163	          "target": "paddock"
164	        },
165	        {
166	          "theme": "Federate noun sits beside RBSHR's 'federates' verb across the Workforce/Workload seam",
167	          "severity": "low",
168	          "disposition": "apply-now",
169	          "issue": "The memo's peer noun 'federate' (Workforce-federated operator) reads cleanly against memo-20260527, but RBSHR uses 'federates' as a verb for the envoy (Workload/machine egress, lines 213/238) — the two sit one grep apart on the very Workforce/Workload seam the project takes pains to separate. RBSHR line 316 and memo-20260527 already draw the seam, but the new memo does not point to it.",
170	          "fix": "Append a one-clause pointer to the Federate bullet: '(Workforce-federated operator subject; not the envoy's Workload federates-to-AWS egress, which RBSHR Envoy and memo-20260527 Workforce-vs-Workload already separate).' Point to the existing disambiguation rather than re-deriving it; do not assert 'unrelated' without naming where the seam is drawn.",
171	          "target": "memo"
172	        },
173	        {
174	          "theme": "What-done-looks-like carries a redundant verb-mapping parenthetical",
175	          "severity": "low",
176	          "disposition": "apply-now",
177	          "issue": "The paddock 'What done looks like' parenthetical '(invest/divest/roster/mantle -> add/grant/revoke/remove + read, parameterized by capability-set)' restates the cinched verb dissolution already present at the cinch line and in the memo table. (The six-surface list and the load-bearing-core sentence are legitimate paddock 'what done looks like' shape — durable domain entry points, not stale file:line enumerations — and should stay.)",
178	          "fix": "Trim only the redundant verb-mapping parenthetical to '(the admin verbs collapse to the dissolved set)' or drop it, since the dissolution is cinched elsewhere. Keep the six-surface scope and the identity-keyed-accessor core sentence; keep the 'generalizing memo-20260527 single code seam' pointer (a compliant pointer).",
179	          "target": "paddock"
180	        },
181	        {
182	          "theme": "Committed memo-20260527 carries no forward breadcrumb to the active redesign",
183	          "severity": "low",
184	          "disposition": "operator-decision",
185	          "issue": "memo-20260527's Status block still reads 'Committed plan' with no pointer to the citizen redesign, and RBSHR cites it as authoritative — so a reader landing there first misses that the keyfile-tier shape is under revision. The memo deliberately defers the full divergence note to 'when the model is frozen' (line 240), so a dated supersede note now would contradict that cinched deferral.",
186	          "fix": "Add a minimal, deferral-honoring breadcrumb near the Status line (NOT the full supersede note): 'NOTE (2026-06-05): the keyfile-tier shape is under active redesign in heat BZ (memo-20260605-citizen-capability-model.md); the one-SA-per-role and verb-naming assumptions here may change. Not yet revised — the citizen memo will fold its divergences in once that model is frozen.' This closes the read-from-here-first gap without presuming the redesign is settled.",
187	          "target": "memo-20260527-operator-credential-models.md"
188	        }
189	      ],
190	      "overall": "This is a strong, unusually rigorous design-capture pair: the paddock holds shape cleanly with consistent memo-pointers, and the memo is candid about its open questions and even self-incorporated a prior review pass. The findings that survived verification fall into three tiers. The most valuable are real soundness gaps that undermine the model's own central claims: (1) the SA-naming migration to identity-only names is the unstated foundation the whole one-SA-per-person/union-key thesis rests on, yet only the governor's analogous migration is spelled out; (2) the audit affirmatively claims to cover the repoAdmin setIamPolicy residual but, being roster-identity-first, cannot see escalation onto a fresh non-roster principal; and (3) divest never deletes the roster row, so auto-converge would resurrect a torn-down citizen — a direct contradiction of the asymmetric-heal invariant the design is built on. A middle tier of vocabulary collisions (citizen vs RBSHR's artifact-citizen; declared-roster vs the actual-reading roster verb) are genuine within-domain monosemy hazards against committed text and warrant operator judgment before the terms freeze, since they touch RBSHR. The remaining security refinements (definition-expansion auto-grant, 10-key quota, concurrency/revoke ordering, half-failed revoke classification, forgeable orphan marker, the depot-bucket home violating its own invariant) are each real and cheap to fold in. The low-severity items are mostly paddock/memo hygiene — duplication, one mechanism leak, contingent claims stated absolutely — safe to apply now. Note that several findings the original reviewers rated high were correctly de-rated by verification because the memo already flags the issue as open or already hedges the option; I have preserved those de-ratings.",
191	      "ordered_actions": [
192	        "Add a 'Citizen naming migration' subsection to the memo (identity-only SA names; enumerate RBSDK/RBSRK/RBSDD/RBSDR/RBSRR surfaces; existing-SA delete+recreate cutover via the rbk-08 revoke layer + Class-C tolerance) — the foundation both the union-key claim and the lossy-roster justification rest on.",
193	        "Add the member-first audit axis (enumerate actual members on repo/bucket/SA policies, flag non-roster/non-system members) and correct line 134 + soften lines 166-168 — closes the invisible-escalation-via-fresh-principal gap the memo claims it covers.",
194	        "Specify divest/remove ordering: withdraw roster intent FIRST, then revoke IAM, then delete SA; state the 'no IAM revoke without prior roster withdrawal' dual of grant-writes-intent-first — prevents auto-converge resurrecting a torn-down citizen.",
195	        "Decide and resolve the 'citizen' term collision with RBSHR (reword RBSHR's artifact-citizen prose OR pick a different identity noun; add a memo divergence note) before the term freezes.",
196	        "Decide and resolve the 'declared roster' overload of the actual-reading roster cult-verb (preferred: rename the intent store to a 'declared ledger') before the term freezes.",
197	        "Fold in the definition-expansion auto-grant gate, the 10-key-per-SA rekey constraint, the revoke-ordering/concurrency contract, the half-failed-revoke taxonomy row + in-operation retry, and the integrity-advisory orphan-sweep cross-check — five cheap memo additions.",
198	        "Note in the 'Physical home (open)' bullet that the depot bucket (= build bucket, director holds objectCreator) fails the roster-write invariant; require a governor/payor-only write ACL and cinch 'roster-write is never a capability-set member.'",
199	        "Make the Writers rule topology-conditional (writer == grant-authority holder under the active topology) to discharge the keyfile drop/demote collision.",
200	        "Apply the paddock/memo hygiene set: drop the GCP-primitive mechanism leak from the spine bullet; compress the duplicated vocabulary definitions; de-duplicate the thrice-stated roster-write invariant; trim the redundant verb-mapping parenthetical; qualify 'mantle is not special'; reword the migration cinch + add the orphan/grandfather line; add the ephemeral-governor key-coupling caveat and the federate/Workload pointer.",
201	        "Add a minimal deferral-honoring breadcrumb to memo-20260527 pointing at the active redesign (without the full supersede note, honoring the 'record when frozen' deferral)."
202	      ]
203	    }
204	  }
205	}

file.content:
{
  "summary": "Exhaustive multi-lens, adversarially-verified review of the ₣BZ citizen-model paddock + mechanics memo; synthesize prioritized improvements",
  "agentCount": 55,
  "logs": [
    "Fanning out 9 review lenses over the citizen-model artifacts",
    "Candidate findings: 45; survived adversarial verify: 26"
  ],
  "result": {
    "candidate_count": 45,
    "confirmed_count": 26,
    "report": {
      "recommendations": [
        {
          "theme": "SA-naming migration is the unstated foundation (identity-only SA names)",
          "severity": "high",
          "disposition": "apply-now",
          "issue": "The whole model rests on the SA name becoming identity-only (alice, not director-alice) — that is the precondition for 'one citizen = one SA = one key carrying the union' and for the lossy-IAM-roster justification — yet the memo never names the change for director/retriever or its downstream spec edits, even though it handled the analogous governor-name migration (lines 207-210). All four identity verbs hard-encode the role prefix (RBSDK/RBSRK compose ${RBCC_account_director}-<identity>, RBSDR filters/strips it, RBSDD synthesizes it, RBSRR defines three role-keyed RBRA paths).",
          "fix": "Add a 'Citizen naming migration' subsection mirroring what lines 207-210 already do for the governor: (1) state the citizen SA name becomes identity-only; (2) enumerate the surfaces — RBSDK/RBSRK composition drops the prefix, RBSDD email synthesis becomes identity-only, RBSDR's prefix-filter roster is retired in favor of the declared-roster read, RBSRR's three role-keyed rbra.env paths collapse toward one identity-keyed path; (3) state existing-SA cutover: GCP cannot rename an SA, so director-*/retriever-* SAs migrate by delete-old + invest-new, re-entering the divest revoke-before-delete + the invest create/propagation race (the memo-20260604 churn), so it must run through the rbk-08 revoke layer + Class-C tolerance. Leave whether-this-heat-does-it under the existing open scoping flag (line 238).",
          "target": "memo"
        },
        {
          "theme": "Audit domain excludes non-roster principals — escalation via a fresh SA is invisible",
          "severity": "high",
          "disposition": "apply-now",
          "issue": "Line 134 scopes the audit to 'roster-identities × set-defined bindings; non-roster principals are out of scope,' and the surplus taxonomy is computed per-roster-identity. But a leaked director key holds repo-scope setIamPolicy (roles/artifactregistry.repoAdmin per RBSDK) and can grant a powerful role to a FRESH principal the roster never names — which the audit never enumerates, so surplus never fires. The memo affirmatively claims this repoAdmin residual is 'exactly the resource-scope residual the detective audit covers' (lines 166-168), but an identity-first audit cannot see it.",
          "fix": "Add a second, member-first audit axis: for every depot resource where an RBK principal can reach setIamPolicy — the AR repo, the build bucket, and individual SAs — enumerate ACTUAL members and flag any RBK-relevant binding whose member is neither a roster identity nor an expected system identity (Mason, payor/governor, future Envoy, gated by an allowlist to avoid false positives). State the audit must walk policies member-first as well as roster-first. Correct line 134 so resource-policy MEMBERS are in scope even when not roster identities, and soften lines 166-168's 'covers' claim to 'covers only once this member-first axis exists.'",
          "target": "memo"
        },
        {
          "theme": "Divest omits the roster write — auto-converge resurrects a torn-down citizen",
          "severity": "high",
          "disposition": "apply-now",
          "issue": "Verb dissolution decomposes divest into 'revoke(all held) + remove-citizen' (line 49) and remove as 'revoke-all then delete the SA' (line 63), never mentioning deletion of the roster row, and the ordering is unspecified. If a divested identity's roster row survives, the audit reads declared-but-absent = deficit and auto-converges (lines 104-112), silently re-granting capabilities to the identity just torn down — acute because RBSDD anticipates immediate same-folio re-invest, so a stale roster would silently re-confer the old capability union on the freshly re-issued SA. The memo states the grant-side invariant ('grant writes intent first', line 126) but no teardown-side dual.",
          "fix": "Add the teardown dual of 'grant writes intent first': specify remove-citizen as (1) withdraw intent — delete the identity's roster row(s) — FIRST, (2) then revoke IAM, (3) then delete the SA. State the invariant: 'no IAM revoke without a prior-or-atomic roster withdrawal.' Justify by the audit's own asymmetry — roster-first leaves only a transient surplus (reported, never auto-healed), which is safe; roster-last/never leaves an auto-converging deficit.",
          "target": "memo"
        },
        {
          "theme": "'citizen' collides with and inverts RBSHR's existing 'citizen' (artifact)",
          "severity": "medium",
          "disposition": "operator-decision",
          "issue": "The memo mints 'citizen' = operator IDENTITY (a person), but RBSHR already uses 'citizen' for a depot-resident ARTIFACT (property) — hoard line 97 'A {rbtgi_depot} citizen alongside the {rbtga_ark}' and bullion line 222 'bullions are the new AWS-side citizen' — the opposite category. Worse, the memo's other new term 'holdings' (line 27) means exactly what RBSHR's 'citizen' currently means. RBSHR's uses are casual prose metaphors (not formal MCM quoins), and line 193 already flags the embassy/bullion vocabulary as tentative — but the memo never acknowledges the collision against committed text.",
          "fix": "Resolve before freezing 'citizen' as the identity noun. Preferred: keep 'citizen' for the operator identity and reword RBSHR's two prose uses away from 'citizen' (e.g. '{rbtgi_depot}-resident artifact'; 'AWS-side artifact/deliverable'); add a memo note 'supersedes RBSHR prose citizen (artifact) usage' paralleling the memo-20260527 divergence note. Alternatively pick a different identity noun and leave RBSHR untouched, but still add a one-line note that 'citizen' was checked against RBSHR and deliberately diverged. Cite the general minting rule (semantic uniqueness across all persistent names), not the quoin sub-letter discipline.",
          "target": "both"
        },
        {
          "theme": "'declared roster' overloads the existing roster cult-verb (actual vs intent)",
          "severity": "medium",
          "disposition": "operator-decision",
          "issue": "The memo mints 'declared roster' = stored INTENT, but RBK's existing roster cult-verb (RBSDR) emits ACTUAL discovered IAM state ('one roster-director fact file per discovered identity'). The verb-dissolution table (line 50) maps 'roster | read the declared roster,' silently retargeting the actual-reading verb onto the intent noun — the two are opposite poles of the very intent-vs-actual axis the audit is built on. The word now spans declared-intent, discovered-actual, and the producing verb across the memo, memo-20260527, and RBSHR.",
          "fix": "Rename the intent store to a distinct noun (e.g. 'capability ledger' / 'declared ledger', patterned after the existing 'Holdings' civic noun on line 27); change line 50's RHS to 'read the declared ledger'; sweep the ~20 bare 'roster' uses to disambiguate ledger-data vs roster-verb. The audit then names cleanly as the diff between the actual-state roster and the declared ledger. If rename is rejected, at minimum add a sentence to Verb dissolution stating the meaning flip and that the audit is precisely the diff between them.",
          "target": "memo"
        },
        {
          "theme": "Capability-set definition EXPANSION is auto-applied as a silent privilege grant",
          "severity": "medium",
          "disposition": "apply-now",
          "issue": "The memo treats a capability-set definition change as routine 'deficit-on-additions, auto-heal ... no special versioning machinery' (line 131). But definitions are code (line 85), and an expansion (adding a binding) turns every holder of that set into a deficit the audit auto-converges — granting the new binding to all holders automatically, with the same ungated treatment as a failed-grant retry. This silently relocates the privilege-expansion trust boundary from 'who can setIamPolicy / write the roster' (which the memo carefully guards at lines 118-122) to 'who can merge a one-line definition change' — a boundary the memo never names.",
          "fix": "Extend the line 118-122 invariant: state that 'who may edit capability-set definitions' is a first-class authority equal to grant/setIamPolicy authority. Distinguish two deficit sources rather than auto-converging all identically — a per-identity deficit against an UNCHANGED definition (a genuine failed grant, safe to auto-heal) vs a deficit appearing across ALL holders of a set simultaneously (the signature of a definition expansion). Gate the synchronized-roster-wide case behind the same human adjudication the memo requires for surplus-legitimization, preserving 'no special versioning machinery' for the common unchanged-definition case.",
          "target": "memo"
        },
        {
          "theme": "10-key-per-SA quota unaddressed against the rekey lifecycle",
          "severity": "medium",
          "disposition": "apply-now",
          "issue": "The model introduces rekey as a first-class verb ('rotate the key, bindings untouched', line 63) and the whole pitch is freely decoupled key rotation — but GCP enforces a hard, non-adjustable 10-keys-per-SA limit. A rekey that fails partway (create ok, delete fails — the orphan-hazard class) or that does not reclaim old keys accumulates toward the ceiling; the 11th errors. The memo never states the create-vs-delete ordering for rekey nor acknowledges the quota, despite already maintaining a GCP-constraints 'Verified facts' section.",
          "fix": "Add the 10-key constraint to the rekey description and the Verified-facts list. Do NOT mandate delete-before-create (it risks leaving an active citizen keyless if create fails after delete); specify the availability-safe order: create-new, deliver RBRA, verify, delete-old. Add a stale-key reclaim step, and extend the orphan/audit machinery to detect surplus keys on a tracked SA (>1 user-managed key where one is expected), so a stranded key is both findable and quota-reclaimable.",
          "target": "memo"
        },
        {
          "theme": "Concurrent grant vs audit auto-converge can fight; revoke ordering unspecified",
          "severity": "medium",
          "disposition": "apply-now",
          "issue": "Two writers to IAM run concurrently: the grant verb and the audit's deficit auto-converge. The memo states grant ordering (roster-first, line 126) but is silent on revoke ordering and on whether auto-converge reads the roster fresh. Concrete race: a revoke removes the roster entry, a concurrent audit that already read the OLD roster auto-converges the just-revoked set back into IAM — silently re-granting what an operator just revoked. The asymmetric-healing reasoning addresses surplus but not this deficit-side resurrection-by-stale-read.",
          "fix": "Add a deficit-side concurrency contract mirroring the surplus rule: (1) REVOKE writes roster FIRST (withdraw intent), then revoke IAM — so a crashed revoke lands as a SURPLUS (report-only/safe), not an auto-converging deficit (NB: the finding's proposed IAM-then-roster ordering is backwards); (2) auto-converge re-reads the roster under etag immediately before each grant (not from a start-of-audit snapshot), so a concurrent revoke wins; (3) state explicitly that the deficit side carries the same staleness hazard the surplus side does.",
          "target": "memo"
        },
        {
          "theme": "Half-failed multi-scope REVOKE misclassified as adversarial surplus",
          "severity": "medium",
          "disposition": "apply-now",
          "issue": "The drift taxonomy covers half-failed multi-scope GRANT (deficit-fragment, auto-heal) but not the symmetric half-failed REVOKE. A director set spans project+Mason+repo+bucket; if revoke-all fails partway (exactly the Class-C 403-flap memo-20260604 documents), the citizen holds a fragment of a no-longer-declared set, which the taxonomy classifies as surplus-fragment (report, never auto-revoke). So a routine, already-authorized incomplete revoke is sent to human adjudication as if adversarial-Pale — origin-blind, when the Pale test would call it ours. The standing assertion 'the destructive operation is always human-driven / Never auto-revoke' (line 116) also conflicts with the routine auto-revoke in divest/remove (lines 50, 65).",
          "fix": "Address in two tiers: (Tier 1, primary) the divest/remove verb retries its own multi-scope revoke idempotently to completion, carrying the memo-20260604 Class-C tolerance — most flaps resolve in ~81s, avoiding a stranded fragment. (Tier 2) for a fully abandoned mid-divest, record revoke intent before touching IAM (a roster 'revoking' marker) so the audit recognizes an interrupted authorized revoke as ours and finishes it idempotently, distinct from out-of-band surplus. Add the missing taxonomy row and reconcile line 116 with the routine auto-revoke in divest/remove.",
          "target": "memo"
        },
        {
          "theme": "Orphan description-sentinel marker is forgeable/strippable by the SA's own operator",
          "severity": "medium",
          "disposition": "apply-now",
          "issue": "The fallback orphan marker is an SA description sentinel ('rbk:…', lines 143-144). serviceAccounts.update (which writes description) lives in roles/iam.serviceAccountAdmin — the same authority that mints citizens. So a crash mid-add, or a tamperer, can leave a live key with no sentinel (defeating the sweep), and SA tags (the preferred option) have a separable tag-binding ACL that is strictly stronger. The memo presents the sentinel as a 'stable fallback' earning 'exactly this one job' without engaging that it sits in operator-writable metadata.",
          "fix": "State the orphan sweep is integrity-advisory, not authoritative: marker absence must not be trusted. Make the authoritative signal the same cross-check the capability audit already uses — a USER_MANAGED key on an SA absent from the declared roster is an orphan whether or not it carries the marker; use the marker only as a corroborating hint. Prefer SA tags when they leave Preview. Lead the writeup with the marker-strip / no-marker-on-crash case (the robust failure); note the forge case only as a reason the sweep must scope to roster-correlated SAs.",
          "target": "memo"
        },
        {
          "theme": "Roster-write invariant violated by the memo's own preferred home (depot bucket)",
          "severity": "medium",
          "disposition": "operator-decision",
          "issue": "The memo's abstract invariant (lines 118-122, roster-write authority >= grant authority) is correct, but its preferred concrete home — 'A GCS object in the depot bucket ... the natural fit' (line 88) — violates it: that bucket is the build bucket, and per RBSDK the director already holds roles/storage.objectCreator on it. So a director can overwrite the roster, add a capability-set to itself, and let auto-converge apply the escalation — turning detective-only surplus into an auto-applied grant. The memo never connects line 88 to the director's existing bucket write grant.",
          "fix": "In the 'Physical home (open)' bullet, note the depot GCS bucket IS the build bucket and the director already holds objectCreator on it, so a GCS object there FAILS the roster-write invariant unless isolated under an ACL no operator capability-set can reach. Strike or qualify 'the natural fit.' Require the roster home's write-ACL to be held only by governor/payor, never any director/retriever set; add an explicit cinch 'roster-write authority is not, and can never become, a capability-set member.' When restating the invariant, name the concrete resource-scope holders (director repoAdmin + director objectCreator).",
          "target": "both"
        },
        {
          "theme": "Writers rule + keyfile topology cinch + roster-write invariant cannot all hold",
          "severity": "medium",
          "disposition": "operator-decision",
          "issue": "Three settled statements collide in the keyfile tier. The Writers rule (line 93) hardcodes 'the governor writes director/retriever entries.' The topology cinch pulls grant authority to the payor and drops or demotes the standing governor (lines 173-176). The roster-write invariant requires roster-write authority >= grant authority. If the governor is dropped there is no named writer; if demoted to non-privileged it still writing the roster makes roster-write more accessible than payor-held grant — the 'softer escalation path' the invariant forbids. The Writers rule encodes the federation topology, not the keyfile one.",
          "fix": "Make the Writers rule topology-conditional and bind it to the audit invariant: 'The roster writer for an entry must be the holder of grant authority for that entry's capability-sets under the active topology.' Federation/multi-admin: governor writes director/retriever, payor writes governor. Keyfile/solo: the payor writes director/retriever entries directly; a demoted or absent governor must NOT write roster entries it lacks grant authority for. This resolves both open drop/demote branches.",
          "target": "memo"
        },
        {
          "theme": "'mantle is not special' asserted unconditionally despite contingent governor refinement",
          "severity": "low",
          "disposition": "apply-now",
          "issue": "The dissolution table (line 48) flattens mantle to 'add-citizen + grant(governor-set) ... mantle is not special.' But that clean shape holds only once the governor is idempotent (stable name, add-once + rekey), which the memo's own teardown section (lines 207-211) flags as 'not free' (needs a stable name replacing today's datestamped governor-YYYYMMDDHHmm + migration) and possibly descoped. Today's mantle is delete+recreate (RBSGM), i.e. mantle IS special. The table presents a contingent outcome as absolute.",
          "fix": "Qualify the table note: 'not special once the governor is idempotent (stable name, add-once + rekey) — if it stays datestamped delete+recreate, mantle decomposes to divest + add-citizen + grant and remains a replace verb (see Governor teardown leak).' Or render the idempotent-governor decomposition as the target with a parenthetical for the delete+recreate branch using the existing divest row.",
          "target": "memo"
        },
        {
          "theme": "Migration cinch 'read grants from IAM' reads as the retired lossy derivation",
          "severity": "low",
          "disposition": "apply-now",
          "issue": "The paddock migration cinch (lines 132-133) says 'read grants from IAM, register citizens in the declared roster,' sitting beside the Retired cinch (lines 139-141) that retires 'roster derives from IAM' precisely because IAM-derivation is lossy. Migration actually works because legacy SAs carry a role-bearing NAME prefix (director-…/retriever-…), so the role is recoverable from the name — not by lossy binding reverse-mapping. The cinch already says 'grandfathers role-named SAs' but the 'read grants from IAM' phrase muddies that it is a one-time name-based bootstrap, not the forbidden standing equivalence. The memo also has no migration section at all.",
          "fix": "Reword the paddock cinch to recover each legacy citizen's role (and thus its capability-set) from the role-prefixed SA name as a one-time bootstrap, explicitly distinct from the retired standing 'roster derives from IAM' equivalence; thereafter the roster stands independently. Add a short Migration section to the memo covering name-prefix recovery (cite the RBSDR roster regex), that the bootstrap reads bindings only to seed/confirm, and how grandfathered SAs interact with the orphan marker.",
          "target": "both"
        },
        {
          "theme": "Orphan marker doesn't account for grandfathered legacy SAs during migration",
          "severity": "low",
          "disposition": "apply-now",
          "issue": "The orphan hazard rests on 'no name prefix and no SA labels, nothing marks an SA as RBK-managed' (line 138), so new citizens get an rbk: marker found by the sweep. But migration grandfathers legacy director-…/retriever-… SAs that are RBK-managed yet bear no marker, and both populations coexist during the age-out window. Neither memo nor paddock says whether migration stamps the marker onto grandfathered SAs or whether the sweep falls back to name-prefix — and a name-prefix fallback would resurrect the lossy derivation the model retires.",
          "fix": "Add one line: migration stamps the rbk: marker onto each grandfathered legacy SA so the orphan sweep operates on a marker-uniform input domain (prefer this over a name-prefix fallback, which contradicts the retired lossy-derivation). State that until the marker is back-stamped the sweep's coverage of the legacy population is incomplete, making the back-stamp a migration prerequisite.",
          "target": "both"
        },
        {
          "theme": "Ephemeral governor 'shrinks resting exposure' only of the binding, not the key",
          "severity": "low",
          "disposition": "apply-now",
          "issue": "The ephemeral-governor option (lines 181-183) — 'grant the governor-set for one op, then revoke' — claims it 'shrinks resting owner-exposure.' But in the keyfile tier the governor is an SA with an RBRA key on disk (RBSGM), and capability-revoke is decoupled from key-destruction (lines 59-66), so revoking the governor-set strips the binding but leaves the key on disk. It shrinks only the binding, not the keyfile tier's actual attack surface (the file to steal); during grant->use->revoke and any revoke lag (~81s, memo-20260604) the on-disk key carries full owner.",
          "fix": "Add a caveat to the ephemeral-governor bullet: because capability-revoke and key-destruction are decoupled, 'shrinks resting owner-exposure' shrinks only the binding — to reduce the keyfile attack surface, couple key-destruction (rekey-to-nothing or SA delete) to the revoke; and note the grant->use->revoke window plus revoke lag. Do not add the 'circular setIamPolicy' framing (the payor is the grant authority in keyfile/solo) and do not re-label the option (already hedged to 'relevant only if a keyfile delegated governor is ever wanted').",
          "target": "memo"
        },
        {
